topics.title: Strong and Weak Inversion, Small Signal Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strong and Weak Inversion, Small Signal Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regions of Inversion in a MOSFET

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** operates in three primary inversion regimes based on the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) relative to the threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$). These regimes dictate the dominant charge transport physics — drift (strong inversion) versus diffusion (weak inversion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transition between **Strong Inversion** and **Weak Inversion** is the single most important boundary in low-power analog VLSI design, as it determines whether a transistor behaves as a *square-law device* or an *exponential (BJT-like) device*.

### Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

| Region | Condition (NMOS) | Surface Potential $\psi_S$ | Governing Transport |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong Inversion** | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ | $\psi_S \geq 2\psi_B + V_{DS,sat}$ | Drift (mobility-driven) |
| **Moderate (Edge) Inversion** | $V_{GS} \approx V_{TH}$ | $\psi_S \approx 2\psi_B$ | Mixed drift-diffusion |
| **Weak Inversion (Subthreshold)** | $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ | $\psi_B < \psi_S < 2\psi_B$ | Diffusion (exponential) |

Where:
- $\psi_B = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ is the **Fermi potential** of the p-type body.
- $2\psi_B \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$ for typical silicon doping ($N_A = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Strong Inversion:** A condition in which the surface potential $\psi_S$ is large enough that the electron concentration at the Si–SiO$_2$ interface equals or exceeds the bulk hole concentration. The induced inversion layer charge $Q_I$ is given by the classical square-law model.
>
> **Definition — Weak Inversion (Subthreshold):** A condition in which the surface is only *weakly* inverted; the channel charge is exponentially small and transport is governed by the **Boltzmann distribution** rather than drift. $Q_I$ follows an exponential law in $V_{GS}$.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Floodgate Model:**
> Imagine a dam (the gate oxide) holding back a reservoir of water (electrons in the n+ source).
> - **Strong Inversion** = The dam is fully lifted; water rushes through a wide open channel at a rate proportional to the *height of the dam opening squared* (drift, square-law).
> - **Weak Inversion** = The dam is barely cracked open; water *seeps* through tiny pores, and the seepage rate is **exponential** with respect to the crack width (diffusion, exponential law).
> - **Subthreshold Leakage** = That small seepage is enough to drain the reservoir slowly — this is the dominant leakage current in modern nanometer CMOS.

For **small-signal capacitances**, think of the MOSFET gate as a **parallel-plate capacitor** with the oxide as the dielectric. The capacitance is *non-linear* because the channel charge is distributed, and how it redistributes when $v_{gs}$ or $v_{ds}$ changes defines the four small-signal capacitors: $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{gb}$, and $C_{db}$/$C_{sb}$.

---

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300\ K$).
- **Silicon Intrinsic Carrier Concentration:** $n_i \approx \mathbf{1.45 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}}$ at 300 K.
- **Subthreshold Slope (ideal):** $S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx \mathbf{60\ mV/decade}$ (low-doped body, $T = 300\ K$).
- **Oxide Capacitance per Unit Area:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$, typically **1 to 15 fF/μm²** in modern processes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strong Inversion — The Square-Law Regime

### Physical Picture
Once $V_{GS}$ exceeds $V_{TH}$, an *inversion layer* of mobile electrons is created at the Si–SiO$_2$ interface. The areal density of this charge is:
$$Q_I = -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - \frac{V_{DS}}{2}\right) \quad \text{(triode)}$$

$$Q_I = -\frac{C_{ox}}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \quad \text{(saturation, } V_{DS} \geq V_{DS,sat}\text{)}$$

### Why Drift Dominates
- The longitudinal electric field $E_y = \frac{dV}{dy}$ along the channel is *large* (set by $V_{DS}$).
- The transverse field $E_x$ (vertical) is *also* large due to strong inversion.
- Result: $I_D \propto \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH}) V_{DS}$ (linear) or $(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ (saturation).

### Velocity Saturation (Advanced)
In short-channel devices ($L < 0.25\ \mu m$), the carrier velocity saturates at $v_{sat} \approx \mathbf{10^7\ cm/s}$, and the square-law degrades to a *linear* dependence on $V_{GS}$:
$$I_{D,sat} = C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) v_{sat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Insight:** Velocity saturation is why short-channel CMOS transconductance $g_m$ scales as $W$ (not $W/L$), a critical fact for high-speed analog design.

---

## 2.2 Weak Inversion (Subthreshold) — The Exponential Regime

### Physical Picture
Even when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$, a *thin* sheet of electrons exists at the surface. The electron density follows the **Boltzmann statistics**:
$$n(y) = n_0 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V(y)}{V_T}\right)$$

The drain current is dominated by the **diffusion** of carriers from source to drain (no drift, because the inversion layer is too thin to sustain a meaningful lateral field profile).

### The Subthreshold Drain Current
$$I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

For $V_{DS} \geq 3 V_T \approx 75\ mV$, the bracketed term $\to 1$ and $I_D$ becomes independent of $V_{DS}$ (saturation-like behavior in subthreshold).

### Subthreshold Slope
$$S = \left(\frac{d(\log_{10} I_D)}{dV_{GS}}\right)^{-1} = n V_T \ln(10)$$

The **body-effect coefficient** is:
$$n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}} = 1 + \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\psi_B + V_{SB}}}$$

For a typical 0.18 μm process, $n \approx 1.3\text{–}1.5$, giving $S \approx \mathbf{80\text{–}90\ mV/dec}$ — the **fundamental CMOS switch leakage barrier**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Analog Design:** In weak inversion, the MOSFET behaves like a **BJT** — it offers the highest $g_m/I_D$ efficiency (transconductance per unit current), making it ideal for **ultra-low-power** subthreshold op-amps, LNAs, and biomedical front-ends.

---

## 2.3 Small-Signal Capacitances in MOSFETs

The intrinsic MOSFET has a distributed RC nature. For hand analysis, we lump the channel into **four small-signal capacitors** between the four terminals (G, D, S, B).

### Intrinsic Capacitance Source: Gate-Channel Charge Partitioning

The total inversion charge $Q_I$ is split between source and drain ends of the channel. In **saturation**:
$$Q_S = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}), \qquad Q_D = 0$$
$$Q_B = -C_{ox} W L (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \cdot f(\text{body factor})$$

Taking partial derivatives with respect to terminal voltages gives the small-signal capacitances.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** In all table entries below, the vertical bar is encoded as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table syntax. Do not use raw `|`.

| Capacitance | Triode Region $V_{DS} \ll V_{GS}-V_{TH}$ | Saturation Region $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS}-V_{TH}$ | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{gs}$ (gate–source) | $\frac{1}{2} W L C_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ | $\frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ | F |
| $C_{gd}$ (gate–drain) | $\frac{1}{2} W L C_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ | $W C_{ov}$ (only overlap) | F |
| $C_{gb}$ (gate–body) | $0$ (channel shields body) | $0$ (channel shields body) | F |
| $C_{db}$ (drain–body) | Junction capacitance, function of $V_{DB}$ | Junction capacitance | F |
| $C_{sb}$ (source–body) | Junction capacitance | Junction capacitance | F |
| $C_{ox}$ (per unit area) | $\frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Same | F/m² |

Where $C_{ov} = C_{ox} \cdot L_D$ is the **gate-overlap capacitance** (typically $L_D \approx 0.05\text{–}0.1\ \mu m$ per side).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** In saturation, the gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ is *almost zero* (no channel charge near the drain end). This is why **cascode transistors** are added in high-gain op-amps — they reduce the **Miller effect** by lowering the impedance at the drain node.

### The Meyer Capacitance Model
The classical formulation (used in SPICE Level 1–3) is:
$$C_{gs} = -\frac{\partial Q_S}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg|_{V_{DS}}, \quad C_{gd} = -\frac{\partial Q_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg|_{V_{DS}}$$
$$C_{gb} = -\frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg|_{V_{DS}}$$

Charge conservation requires: $C_{gs} + C_{gd} + C_{gb} = W L C_{ox} = C_{ox}''$ (total gate capacitance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Charge-Conservation Pitfall:** The simple Meyer model is *not* charge-conserving. Modern BSIM3/4/6 models use the **Ward-Dutton partition** which guarantees $\frac{dQ_G}{dt} + \frac{dQ_D}{dt} + \frac{dQ_S}{dt} + \frac{dQ_B}{dt} = 0$.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Inversion Regime | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Switched-Capacitor Op-Amps | **Strong Inversion** | High $g_m$, fast settling, large slew rate |
| Subthreshold LNAs (IoT/RF) | **Weak Inversion** | Maximum $g_m/I_D$ efficiency, lowest power |
| Voltage Reference (PTAT/CTAT) | **Weak Inversion** | Exponential $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ enables precise temperature scaling |
| Memory Sense Amplifiers | **Moderate Inversion** | Balance between speed and power |
| High-Frequency RF Front-End | **Strong Inversion + Velocity Saturation** | Maximum $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{gs}+C_{gd})}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drain Current in Strong Inversion (Square Law)

### Starting Point — Charge Density in the Channel
Consider an NMOS in triode. The inversion layer charge per unit area at position $y$ along the channel is:
$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)\right]$$

**Step 1:** Apply the gradual-channel approximation. The current density at position $y$ is:
$$J(y) = \mu_n \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot E_y = \mu_n \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot \left(-\frac{dV}{dy}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by channel width $W$ to get the line current (constant along $y$ by KCL):
$$I_D = -W \mu_n Q_I(y) \frac{dV}{dy}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange and integrate from source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):
$$I_D \int_0^L dy = -W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}}\left(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V\right) dV$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the right-hand integral:
$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $I_D$ (triode equation):
$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** For saturation, set $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ and note the channel pinches off. Substituting:
$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})\,}$$

The factor $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ models **channel-length modulation** (CLM), with $\lambda \propto 1/L$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drain Current in Weak Inversion (Subthreshold)

### Starting Point — Surface Potential vs. $V_{GS}$
The surface potential $\psi_S$ in weak inversion is a *linear* function of $V_{GS}$ (this is the key insight, since the depletion region is fully formed):
$$\psi_S = V_{GS} - V_{TH} + 2\psi_B \cdot (\text{const})$$
More precisely:
$$\psi_S \approx \frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n} + \text{const}, \quad n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}$$

### Step 1: Electron Density at Surface
The surface electron concentration (per cm³) is:
$$n_s = n_i \exp\left(\frac{q\psi_S}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the $\psi_S$ relationship:
$$n_s = n_i \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \exp(\text{const})$$

Absorbing the constant into a pre-factor $I_{D0}$:
$$n_s \propto \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the diffusion current law along the channel:
$$I_D = -q W D_n \frac{dn}{dy} \bigg|_{\text{surface}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $n$ decays exponentially from source to drain:
$$n(y) = n_s \exp\left(-\frac{V(y)}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and integrate:
$$I_D = \mu_n V_T C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (n-1) \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

For $V_{DS} \gg V_T$:
$$\boxed{\,I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)\,}$$

where $I_{D0} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 (n-1) e^{1.8} \exp\left(-\frac{V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$ is a process-dependent constant.

### Step 6: Transconductance in Weak Inversion
Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$:
$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{I_D}{n V_T}$$

**Compare to Strong Inversion:**
$$g_m^{SI} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}$$

**Efficiency Ratio:**
$$\frac{g_m/I_D \big\vert_{WI}}{g_m/I_D \big\vert_{SI}} = \frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{2 n V_T} \gg 1 \text{ for low } V_{GS}-V_{TH}$$

This is the mathematical proof of why **subthreshold operation is preferred for ultra-low-power analog design**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Small-Signal Capacitances via Charge Partitioning

### Step 1: Total Inversion Charge in Saturation
In saturation, the channel pinches off at the drain. The charge per unit length at position $y$ is:
$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq y \leq L - \Delta L$$

where $\Delta L$ is the pinch-off region (assumed $\to 0$ for ideal long-channel).

### Step 2: Integrate to Get Total Source-End Charge
The total charge associated with the source terminal is the integral of $Q_I$ weighted by the *fraction* of charge controlled by the source (full for source, zero for drain in saturation):
$$Q_S = \int_0^L Q_I(y) \left(1 - \frac{y}{L}\right) dy$$

Actually, the **Ward-Dutton partition** gives:
$$Q_S = \int_0^L \left(1 - \frac{y}{L}\right) Q_I(y) W dy$$

### Step 3: Compute the Integral
For saturation, $V(y)$ increases linearly from 0 to $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Using the channel potential profile $V(y) = (V_{GS}-V_{TH}) \cdot \frac{y}{L}$:
$$Q_S = W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \int_0^L \left(1 - \frac{y}{L}\right) \left(1 - \frac{y}{L}\right) dy$$

Let $u = y/L$:
$$Q_S = W C_{ox} L (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \int_0^1 (1-u)^2 du$$

$$\int_0^1 (1-u)^2 du = \left[-\frac{(1-u)^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_S = \frac{1}{3} W L C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

Wait — accounting for the *voltage division* (only half the overdrive contributes on average in saturation), the well-known result is:
$$Q_S = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

This factor of $2/3$ comes from the proper partition (drain takes $1/3$, source takes $2/3$).

### Step 4: Differentiate to Get $C_{gs}$
$$C_{gs} = -\frac{\partial Q_S}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg|_{V_{DS}} = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{gs}^{sat} = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox}\,}$$

### Step 5: Gate-Drain Capacitance in Saturation
Since $Q_D \to 0$ in saturation (pinch-off), the *intrinsic* $C_{gd} \to 0$, leaving only the overlap:
$$\boxed{\,C_{gd}^{sat} = W C_{ov} = W L_D C_{ox}\,}$$

### Step 6: Triode Region (Symmetric)
In triode, the channel extends fully, and by symmetry, $Q_S = Q_D = \frac{1}{2} Q_I^{total}$:
$$\boxed{\,C_{gs}^{tri} = C_{gd}^{tri} = \frac{1}{2} W L C_{ox} + W C_{ov}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The **2/3 factor** in $C_{gs}^{sat}$ is a high-yield exam fact. It is the basis of the **Miller effect** in CMOS amplifiers and explains why cascode stages have wider bandwidth.

---

## 3.4 Summary — All Key Equations at a Glance

| # | Equation | Regime | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | Strong Inv. Sat. | Square-law current |
| 2 | $I_D = I_{D0} \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)$ | Weak Inv. Sat. | Exponential current |
| 3 | $S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 60\text{–}90\ mV/dec$ | Weak Inv. | Subthreshold slope |
| 4 | $n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}} = 1 + \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\psi_B + V_{SB}}}$ | All | Body-effect factor |
| 5 | $g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS}-V_{TH}}$ | Strong Inv. | Square-law transconductance |
| 6 | $g_m = \frac{I_D}{n V_T}$ | Weak Inv. | Exponential transconductance |
| 7 | $C_{gs} = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox}$ | Strong Inv. Sat. | Gate-source capacitance |
| 8 | $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = \frac{1}{2} W L C_{ox}$ | Strong Inv. Triode | Symmetric gate cap |
| 9 | $C_{gb} \approx 0$ (intrinsic) | Strong Inv. | Channel shields body |
| 10 | $C_{gd}^{sat} = W L_D C_{ox}$ | Strong Inv. Sat. | Overlap-only |
| 11 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs}+C_{gd})}$ | All | Unity-gain frequency |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Decision Tree: Region of Operation

This block-level functional flow shows how the bias voltages determine the inversion regime.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart([Start: Apply VGS, VDS to NMOS]) --> nodeCheckGS{VGS greater than VTH?}
    nodeCheckGS -- NO --> nodeWeak[Weak Inversion Region]
    nodeCheckGS -- YES --> nodeCheckDS{VDS greater than VGS minus VTH?}
    nodeCheckDS -- NO --> nodeTriode[Triode Linear Region]
    nodeCheckDS -- YES --> nodeStrong[Strong Inversion Saturation Region]

    nodeWeak --> nodePropA1[Dominant Transport: Diffusion]
    nodeWeak --> nodePropA2[ID proportional to exp of VGS over n VT]
    nodeWeak --> nodePropA3[Slope S = 60 to 90 mV per decade]
    nodeWeak --> nodePropA4[Use case: Ultra low power analog]

    nodeTriode --> nodePropB1[Channel exists end to end]
    nodeTriode --> nodePropB2[ID proportional to VDS linear]
    nodeTriode --> nodePropB3[Use case: Switch, resistor, triode load]

    nodeStrong --> nodePropC1[Dominant Transport: Drift]
    nodeStrong --> nodePropC2[ID proportional to VGS minus VTH squared]
    nodeStrong --> nodePropC3[Cgs equals 2 over 3 of WLCox]
    nodeStrong --> nodePropC4[Use case: High gain amplifiers]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Small-Signal MOSFET Model with Capacitances

This functional architecture flow shows the small-signal equivalent circuit of an intrinsic MOSFET in saturation, with all four small-signal capacitances explicitly labeled.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeG[Gate Terminal G] --- nodeCgs{Cgs equals 2 over 3 WLCox}
    nodeG --- nodeCgd{Cgd equals W Lov Cox overlap}
    nodeG --- nodeCgb{Cgb equals 0 in strong inversion}

    nodeCgs --- nodeS[Source Terminal S]
    nodeCgd --- nodeD[Drain Terminal D]
    nodeCgb --- nodeB[Bulk Terminal B]

    nodeD --- nodeGmDep[Current Source gm times Vgs]
    nodeGmDep --- nodeS

    nodeD --- nodeRo[Output Resistance ro equals 1 over lambda ID]
    nodeRo --- nodeS

    nodeS --- nodeCsb[Csb junction capacitance]
    nodeCsb --- nodeB

    nodeD --- nodeCdb[Cdb junction capacitance]
    nodeCdb --- nodeB

    nodeGmDep -.controlled by.-> nodeG
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **current source $g_m v_{gs}$** is the heart of the small-signal model.
- **$C_{gs}$** is the dominant intrinsic capacitance (signal input path).
- **$C_{gd}$** is the **Miller capacitance** — multiplied by $(1 + A_v)$ in a common-source amplifier.
- **$C_{gb}$** is zero in strong inversion but *non-zero* in weak inversion (the channel no longer fully shields the body).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Charge Distribution Visualization

A schematic view of how gate charge partitions between source, drain, and body in saturation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeLeft[Source End y equals 0] --> nodeArrow1[Charge Density Q I at y equals 0 is Maximum]
    nodeArrow1 --> nodeMiddle[Channel Position y at L over 2]
    nodeMiddle --> nodeArrow2[Charge Density decreases linearly]
    nodeArrow2 --> nodeRight[Drain End y equals L]
    nodeRight --> nodeArrow3[Charge Density goes to 0 due to Pinch Off]

    nodeMiddle --> nodePartition[Total Charge Q I splits as]
    nodePartition --> nodeQS[Q S to Source = 2 over 3 of Total]
    nodePartition --> nodeQD[Q D to Drain = 0 in Saturation]
    nodePartition --> nodeQB[Q B to Body = function of Body Factor]

    nodeQS --> nodeCapGS[Derive Cgs equals 2 over 3 WLCox]
    nodeQD --> nodeCapGD[Derive Cgd equals W Lov Cox]
    nodeQB --> nodeCapGB[Derive Cgb equals WLCox minus Cgs minus Cgd]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — gm/ID Efficiency Curve

This block diagram maps the analog designer's "knob of choice" — the inversion coefficient (IC).

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[IC less than 0.1 Weak Inversion] --> nodeProp1[gm over ID is maximum near 1 over nVT]
    nodeA --> nodeProp2[ID is exponential in VGS]
    nodeA --> nodeProp3[Best for low power op amps]

    nodeB[IC between 0.1 and 10 Moderate Inversion] --> nodeProp4[Transition region mixed drift and diffusion]
    nodeB --> nodeProp5[Highest intrinsic gain gm times ro]

    nodeC[IC greater than 10 Strong Inversion] --> nodeProp6[gm over ID is minimum near 2 over VOV]
    nodeC --> nodeProp7[ID is square law in VGS]
    nodeC --> nodeProp8[Best for high speed amplifiers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Inversion Coefficient:** $IC = \dfrac{I_D}{I_{D0} \cdot (W/L)}$, a dimensionless knob that lets the analog designer pick the operating regime explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define strong inversion and weak inversion regions in a MOSFET. How are the channel charge and drain current modeled differently in each regime?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Strong Inversion** occurs when $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, producing a substantial inversion layer at the Si–SiO$_2$ interface. The channel charge is modeled by the square-law:
$$Q_I = -C_{ox}\left(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)\right)$$
and the drain current in saturation is:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$
Transport is **drift-dominated**.

**Weak Inversion (Subthreshold)** occurs when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$. The channel is a thin sheet of electrons whose density follows the **Boltzmann distribution**. The drain current is exponential:
$$I_D = I_{D0}\exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)$$
with $n = 1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox}$. Transport is **diffusion-dominated**.

**Key Distinction:** Strong inversion → algebraic (quadratic) law; Weak inversion → exponential law (BJT-like). *(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 1 for charge models, 1 for transport physics.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**List the four intrinsic small-signal capacitances of a MOSFET and state their values in saturation and triode regions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four small-signal capacitances of an intrinsic MOSFET are $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{gb}$, and $C_{db}$/$C_{sb}$ (the last two are junction capacitances).

| Capacitance | Saturation | Triode |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{gs}$ | $\frac{2}{3}WLC_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ | $\frac{1}{2}WLC_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ |
| $C_{gd}$ | $W C_{ov}$ (overlap only) | $\frac{1}{2}WLC_{ox} + W C_{ov}$ |
| $C_{gb}$ | $\approx 0$ (channel shields body) | $\approx 0$ (channel shields body) |
| $C_{db}, C_{sb}$ | Junction (function of bias) | Same |

Total gate capacitance: $C_{gs} + C_{gd} + C_{gb} = WLC_{ox}$. *(3 marks — 1 for naming, 1 for saturation values, 1 for triode values.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`** (14 Marks)

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Derive the expression for the drain current of an NMOS transistor operating in **strong inversion and saturation**. Clearly state all assumptions and show each algebraic step.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: For the transistor designed with $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\ \mu A/V^2$, and $V_{TH} = 0.5\ V$, biased at $V_{GS} = 1.0\ V$ and $V_{DS} = 1.5\ V$, calculate the drain current. Then compute the **transconductance** $g_m$ and the **small-signal gate-source capacitance** $C_{gs}$ assuming $C_{ox} = 10\ fF/\mu m^2$ and $L = 0.5\ \mu m$.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the assumptions of the gradual-channel approximation (GCA):**
1. The transverse field $E_x$ is much larger than the longitudinal field $E_y$.
2. The mobile inversion charge per unit area is given by $Q_I = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y))$.
3. The drift current dominates (no diffusion).
4. The channel length modulation effect is ignored initially (or absorbed via $\lambda$).

**Step 2 — Channel charge density at position $y$:**
$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Current continuity along channel (1D drift):**
$$I_D = -\mu_n W Q_I(y) \frac{dV}{dy} = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y)\right] \frac{dV}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$):**
$$I_D \int_0^L dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \int_0^{V_{DS}}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V\right] dV$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the right-hand integral:**
$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the triode-region $I_D$:**
$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Apply saturation condition $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$:** Beyond $V_{DS,sat}$, the channel pinches off and $I_D$ saturates. Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$:
$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

*(Award 1 mark per key step; 7 marks for the complete derivation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Verify saturation:** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 1.0 - 0.5 = 0.5\ V$. Since $V_{DS} = 1.5\ V > 0.5\ V$, the device is indeed in **saturation**.

**Step 2 — Compute drain current (neglecting $\lambda$ for simplicity, or take $\lambda = 0.05\ V^{-1}$):**
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}(100\ \mu A/V^2)(10)(0.5\ V)^2(1 + 0.05 \cdot 1.5)$$
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1.075 = 134.4\ \mu A$$

**Step 3 — Transconductance in saturation:**
$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 \cdot 134.4\ \mu A}{0.5\ V} = 537.6\ \mu A/V \approx 0.538\ mA/V$$

Equivalently, $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 100 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.5 = 500\ \mu A/V$ (without CLM).

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{gs}$ in saturation:**
$$C_{gs} = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot (10 \cdot 0.5\ \mu m^2) \cdot 10\ fF/\mu m^2$$
$$C_{gs} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 5 \cdot 10 = 33.3\ fF$$

(If overlap capacitance is included, add $W C_{ov} \approx 2\ fF$ for a typical $L_D = 0.02\ \mu m$.)

**Step 5 — Unity-gain frequency (bonus insight):**
$$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})} \approx \frac{537.6\ \mu A/V}{2\pi \cdot 33.3\ fF} \approx 2.57\ GHz$$

*(Award marks: 1 for saturation check, 2 for $I_D$, 2 for $g_m$, 2 for $C_{gs}$.)*

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`** (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Explain the concept of **subthreshold (weak inversion) operation** of a MOSFET. Derive the expression for the subthreshold drain current and the **subthreshold slope $S$**. Why is $S$ fundamentally limited to $\approx 60\ mV/dec$ at room temperature?

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: A subthreshold NMOS circuit is biased at $V_{GS} = 0.3\ V$ with $V_{TH} = 0.5\ V$ and $n = 1.4$. If $I_{D0} = 10\ nA$, calculate: (i) the drain current, (ii) the transconductance $g_m$, and (iii) the subthreshold slope $S$ at $T = 300\ K$. Comment on the $g_m/I_D$ efficiency compared to strong inversion with $V_{OV} = 0.2\ V$.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition:** In weak inversion, $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ and the surface is only weakly inverted. Although no "channel" exists in the classical sense, a thin sheet of mobile electrons is present at the Si–SiO$_2$ interface, with surface concentration:
$$n_s = n_i \exp\left(\frac{q\psi_S}{kT}\right) \propto \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Transport mechanism:** Because the inversion layer is extremely thin, the lateral field cannot sustain drift transport. The drain current is **diffusion-dominated**:
$$I_D = -q W D_n \frac{dn}{dy}\bigg|_{surface}$$

**Step 3 — Solving the diffusion equation** along the channel (with the assumption $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ so the channel "saturates" in subthreshold):
$$I_D = \mu_n V_T C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (n-1) \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right)$$

Combining all process-dependent constants into $I_{D0}$:
$$\boxed{\,I_D = I_{D0} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the subthreshold slope:**
Take the log base 10 of $I_D$:
$$\log_{10}(I_D) = \log_{10}(I_{D0}) + \frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T \ln(10)}$$

The slope (inverse) is:
$$S = \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\log_{10} I_D)} = n V_T \ln(10)$$

At $T = 300\ K$, $V_T = 25.85\ mV$, so:
$$S_{min} = 1 \cdot 25.85 \cdot 2.303 \approx 60\ mV/dec$$

**Step 5 — The 60 mV/decade Limit:** The factor $V_T = kT/q$ is *fundamental* — it comes from the Boltzmann distribution of carriers. To switch a current by a factor of 10, you must change $V_{GS}$ by at least $V_T \ln(10) \approx 60\ mV$. The body-effect factor $n > 1$ makes it *worse*. This is the **Boltzmann tyranny** limiting all CMOS scaling.

*(Award 2 marks for the physical picture, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for slope, 1 mark for the 60 mV discussion.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Calculate drain current in weak inversion:**
$$I_D = I_{D0} \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T}\right) = 10\ nA \cdot \exp\left(\frac{0.3}{1.4 \cdot 0.02585}\right)$$
$$\frac{V_{GS}}{n V_T} = \frac{0.3}{0.03619} = 8.29$$
$$I_D = 10\ nA \cdot e^{8.29} = 10\ nA \cdot 4010 \approx 40.1\ \mu A$$

**Step 2 — Transconductance in weak inversion:**
$$g_m = \frac{I_D}{n V_T} = \frac{40.1\ \mu A}{1.4 \cdot 25.85\ mV} = \frac{40.1\ \mu A}{36.19\ mV} \approx 1.11\ mA/V$$

**Step 3 — Subthreshold slope:**
$$S = n V_T \ln(10) = 1.4 \cdot 25.85 \cdot 2.303 \approx 83.4\ mV/dec$$

**Step 4 — $g_m/I_D$ efficiency comparison:**

*Weak inversion:*
$$\left(\frac{g_m}{I_D}\right)_{WI} = \frac{1}{n V_T} = \frac{1}{36.19\ mV} \approx 27.6\ V^{-1}$$

*Strong inversion with $V_{OV} = 0.2\ V$:*
$$\left(\frac{g_m}{I_D}\right)_{SI} = \frac{2}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2}{0.2} = 10\ V^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The weak-inversion circuit has a $g_m/I_D$ efficiency that is **$\approx 2.76\times$ higher** than the strong-inversion design. This means the subthreshold circuit delivers the same transconductance while consuming **2.76× less bias current** — the mathematical basis for ultra-low-power analog design.

*(Award 2 marks for $I_D$, 1 mark for $g_m$, 1 mark for $S$, 3 marks for the $g_m/I_D$ comparison and conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2/3 in $C_{gs}^{sat}$** → most common mistake; loses 2–3 marks. Always state the *region* (saturation vs. triode) explicitly.
> 2. **Using $V_T$ instead of $n V_T$** in subthreshold equations → entire derivation becomes wrong by a factor of $n \approx 1.4$.
> 3. **Confusion between $C_{gs}$ and $C_{ox}$** → $C_{ox}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area (F/m²); $C_{gs}$ is the small-signal gate-source capacitance (F). The unit mismatch loses easy marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the overlap capacitance $C_{ov}$** in $C_{gd}^{sat}$ → examiner may deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Using $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{DS}$ in triode** → that is the *triode transconductance*. In saturation, $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$.
> 6. **Dropping the $(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ factor** when channel-length modulation is asked → full 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Strong/Weak Inversion & Small-Signal Capacitance)**

### Strong Inversion
- ☐ Occurs when $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$; surface potential $\psi_S \geq 2\psi_B + V_{DS,sat}$.
- ☐ Triode: $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)\left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2\right]$.
- ☐ Saturation: $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1+\lambda V_{DS})$.
- ☐ $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$.
- ☐ $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox} = 3.9 \varepsilon_0 / t_{ox}$ (F/m²).

### Weak Inversion
- ☐ Occurs when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$; transport is **diffusion-dominated**.
- ☐ $I_D = I_{D0} \exp(V_{GS}/(n V_T))$.
- ☐ $n = 1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox} = 1 + \gamma/(2\sqrt{2\psi_B + V_{SB}})$.
- ☐ Subthreshold slope $S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 60 n\ mV/dec$.
- ☐ $g_m = I_D/(n V_T)$; **highest $g_m/I_D$ efficiency** of any bias regime.
- ☐ Used in ultra-low-power IoT, biomedical, subthreshold op-amps.

### Small-Signal Capacitances
- ☐ **Meyer model** (Level 1–3 SPICE): $C_{gs} = (2/3)WLC_{ox}$ (sat), $C_{gd} = WC_{ov}$ (sat).
- ☐ Triode: $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = (1/2)WLC_{ox} + WC_{ov}$.
- ☐ $C_{gb} \approx 0$ in strong inversion; **non-zero in weak inversion** (channel does not fully shield body).
- ☐ Charge conservation: $C_{gs} + C_{gd} + C_{gb} = WLC_{ox}$.
- ☐ $C_{gd}$ is the **Miller capacitance** — defines high-frequency pole of CS amplifier.
- ☐ Ward-Dutton partition used in BSIM for charge conservation.
- ☐ Junction capacitances $C_{db}$, $C_{sb}$ are bias-dependent (depletion approximation).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ☐ $g_m/I_D$ is the analog designer's **figure of merit** for power efficiency.
- ☐ $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_{gs}+C_{gd})]$ is the unity-gain frequency.
- ☐ Transition between weak and strong inversion is *gradual*, not sharp — "moderate inversion" is a valid design region.
- ☐ The **60 mV/decade** Boltzmann limit is the *fundamental barrier* to low-voltage CMOS design.
- ☐ Velocity saturation in short-channel devices breaks the square law → $I_D$ becomes linear in $V_{GS}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3-Terminal MOS Structure - Contacting Inversion Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 3-Terminal MOS Structure - Contacting Inversion Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **3-Terminal MOS Structure** is the foundational geometry from which the modern **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is derived. It augments the basic two-terminal MOS capacitor (Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor) by adding two heavily-doped regions of the **same conductivity type opposite to the substrate**, called the **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)**, which are diffused/ion-implanted into the semiconductor surface on either side of the gate. The **Gate (G)** is the third terminal.

The act of **"Contacting the Inversion Layer"** refers to the physical process by which the two heavily-doped source/drain regions provide **ohmic (low-resistance) electrical contact** to the induced electron inversion layer (in an NMOS device) that forms at the Si–SiO₂ interface when the gate voltage exceeds the threshold voltage. Without these contacts, the inversion layer would be a "dead-end sheet of charge" with no external accessibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 3-terminal MOS structure is the conceptual bridge between the passive MOS capacitor (covered in Module 2) and the four-terminal MOSFET (often analyzed as a 3-terminal device with Source tied to Body). Mastering this transition is essential for understanding I–V characteristics, threshold voltage, and the body effect in subsequent modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Tap"

Imagine a flat, thin rubber sheet (the **inversion layer**) lying on top of a sealed water pipe (the **p-type substrate**). The sheet only forms when you press down on a plunger (the **gate voltage**). Once the sheet exists, water (current) can flow from one end to the other, **but only if you have an inlet and an outlet pipe connected to the sheet**. The n⁺ source and drain regions are exactly these "inlet and outlet pipes" — they make the induced channel practically useful.

- **Gate voltage** is like the hand pressing the plunger — it controls the *formation* of the channel.
- **Threshold voltage (Vₜ)** is the minimum pressure needed before the rubber sheet even starts to form.
- **Source and Drain** are the *plumbing* that lets current actually flow out of the device.

### 1.3 The Four Physical Constants You Must Memorize

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Purpose |
|----------|--------|-------|---------|
| Electron charge | **q** | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | Charge carrier quantization |
| Boltzmann constant | **k** | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Thermal voltage computation |
| Thermal voltage (300 K) | **Vₜ = kT/q** | **≈ 25.85 mV** | Subthreshold swing, body effect |
| Intrinsic carrier concentration in Si | **nᵢ** | **≈ 1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³** | Surface potential reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Body Tie Convention in the 3-Terminal View:** In the KTU 3-terminal analysis, the **Source terminal is internally shorted to the Body (substrate)** terminal. This implicitly sets **V_SB = 0**, eliminating the body effect and giving the simplest threshold voltage V_T0. Real circuits often tie body to the most negative supply (for NMOS), which introduces the body effect — a critical 14-mark topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy — From MOS Capacitor to 3-Terminal Device

The construction of the 3-terminal MOS device follows a precise sequence:

1. **Start with a uniformly doped p-type silicon substrate** (Boron-doped, N_A ≈ 10¹⁵ – 10¹⁷ cm⁻³).
2. **Grow a thin SiO₂ layer** (typical thickness t_ox = 2 – 20 nm) thermally on the surface.
3. **Deposit and pattern the polysilicon or metal gate** on top of the oxide. This defines the **channel length L** and the gate area.
4. **Perform two heavily-doped n⁺ implant/diffusion steps** on either side of the gate, self-aligned to the gate edges, forming the **Source** and **Drain** regions.
5. **Deposit metal contacts** to the n⁺ regions and (optionally) to the p-substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Alignment Magic:** The poly-gate acts as a mask during the n⁺ implant, ensuring the source and drain are *automatically aligned* to the gate edges. This eliminates parasitic overlap capacitance to first order and is the cornerstone of modern CMOS scaling.

### 2.2 The Inversion Layer — How It Forms (and Why It Needs Contacting)

When a positive gate-to-source voltage **V_GS** is applied (for NMOS):

- Holes in the p-substrate are **pushed away** from the oxide interface (depletion region forms).
- Negatively charged acceptor ions (B⁻) are left behind, forming the **depletion region charge Q_B**.
- As V_GS increases further, the surface potential ψ_s rises.
- When **ψ_s ≥ 2φ_F**, where **φ_F = (kT/q) ln(N_A/nᵢ)** is the Fermi potential, the electron concentration at the surface **exceeds** the hole concentration → **strong inversion** is triggered.
- The induced electron sheet is the **inversion layer**, with sheet charge **Q_inv < 0** (C/m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Contacting Is Indispensable:** A bare inversion layer is a "floating sheet" of electrons sandwiched between the oxide above and the depletion region below. It has *no terminal to inject or extract current*. The n⁺ source and drain are reservoirs of electrons in equilibrium with the inversion layer, providing the **source of carriers** and the **drain to collect them**. Without them, no controlled current I_DS can flow.

### 2.3 The Threshold Voltage Equation (with Body Effect)

The **gate voltage at the onset of strong inversion** is the threshold voltage V_T. For an n-channel device:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)
$$

Where:
- $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ is the zero-bias threshold voltage (V_SB = 0).
- $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ is the flat-band voltage.
- $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ is the **body-effect coefficient** (typically 0.3 – 0.7 V¹/²).
- $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ is the gate-oxide capacitance per unit area.
- $V_{SB}$ is the source-to-body reverse-bias voltage (≥ 0 for NMOS).

### 2.4 The Inversion-Layer Charge Density (Linear Approximation)

Once V_GS > V_T, the mobile inversion charge per unit area is:

$$
Q_{inv}(y) = -C_{ox}\,\bigl[V_{GS} - V_{T} - V(y)\bigr]
$$

where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential (0 at source, V_DS at drain). At the source end (y=0):

$$
Q_{inv}(0) = -C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_{T})
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Gold)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|----------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential of p-substrate | V |
| 2 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance density | F/m² |
| 3 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V¹ᐟ² |
| 4 | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold with body effect | V |
| 5 | $Q_{inv} = -C_{ox}(V_{GS}-V_T)$ | Inversion charge at source (strong inv.) | C/m² |
| 6 | $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Zero-bias threshold | V |
| 7 | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ | Flat-band voltage | V |
| 8 | $\Delta V_T = \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Body-effect shift in V_T | V |
| 9 | $Q_B = -\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A(2\phi_F+V_{SB})}$ | Depletion-region bulk charge | C/m² |
| 10 | $\psi_{s,inv} = 2\phi_F$ | Surface potential at strong inversion onset | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The 3-terminal MOS structure is the literal heart of every CMOS inverter, op-amp input stage, and analog switch. The body effect equation is what forces analog IC designers to **tie the body of every NMOS to its source** (using a separate p-tap) whenever possible — failing to do so introduces unwanted V_T modulation and circuit non-linearity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage V_T0 (Zero Body Bias)

**Step 1 — Gauss's Law at the oxide–silicon interface.**
At the onset of strong inversion, the total charge induced in the silicon must equal the charge on the gate plate. Applying Gauss's law across the gate oxide and the depletion region:

$$
Q_G = -\,(Q_B + Q_{inv})
$$

**Step 2 — Express the gate charge via the gate–body voltage.**
The gate-to-body voltage drops across the oxide and the silicon surface potential:

$$
V_{GB} = V_{FB} + \psi_s + \dfrac{\vert Q_G \vert}{C_{ox}}
$$

At the strong-inversion boundary, $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ and $Q_{inv} = 0$ (just at the threshold), so $\vert Q_G \vert = \vert Q_B \vert$:

$$
V_{GB} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\vert Q_B \vert}{C_{ox}}
$$

**Step 3 — Depletion-region charge in terms of doping and surface potential.**
For a uniformly doped p-substrate, integrating Poisson's equation from the bulk to the surface gives:

$$
\vert Q_B \vert = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \,\psi_s}
$$

Substituting $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$:

$$
\vert Q_B \vert = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the threshold voltage V_T0.**
For a 3-terminal device with Source = Body, $V_{GB} = V_{GS} = V_{T0}$. Therefore:

$$
V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
$$

This is the **zero-bias** threshold voltage — a recurring 7-mark derivation in KTU papers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Body-Effect Coefficient γ (Full Algebra)

**Step 1 — Define γ as the sensitivity of V_T to the depletion charge:**

$$
\gamma \equiv \dfrac{1}{C_{ox}}\,\dfrac{d\, \vert Q_B \vert}{d V_{SB}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $\vert Q_B \vert$ as a function of $V_{SB}$.**
With $\psi_s = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$ at strong inversion:

$$
\vert Q_B \vert = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to V_SB:**

$$
\dfrac{d\, \vert Q_B \vert}{d V_{SB}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the γ definition:**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{1}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}
$$

At the threshold point (and as a technology constant), the **conventional textbook form** uses $V_{SB}$ embedded inside the square root, giving the body-effect term seen in the V_T equation:

$$
\Delta V_T = \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

The constant $\gamma$ itself is technology-defined and equal to $\dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Inversion-Layer Charge Density Q_inv(y)

**Step 1 — Local charge balance at position y along the channel.**
Let $V(y)$ be the channel potential at position y measured from the source. The local gate-to-channel voltage is $V_{GS} - V(y)$, and the local surface potential is $2\phi_F + V(y)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the charge-neutrality condition locally:**

$$
Q_G(y) = -\,(Q_B(y) + Q_{inv}(y))
$$

where $Q_B(y) = -\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V(y))}$.

**Step 3 — Express Q_G through the gate voltage and the local oxide drop:**

$$
Q_G(y) = -\,C_{ox}\bigl[V_{GS} - V_{FB} - \psi_s(y)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the threshold condition to obtain the mobile (inversion) charge only.**
After algebraic cancellation of the $2\phi_F + V(y)$ and depletion terms, the **mobile inversion charge** reduces to the well-known linear form:

$$
Q_{inv}(y) = -\,C_{ox}\bigl[V_{GS} - V_T - V(y)\bigr]
$$

This equation is the *cornerstone of the gradual-channel approximation* and is the direct starting point for deriving the MOSFET I–V equation in Module 3.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** An NMOS transistor in a 3-terminal configuration has the following parameters: $t_{ox} = 10$ nm, $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $V_{FB} = -0.9$ V, $\phi_{MS} = -0.55$ V, $V_{SB} = 1$ V. Compute $V_T$ and the body-effect shift.

**Given:** $t_{ox} = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $N_A = 10^{16} \times 10^6 = 10^{22}$ m⁻³, $V_{SB} = 1$ V.

**Step 1 — Oxide capacitance per unit area:**

$$
C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 3.45 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{F/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Fermi potential at 300 K:**

$$
\phi_F = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(6.67 \times 10^5) \approx 0.0259 \times 13.41 \approx 0.347 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Body-effect coefficient γ:**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{22}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator inside the square root: $2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{22} = 3.316 \times 10^{-7}$

$$
\sqrt{3.316 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 5.76 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{-4}}{3.45 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.167 \ \text{V}^{1/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Body-effect shift:**

$$
\Delta V_T = 0.167 \times \left(\sqrt{0.694 + 1} - \sqrt{0.694}\right) = 0.167 \times (1.302 - 0.833) \approx 0.167 \times 0.469 \approx 0.078 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Zero-bias threshold V_T0 (using $V_{FB} = -0.9$ V):**

$$
V_{T0} = -0.9 + 0.694 + \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{-4}}{3.45 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \sqrt{0.694} \approx -0.206 + 0.167 \times 0.833 \approx -0.206 + 0.139 \approx -0.067 \ \text{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Final V_T with body effect:**

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \Delta V_T = -0.067 + 0.078 \approx 0.011 \ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The negative $V_{FB}$ (likely due to positive oxide charge $Q_{ox}$ in this older process) made the device a *natural* (depletion-mode-like) NMOS. Applying $V_{SB} = 1$ V pushed the threshold back to a small positive value — a classic textbook example of using the body effect to *tune* the threshold of an n-channel device.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the 3-Terminal MOS Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    gateT[GATE TERMINAL VGS] --> gateNode[Polysilicon Gate Electrode]
    gateNode --> oxideLayer[SiO2 Gate Oxide tox]
    oxideLayer --> interfaceNode[Si SiO2 Interface]
    interfaceNode --> channelNode[Inversion Layer Channel Qinv]
    channelNode --> condCheck{Channel Conducting}
    condCheck -- VGS less than VT --> cutOff[CUTOFF REGION IDS 0]
    condCheck -- VGS greater than VT --> triode[TRIODE REGION Qinv linear in VDS]
    triode --> satCheck{VDS greater than VGS minus VT}
    satCheck -- No --> triodeMode[Linear Triode Mode]
    satCheck -- Yes --> satMode[SATURATION MODE Pinch Off]
    sourceT[SOURCE TERMINAL] --> nplus1[n plus Source Reservoir]
    drainT[DRAIN TERMINAL] --> nplus2[n plus Drain Reservoir]
    nplus1 --> interfaceNode
    nplus2 --> interfaceNode
    bodyT[BODY SUBSTRATE TIED TO SOURCE] --> pSub[P type Substrate NA]
    pSub --> nplus1
    pSub --> nplus2
    pSub --> depletionNode[Depletion Region QB]
    depletionNode --> interfaceNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The "Channel Conducting" decision block embodies the **V_GS > V_T** test that defines the boundary between cutoff and conduction. The subsequent **V_DS > (V_GS − V_T)** test determines whether the device is in triode or saturation. The two n⁺ reservoirs are the physical "contacts" that make the inversion layer electrically useful.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Map (Mermaid Adaptation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    metalContact1[Metal Contact Source] --> nplusA[n plus Region Source]
    metalContact2[Metal Contact Drain] --> nplusB[n plus Region Drain]
    nplusA --> channelRegion[Inversion Layer at Si SiO2 Interface]
    nplusB --> channelRegion
    gateContact[Metal or Polysilicon Gate Contact] --> gateStack[Gate Stack Metal Poly Oxide]
    gateStack --> channelRegion
    substrateTap[Body Contact p plus Tap] --> pBulk[P type Substrate Bulk]
    pBulk --> nplusA
    pBulk --> nplusB
    pBulk --> depletionZone[Depletion Region below Inversion Layer]
    depletionZone --> channelRegion
    oxideField[Vertical Electric Field from VGS] --> channelRegion
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Board Exams:** A common valuation comment is *"Student drew the device but failed to label the inversion layer, depletion region, and n⁺ source/drain distinctly."* A clean block-level functional map such as the one above earns full marks even when a literal cross-sectional drawing is hard to render in Mermaid.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is it necessary to have two heavily-doped n⁺ regions on either side of the gate in a 3-terminal MOS structure? What role do they play in "contacting the inversion layer"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two n⁺ regions act as the **Source** and **Drain** terminals of the MOSFET. They serve two essential purposes: **(1)** They provide a *reservoir of free electrons* in ohmic contact with the Si–SiO₂ interface, allowing carriers to be injected into the inversion layer when a positive V_GS is applied. **(2)** They provide an *exit path* for the inversion-layer electrons to leave the device as drain current I_DS. Without these n⁺ contacts, the inversion layer would be a floating, inaccessible sheet of charge and no controlled current flow would be possible between any two external terminals.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the threshold voltage V_T of an NMOS transistor. Explain the significance of the surface potential $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ at the onset of strong inversion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **threshold voltage V_T** is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting inversion layer of electrons at the Si–SiO₂ interface of an NMOS transistor. At this voltage, the surface potential reaches $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$ is the Fermi potential of the p-type substrate. The condition $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ signifies that the **electron concentration at the surface equals the bulk hole concentration**, marking the mathematical boundary of *strong inversion*. Below this, only depletion and weak inversion exist, and the device is effectively off.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and operation of a 3-terminal MOS structure. Discuss how the inversion layer is contacted by the source and drain regions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** Begin with a p-type silicon substrate (N_A ≈ 10¹⁵–10¹⁷ cm⁻³). Grow a thin gate oxide (t_ox ≈ 2–20 nm) thermally. Deposit a polysilicon gate on top and pattern it. Using the poly-gate as a self-aligned mask, perform two n⁺ ion-implantation steps on either side of the gate to form the source and drain. Add metal contacts to all four terminals (G, S, D, B).

2. **Inversion-Layer Formation (2 marks):** Apply a positive V_GS. The vertical electric field from the gate attracts electrons (minority carriers) toward the Si–SiO₂ interface. When V_GS exceeds V_T, a thin sheet of electrons — the inversion layer — forms at the interface. Its sheet charge density is $Q_{inv} = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_T)$.

3. **Contacting the Inversion Layer (2 marks):** The two n⁺ regions are essentially infinite reservoirs of electrons. When the inversion layer forms, it is in *ohmic contact* with both the source and drain n⁺ regions. Electrons can now flow from the source n⁺ reservoir, through the inversion-layer channel, and out of the drain n⁺ region — completing the current path. The body (p-substrate) is tied to the source in a 3-terminal configuration to set V_SB = 0.

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage $V_{T0}$ of an NMOS transistor in a 3-terminal configuration, clearly stating the meaning of every term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Charge Neutrality at the Interface (1 mark):**

$$
Q_G = -\,(Q_B + Q_{inv})
$$

2. **Gate-Voltage Partition (1 mark):**

$$
V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \psi_s + \dfrac{\vert Q_G \vert}{C_{ox}}
$$

3. **At the Onset of Strong Inversion (2 marks):** Set $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ and $Q_{inv} = 0$, so $\vert Q_G \vert = \vert Q_B \vert$:

$$
V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\vert Q_B \vert}{C_{ox}}
$$

4. **Depletion Charge (2 marks):**

$$
\vert Q_B \vert = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}
$$

5. **Final Compact Form (1 mark):**

$$
V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
$$

Where $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ is the flat-band voltage, $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area, $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$ is the Fermi potential, and the term under the square root represents the depletion charge.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** What is the body effect in a 3-terminal MOS structure? Derive the complete expression for the threshold voltage $V_T$ when a source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$ is applied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of Body Effect (1 mark):** The *body effect* is the increase in the threshold voltage $V_T$ of a MOSFET when a reverse-bias voltage $V_{SB}$ is applied between the source and the body. It arises because $V_{SB}$ widens the depletion region under the channel, requiring a larger gate voltage to achieve strong inversion.

2. **Surface Potential at Strong Inversion with V_SB (1 mark):**

$$
\psi_{s,inv} = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}
$$

3. **Updated Depletion Charge (2 marks):**

$$
\vert Q_B \vert = \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

4. **Updated Threshold Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

5. **Body-Effect Coefficient γ and Compact Form (1 mark):** Defining $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ and noting that at $V_{SB} = 0$ the equation reduces to $V_{T0}$, we obtain the canonical body-effect equation:

$$
V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

---

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has $t_{ox} = 15$ nm, $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $V_{FB} = -1.0$ V, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. Calculate $V_{T0}$, $\gamma$, and $V_T$ when $V_{SB} = 2$ V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Oxide Capacitance (1 mark):**

$$
C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{15 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2.30 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{F/m}^2
$$

2. **Fermi Potential (1 mark):**

$$
\phi_F = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(3.33 \times 10^6) \approx 0.0259 \times 15.02 \approx 0.389 \ \text{V}
$$

3. **Body-Effect Coefficient γ (2 marks):**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{22}}}{2.30 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator inside square root: $2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{22} = 1.658 \times 10^{-6}$

$$
\sqrt{1.658 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.288 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{1.288 \times 10^{-3}}{2.30 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.560 \ \text{V}^{1/2}
$$

4. **V_T0 (1 mark):**

$$
V_{T0} = -1.0 + 0.778 + \dfrac{1.288 \times 10^{-3}}{2.30 \times 10^{-3}} \times \sqrt{0.778} \approx -0.222 + 0.560 \times 0.882 \approx -0.222 + 0.494 \approx 0.272 \ \text{V}
$$

5. **V_T at V_SB = 2 V (2 marks):**

$$
V_T = 0.272 + 0.560 \times (\sqrt{0.778 + 2} - \sqrt{0.778}) = 0.272 + 0.560 \times (\sqrt{2.778} - 0.882)
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.778} \approx 1.667
$$

$$
V_T = 0.272 + 0.560 \times (1.667 - 0.882) = 0.272 + 0.560 \times 0.785 \approx 0.272 + 0.440 \approx 0.712 \ \text{V}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (from actual KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the body-effect sign:** Students often write $\gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F} - \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}})$, getting the **opposite sign** and hence a *decrease* in $V_T$ with $V_{SB}$. This is a direct contradiction to the physics of widening the depletion region. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $C_{ox}$ with $\varepsilon_{ox}$:** $C_{ox}$ has units of F/m², while $\varepsilon_{ox}$ has units of F/m. Substituting $\varepsilon_{ox}$ in a formula requiring $C_{ox}$ will numerically blow up the answer by a factor of $10^9$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Using $N_A$ in cm⁻³ inside a formula expecting m⁻³:** SI units are mandatory. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Omitting the $V_{FB}$ term in the final $V_{T0}$ expression:** A frequent 2-mark deduction. Always include the flat-band voltage, surface potential, and depletion terms in your *full* answer.
> 5. **Calling the inversion layer a "channel" without defining the conditions for its formation:** Examiners expect the explicit statement $V_{GS} > V_T$ and $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The 3-terminal MOS structure = MOS capacitor + two n⁺ contacts.** The n⁺ source and drain *contact* the otherwise-floating inversion layer, making it electrically accessible.
- **Inversion layer charge:** $Q_{inv} = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_T)$ for $V_{GS} > V_T$.
- **Strong inversion boundary:** $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$.
- **Threshold voltage formula:** $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}/C_{ox}$.
- **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}/C_{ox}$ (units V¹ᐟ²).
- **Body-effect equation:** $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **Self-aligned process:** Gate acts as implant mask → minimal overlap capacitance → enables CMOS scaling.
- **Source-Body tie convention:** In 3-terminal analysis, $V_{SB} = 0$ is assumed unless otherwise stated.
- **Surface potential interpretation:** $\psi_s = 2\phi_F$ is the *mathematical definition* of the strong-inversion threshold; remember it is **not** the threshold voltage itself.
- **Sign convention:** For NMOS, $V_{SB} \ge 0$ (reverse bias) → $\Delta V_T > 0$. For PMOS, the polarities of all charges and voltages are flipped.
- **Practical design rule:** Always tie the body of every NMOS in a stacked configuration back to its own source to *eliminate* the body effect and ensure predictable threshold behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of General Analysis

In the context of Analog VLSI Design, **General Analysis** refers to the systematic, step-by-step methodology employed to characterize the small-signal (AC) behaviour of MOSFET-based analog circuits. It is a unified procedure that converts a complex, nonlinear transistor-level schematic into a tractable **linear small-signal equivalent circuit**, from which all critical performance parameters — voltage gain ($A_v$), current gain ($A_i$), input resistance ($R_{in}$), output resistance ($R_{out}$), transconductance ($g_m$), and body-effect gain degradation ($g_{mb}$) — can be extracted analytically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *General Analysis* is the standardized procedure that begins with **DC bias point determination**, proceeds to **small-signal parameter extraction** ($g_m$, $r_o$, $g_{mb}$), replaces the MOSFET with its **hybrid-π or T-equivalent model**, and finally computes the **transfer function, gain, and port impedances** under small-signal excitation.

### 1.2 The Two Portraits of a MOSFET Circuit

Every analog MOSFET circuit has **two superimposed behaviours**:

1. **Large-Signal (DC) Behaviour** — Determines the **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)**. This is where $I_D$, $V_{GS}$, and $V_{DS}$ are calculated using the square-law equation. No time-varying signals are considered.
2. **Small-Signal (AC) Behaviour** — Once the Q-point is known, we linearize the MOSFET around that point. All signals are assumed to be **infinitesimally small perturbations** ($v_{gs} \ll 2(V_{GS} - V_{th})$) about the Q-point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A transistor is inherently nonlinear ($I_D \propto (V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$). General Analysis assumes the signal is so small that the transistor's behaviour can be *approximated* as linear. This is the **Taylor Series Truncation Principle**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hill and the Hiker"

Imagine a hiker standing on the curved slope of a hill. The hill represents the transistor's nonlinear $I_D$-$V_{GS}$ curve, and the hiker's position is the Q-point.

- **DC Analysis (Setting the Position):** The hiker chooses a steady point on the hill — this is like applying a DC bias $V_{GS}$ so that the transistor idles at a certain $I_D$ and $V_{DS}$.
- **Small-Signal Analysis (Taking Tiny Steps):** When the hiker takes a tiny step, the local slope under his feet is essentially **straight** (linear). The steepness of that local slope is the **transconductance $g_m$**.
- **General Analysis** is the technique of replacing the curved hill with a **flat tangent plane** at the Q-point, then doing all the engineering (finding gain, impedance) on this flat plane.

This is exactly what the **hybrid-π model** does mathematically: it replaces the curved transistor with a small linear circuit of dependent sources and resistors.

### 1.4 Standard Small-Signal Parameters (with physical meaning)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}$ at Q-point | Siemens (A/V) |
| Output resistance | $r_o$ | $\partial V_{DS} / \partial I_D$ (channel-length modulation) | Ohms (Ω) |
| Body transconductance | $g_{mb}$ | $\partial I_D / \partial V_{BS}$ (back-gate effect) | Siemens (A/V) |
| Threshold voltage | $V_{th}$ | Minimum $V_{GS}$ to create inversion layer | Volts (V) |
| Process transconductance | $k_n'$ or $\mu_n C_{ox}$ | Technology parameter | A/V² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of General Analysis (Standard Operating Procedure)

The **General Analysis** procedure can be decomposed into five canonical steps. KTU examiners expect students to follow this exact sequence in problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Five-Step SOP" of General Analysis**
> 1. **Step 1 — DC Analysis:** Replace all capacitors with **open circuits** and inductors with **short circuits**. Find the Q-point $(I_D, V_{GS}, V_{DS})$ using the square-law equation and KVL/KCL.
> 2. **Step 2 — Parameter Extraction:** From the Q-point, calculate $g_m$, $r_o$, and (if body is not tied to source) $g_{mb}$.
> 3. **Step 3 — Small-Signal Substitution:** Replace each MOSFET with its **small-signal equivalent** (hybrid-π or T-model). Replace DC sources with **ground**. Coupling/bypass capacitors become **shorts** at signal frequency.
> 4. **Step 4 — Circuit Simplification:** Apply KCL/KVL on the linearized small-signal model. Combine parallel/series elements.
> 5. **Step 5 — Performance Metric Computation:** Solve for $A_v$, $R_{in}$, $R_{out}$, and any other required quantity.

### 2.2 Square-Law Equations — The Starting Point

For an **NMOS transistor in saturation** (the regime where all small-signal amplifiers operate):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

When channel-length modulation is **neglected** (ideal long-channel):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

where $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ is the **process transconductance** (units: A/V²).

### 2.3 The Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model

The MOSFET is replaced by a small linear circuit with these elements between its four terminals (G, D, S, B):

- **Gate–Source port:** Open circuit (no current flows into the gate of an ideal MOSFET) → $R_{gs} = \infty$.
- **Gate–Drain port:** Open circuit (no $C_{gd}$ at low frequency).
- **Drain–Source port:** A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with output resistance $r_o$.
- **Body–Source port:** A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_{mb} v_{bs}$ injecting current into the drain node.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D}$ | Transconductance from bias current | Saturation, no CLM |
| 2 | $g_m = k_n' (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 2 I_D / (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ | Transconductance from overdrive $V_{OV}$ | Saturation |
| 3 | $r_o = 1 / (\lambda I_D) = V_A / I_D$ | Output resistance (Early effect) | $\lambda$ = CLM parameter |
| 4 | $g_{mb} = \chi \cdot g_m = (\gamma / (2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}})) \cdot g_m$ | Body transconductance | Body not tied to source |
| 5 | $A_{v0} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Intrinsic CS gain (no $R_S$) | Common Source |
| 6 | $A_v = -g_m R_D' / (1 + g_m R_S)$ | CS gain with source degeneration $R_S$ | Source resistor unbypassed |
| 7 | $A_v = g_m R_S' / (1 + g_m R_S')$ | Source Follower gain | $R_S' = R_S \parallel r_o$ |
| 8 | $A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Common Gate gain | Positive gain |
| 9 | $R_{in,CS} = \infty$ | Input resistance, CS stage | Ideal MOSFET gate |
| 10 | $R_{out,CS} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | Output resistance, CS stage | Looking into drain |

> **Notation note:** $R_D' = R_D \parallel r_o$ and $R_S' = R_S \parallel r_o$ — these are the *effective* resistances seen at the drain and source after including $r_o$.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why DC first?** Because $g_m$ and $r_o$ are *functions* of the Q-point. A transistor biased at $I_D = 1\,\text{mA}$ has a very different $g_m$ than one at $I_D = 10\,\mu\text{A}$. We must know *where* the transistor is operating before we can linearize it.
- **Why ground the DC sources?** A DC voltage source (e.g., $V_{DD}$) is constant, so its small-signal variation is zero. Therefore, in small-signal analysis, $V_{DD}$ becomes **AC ground**. Similarly, a DC current source becomes an open circuit (its small-signal value is zero).
- **Why short the capacitors?** At the signal frequency of interest (typically mid-band), coupling and bypass capacitors are assumed to have negligible impedance, behaving as short circuits. This couples the signal between stages and grounds AC signals at the source (for a bypass capacitor $C_S$).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

General Analysis is the **backbone of analog IC design**. Without it:
- **Op-amp design** would be impossible (every op-amp is a cascade of CS, CG, and CD stages).
- **RF front-ends** in mobile phones couldn't be designed (low-noise amplifiers are CS/CG cascades).
- **Analog filters** couldn't be synthesized (transconductors and OTAs rely on $g_m$).
- **Data converters** (ADCs/DACs) use these gain stages as building blocks.

The **two-stage Miller op-amp**, the **telescopic cascode**, and the **folded cascode** — all KTU syllabus favorites — are nothing but stacked CS/CG stages whose gains multiply by General Analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $g_m$ from the Square-Law Equation

Starting from the ideal saturation equation (channel-length modulation neglected for clarity):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 1 — Take the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$, holding $V_{DS}$ and $V_{BS}$ constant.** This is the formal definition of transconductance, since $V_{GS}$ is the controlling variable for $I_D$:

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$ is the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$** and substitute:

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $V_{OV}$ in favour of the bias current $I_D$.** From the square-law:

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k_n' (W/L)}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the $g_m$ expression:**

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k_n' (W/L)}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by pulling constants inside the square root:**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

This is the **most-used form in KTU problems** because it expresses $g_m$ purely in terms of bias current and device dimensions, both of which the designer controls.

### 3.2 Derivation of $r_o$ from Channel-Length Modulation

The full saturation equation with CLM is:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

**Step 1 — Take the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{DS}$, holding $V_{GS}$ constant.** This is the output conductance $g_{ds}$:

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \cdot \lambda = \lambda I_D$$

**Step 2 — The output resistance is the reciprocal:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalently, using the Early voltage $V_A = 1/\lambda$:**

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

**Intuition:** A larger $\lambda$ (shorter channel) means the drain current is more sensitive to $V_{DS}$, which corresponds to a *smaller* $r_o$ and hence *lower* intrinsic gain. This is one of the main reasons short-channel analog design is challenging.

### 3.3 Derivation of Common-Source Voltage Gain with Source Degeneration

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2. We consider an NMOS CS stage with drain resistor $R_D$, source resistor $R_S$ (unbypassed), and the small-signal hybrid-π model with $r_o$ and $g_{mb}$ initially neglected for clarity.

**Step 1 — Replace the MOSFET with the small-signal model.** Apply a test input $v_{in}$ at the gate. The source is not at AC ground because $R_S$ is unbypassed.

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the source node.** The current leaving the source node through $R_S$ must equal the current from the dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ (which flows from D to S inside the model):

$$i_s = g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $v_s = i_s R_S$ and that $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$.** Substitute:

$$g_m (v_{in} - v_s) = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_s$ in terms of $v_{in}$:**

$$g_m v_{in} = g_m v_s + \frac{v_s}{R_S} = v_s \left(g_m + \frac{1}{R_S}\right)$$

$$v_s = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S} v_{in}$$

**Step 5 — The output voltage is the drop across $R_D$ due to the drain current** $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$ (flowing from D to S externally, so $v_{out} = -i_d R_D$):

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} R_D = -g_m (v_{in} - v_s) R_D$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expression for $v_s$:**

$$v_{out} = -g_m \left(v_{in} - \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S} v_{in}\right) R_D$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $v_{in}$ and simplify:**

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{in} \left(\frac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Hence the voltage gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 9 — With $r_o$ included** (i.e., $R_D' = R_D \parallel r_o$):

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit Check:** When $R_S = 0$ (bypassed), $A_v = -g_m R_D'$, recovering the standard CS gain. ✓

### 3.4 Complete Worked Example — KTU Style Problem

**Problem:** An NMOS transistor in a CS amplifier has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 50$, $I_D = 0.5\,\text{mA}$, $\lambda = 0.01\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_S = 0$ (bypassed). Find $g_m$, $r_o$, and $A_v$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D} = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.236 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S} = 2.236\,\text{mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r_o$:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective drain resistance:**

$$R_D' = R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 200}{5 + 200}\,\text{k}\Omega = \frac{1000}{205}\,\text{k}\Omega = 4.878\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the CS gain (with $R_S = 0$, no degeneration):**

$$A_v = -g_m R_D' = -2.236 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.878 \times 10^3 = -10.91\,\text{V/V}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB (optional but common in KTU):**

$$A_v\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(10.91) = 20.76\,\text{dB}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Basic Stages

This matrix is **highly examinable** in KTU and should be memorized.

| Parameter | Common Source (CS) | Common Gate (CG) | Common Drain (CD / Source Follower) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input at | Gate | Source | Gate |
| Output at | Drain | Drain | Source |
| $A_v$ (ideal) | $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $\dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ |
| Sign of $A_v$ | **Inverting** (negative) | **Non-inverting** (positive) | **Non-inverting** (positive) |
| $R_{in}$ (no $R_S$) | $\infty$ | $1/g_m$ | $\infty$ |
| $R_{out}$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | $1/g_m \parallel R_S \parallel r_o$ |
| High-frequency response | Poor (Miller effect) | Excellent (no Miller) | Good |
| Use case | Voltage gain stage | Current buffer / RF input | Level shifter, output buffer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The General Analysis Workflow (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block captures the canonical five-step procedure, which is the heart of "General Analysis" in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Transistor-Level Schematic] --> B[Step 1: DC Analysis]
    B --> B1[Open all capacitors]
    B1 --> B2[Short all inductors]
    B2 --> B3[Apply KVL KCL on gate loop]
    B3 --> B4[Solve for Q-point ID, VGS, VDS]
    B4 --> C[Step 2: Extract Small-Signal Parameters]
    C --> C1[Compute gm from ID]
    C --> C1[Compute gm]
    C --> C2[Compute ro from lambda ID]
    C --> C3[Compute gmb if body not tied to source]
    C2 --> D[Step 3: Draw Small-Signal Model]
    D --> D1[Replace MOSFET with hybrid-pi circuit]
    D1 --> D2[Ground all DC supplies]
    D2 --> D3[Short all coupling and bypass capacitors]
    D3 --> E[Step 4: Solve Linear Circuit]
    E --> E1[Apply KCL at drain node]
    E1 --> E2[Apply KCL at source node]
    E2 --> E3[Apply KCL at body node if needed]
    E3 --> F[Step 5: Compute Performance Metrics]
    F --> F1[Voltage Gain Av]
    F --> F2[Input Resistance Rin]
    F --> F3[Output Resistance Rout]
    F --> F4[Current Gain Ai]
    F4 --> G[End: Report Av, Rin, Rout]
```

### 4.2 Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **functional structure of the MOSFET's small-signal equivalent** — the central object that replaces the transistor in Step 3 of General Analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Gate Node] -. infinite impedance .-> N1[Internal Node GS]
    N1 --> S[Source Node]
    D[Drain Node] --> R1[Output Resistance ro]
    R1 --> S
    D --> CS[Controlled Current Source gm vgs]
    CS --> S
    B[Body or Bulk Node] --> CM[Controlled Current Source gmb vbs]
    CM --> S
    N1 --- CB[Body to Source Coupling vbs]
    CB --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Between G and S, no current flows (open circuit). Between D and S, the MOSFET behaves as a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$. The body-source port contributes an additional dependent source $g_{mb} v_{bs}$ which feeds current into the drain side. This is the exact small-signal circuit you draw in your exam.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Three Basic Amplifier Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS[Common Source Stage]
        CS_IN[Vin at Gate] --> CS_AMP[MOSFET with RD at Drain]
        CS_AMP --> CS_OUT[Inverting Output at Drain]
    end
    subgraph CG[Common Gate Stage]
        CG_IN[Vin at Source] --> CG_AMP[MOSFET with RD at Drain]
        CG_AMP --> CG_OUT[Non-Inverting Output at Drain]
    end
    subgraph CD[Common Drain Source Follower]
        CD_IN[Vin at Gate] --> CD_AMP[MOSFET with RS at Source]
        CD_AMP --> CD_OUT[Non-Inverting Output at Source]
    end
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow in a General Analysis Problem

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DC as DC Bias Source
    participant Sig as AC Input Signal
    participant Q as Q-Point of MOSFET
    participant SS as Small-Signal Model
    participant Out as Output Node
```
