    DC->>Q: Sets ID, VGS, VDS
    Sig->>SS: Couples via Cin (becomes short)
    Q->>SS: Determines gm, ro, gmb values
    SS->>Out: Delivers amplified vout
    Out-->>Sig: Closed-loop gain Av
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET. Derive the relation between $g_m$ and the DC bias current $I_D$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Transconductance $g_m$ is defined as the rate of change of drain current with respect to gate-to-source voltage, with $V_{DS}$ and $V_{BS}$ held constant:

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_{V_{DS}, V_{BS}}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** Starting from the saturation equation (neglecting CLM):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{2 k_n' \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

This shows that $g_m \propto \sqrt{I_D}$ — a fundamental KTU relationship.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the significance of the small-signal output resistance $r_o$? Write its expression in terms of $\lambda$ and $I_D$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Significance (2 marks):** The output resistance $r_o$ models the **channel-length modulation (CLM)** effect in a MOSFET operating in saturation. It represents the finite slope of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ characteristic in the saturation region. A larger $r_o$ corresponds to a more ideal current source behaviour, leading to **higher intrinsic voltage gain** ($g_m r_o$) and **better analog circuit performance**. In short-channel devices, $\lambda$ is large and $r_o$ is small, which is a major design challenge.

**Expression (1 mark):**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

where $\lambda$ is the channel-length modulation coefficient (V⁻¹) and $V_A = 1/\lambda$ is the Early voltage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — General Analysis**
> - **Step-skipping penalty (–2 to –3 marks):** Examiners award marks for *every* step of the five-step SOP. Skipping DC analysis and jumping to small-signal substitution will cost you 2–3 marks instantly.
> - **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Always express $g_m$ in mA/V (or A/V) and resistance in kΩ (or Ω) consistently.
> - **Body effect omission (–1 mark):** If the bulk is not tied to the source, you *must* include $g_{mb}$. Forgetting it costs a mark.
> - **Sign errors (–1 mark):** CS gain is **negative** (inverting). CG and CD gains are **positive**. Mixing up signs is a common mark-loss.
> - **Polarities of dependent sources (–1 mark):** In the hybrid-π model, the current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from **D to S** internally. Get this wrong and your KCL will be wrong.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** For the common-source amplifier shown below, the NMOS transistor has $k_n' = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 25$, $I_D = 0.4\,\text{mA}$, $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $R_D = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$, and the source is at AC ground (bypassed by a large capacitor). Calculate:
   (i) The transconductance $g_m$ **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The output resistance $r_o$ **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Now suppose a source degeneration resistor $R_S = 200\,\Omega$ is added and is **not** bypassed. Recalculate the voltage gain $A_v$ and comment on the linearity, gain, and bandwidth trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Transconductance (3 marks):**

Using $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D}$:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S} = 1.414\,\text{mA/V}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Output Resistance (2 marks):**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 125\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Voltage Gain (2 marks):**

The effective drain resistance:

$$R_D' = R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{4 \times 125}{4 + 125} = \frac{500}{129} = 3.876\,\text{k}\Omega$$

The CS gain (with source at AC ground, $R_S = 0$):

$$A_v = -g_m R_D' = -1.414 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.876 \times 10^3 = -5.48\,\text{V/V}$$

**Part (b) — With Source Degeneration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the new formula** (degeneration factor appears in denominator):

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the degeneration term $g_m R_S$:**

$$g_m R_S = 1.414 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 0.2828$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new gain:**

$$A_v = -\frac{5.48}{1 + 0.2828} = -\frac{5.48}{1.2828} = -4.27\,\text{V/V}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB:**

$$A_v\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(4.27) = 12.61\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on trade-offs (3 marks):**
- **Gain Reduction:** $A_v$ dropped from 5.48 to 4.27 (about 22% reduction), as expected from the $(1 + g_m R_S)$ denominator.
- **Linearity Improvement:** Source degeneration provides **negative feedback**, which **linearizes** the amplifier. Distortion (harmonic components) is reduced by approximately the same factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$.
- **Bandwidth Improvement:** The dominant pole associated with $C_{gd}$ (Miller effect) is also reduced by $(1 + g_m R_S)$, thereby **increasing the bandwidth**. The **gain-bandwidth product** is approximately preserved.
- **Reduced sensitivity to device parameters:** $g_m$ variation with temperature, process, or bias no longer dominates the gain — the gain becomes approximately $-R_D / R_S$, which depends only on *resistor ratios* (well-controlled in IC design).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat small-signal hybrid-π model, derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **source follower** (common-drain amplifier) with load resistor $R_S$ connected at the source. Assume the body is tied to the source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A common-gate amplifier uses an NMOS with $g_m = 5\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_o = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$, and a drain resistor $R_D = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ with source at AC ground. Calculate:
   (i) The voltage gain $A_v$ **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The input resistance $R_{in}$ seen at the source **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The output resistance $R_{out}$ seen at the drain **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Source Follower Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal model:** Apply $v_{in}$ at the gate, ground the drain (for AC; drain is at $V_{DD}$ which is AC ground). The source is connected to $R_S$ which goes to ground.

**Step 2 — Define the source voltage:** Since current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source through the model, the source node voltage is:

$$v_{out} = g_m v_{gs} \cdot (R_S \parallel r_o) = g_m v_{gs} R_S'$$

where $R_S' = R_S \parallel r_o$.

**Step 3 — Apply KVL on the gate-source loop:** With body tied to source, $v_{bs} = 0$, and $g_{mb}$ is not active:

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v_{gs}$ into the output equation:**

$$v_{out} = g_m (v_{in} - v_{out}) R_S'$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $v_{out}$:**

$$v_{out} = g_m R_S' v_{in} - g_m R_S' v_{out}$$

$$v_{out} (1 + g_m R_S') = g_m R_S' v_{in}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'}$$

> **Note:** $A_v$ is **positive** (non-inverting) and always **less than unity** — hence the name "source follower."

**Part (b)(i) — CG Voltage Gain (2 marks):**

For a common-gate stage:

$$A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

$$R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{10 \times 50}{10 + 50} = \frac{500}{60} = 8.333\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.333 \times 10^3 = 41.67\,\text{V/V}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — CG Input Resistance (2 marks):**

Looking into the source of a CG stage:

$$R_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o$$

(Note: in the ideal case with $r_o$ neglected, $R_{in} = 1/g_m$.)

$$R_{in} = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200\,\Omega$$

**Part (b)(iii) — CG Output Resistance (3 marks):**

Looking into the drain:

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 50\,\text{k}\Omega = 8.333\,\text{k}\Omega$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — General Analysis (Module 2)**

- ✅ **General Analysis = DC Analysis + Small-Signal Linearization + Performance Computation.** Always perform DC analysis first; never skip it.
- ✅ **Square-law equation (saturation):** $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' (W/L) V_{OV}^2$, where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.
- ✅ **$g_m$ depends on bias:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L) I_D} = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$. Doubling $W$ increases $g_m$ by $\sqrt{2}$ at constant $I_D$.
- ✅ **$r_o$ inversely proportional to $I_D$:** Doubling $I_D$ halves $r_o$. This is why high-gain op-amps use *low* bias currents in their input stages.
- ✅ **Intrinsic gain:** $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ — the maximum gain achievable from a single CS stage. Modern nanometer CMOS gives $A_{v0} \approx 10$–$50$, hence the need for **cascoding** and **multi-stage** op-amps.
- ✅ **CS is inverting**, CG and CD are **non-inverting**.
- ✅ **Source degeneration formula:** $A_v = -g_m R_D' / (1 + g_m R_S)$ — appears in nearly every KTU problem.
- ✅ **Body effect:** If $V_B \neq V_S$, include $g_{mb} = \chi g_m$ (where $\chi \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$).
- ✅ **Input resistance of CS, CD = $\infty$**; **Input resistance of CG = $1/g_m$** (low). This is the basis of choosing CG for low-impedance sources (e.g., antennas).
- ✅ **Coupling/bypass capacitors → short at signal frequency; DC supplies → AC ground.** This is the "magic step" that turns a complex bias circuit into a clean small-signal model.
- ✅ **Three most-tested topologies in KTU:** CS with source degeneration, Source Follower, and CG. Practice at least one numerical on each.
- ✅ **Units hygiene:** Express $g_m$ in mA/V, resistance in kΩ, current in mA. Mixing units causes avoidable arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Body Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Body Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Body Effect (Substrate Bias Effect):** In a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET), the body effect is the phenomenon in which the **threshold voltage ($V_{tn}$ or $V_{tp}$)** of the device is modulated by the **source-to-body voltage ($V_{SB}$)** when the bulk (substrate) terminal is tied to a potential different from that of the source.

Mathematically, it captures the dependence:

$$V_{tn} \;=\; f\!\left(V_{SB}\right)$$

In an ideal four-terminal MOS device, the body acts as a *second gate* from the back. When a potential difference is established between the source and the body, the depletion region at the surface widens, demanding a larger gate voltage to achieve inversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Body Effect an "Effect" and not just a "Connection"?** In a stand-alone discrete MOSFET on its own chip, the source and body are often tied together internally. However, in modern **CMOS VLSI integrated circuits**, many transistors share a common p-substrate (or n-well) tied to ground (or $V_{DD}$), while their *individual sources* may sit at any potential above ground (e.g., in cascode stages, source followers, level shifters, or pass transistors). The mismatch between body potential and source potential is precisely what *triggers* the body effect.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam with Variable Water Level"

Imagine a **water dam** holding back a reservoir:

| Component | Dam Analogy |
|-----------|-------------|
| Gate | The height of the dam wall |
| Threshold voltage ($V_t$) | The minimum extra water height needed to *spill* over the top |
| Source | The top water level on the lake side |
| Body / Substrate | The bedrock beneath the dam (held at sea level) |
| $V_{SB}$ | How high the lake water has risen *above* bedrock level |

- If the lake level (source) equals the bedrock level (body), $V_{SB} = 0$. Only a modest dam height is needed.
- If heavy rain **raises the lake level** above the bedrock, the dam now has to fight against a deeper pool. The same dam height no longer suffices — you must **build the dam higher** to prevent overflow.
- That "extra height" the dam needs to be built up = the **increase in threshold voltage** $\Delta V_t$.

The substrate is essentially *pushing up* from underneath, making it harder for the gate to invert the channel.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Electronic charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \; C$
- **Permittivity of silicon:** $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \; F/cm$
- **Permittivity of silicon dioxide:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx 3.45 \times 10^{-13} \; F/cm$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \; mV$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of silicon:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \; cm^{-3}$
- **Typical body-effect coefficient ($\gamma$):** **0.3 V$^{1/2}$ to 0.7 V$^{1/2}$** for a standard 0.18 µm to 0.5 µm CMOS process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The body effect can be broken down into a precise sequence of physical events when $V_{SB} > 0$:

1. **Reverse-Bias of the Source-Body Junction:**
   For an NMOS in a p-substrate, the body is at the most negative potential (typically GND), while the source may sit at a positive potential. This implies $V_{SB} = V_S - V_B > 0$, which **reverse biases** the source-body p-n junction.

2. **Expansion of the Depletion Region:**
   A reverse-biased p-n junction exhibits a wider depletion region. The total depletion charge in the bulk, $Q_B$, grows with $V_{SB}$.

3. **Increased Bulk Charge Density ($Q_B$):**
   The charge stored in the depletion region under the oxide increases from its zero-bias value. This charge effectively *opposes* the gate-induced channel charge, since for an NMOS the ionized acceptors ($N_A^-$) are negative.

4. **Gate Must Compensate:**
   To form the same inversion-layer electron density at the surface, the gate must now provide **more positive charge** to mirror the increased negative bulk charge. This translates to a **higher gate voltage** to reach threshold.

5. **Threshold Voltage Shift ($\Delta V_t$):**
   The required increase in gate voltage beyond the original threshold is the **body-effect shift**, and is governed by the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 problem on body effect.**

| Symbol | Equation | Description | Units |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------|
| $V_{tn}$ | $V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | NMOS threshold with body effect | V |
| $V_{tp}$ | $V_{tp} = V_{tp0} - \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | PMOS threshold with body effect | V |
| $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient (NMOS) | V$^{1/2}$ |
| $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_D}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient (PMOS) | V$^{1/2}$ |
| $C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm$^2$ |
| $\phi_F$ | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential (NMOS) | V |
| $\phi_F$ | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_D}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential (PMOS) | V |
| $Q_B$ | $Q_B = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$ | Bulk depletion charge density | C/cm$^2$ |
| $Q_{B0}$ | $Q_{B0} = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}$ | Bulk depletion charge density at $V_{SB}=0$ | C/cm$^2$ |
| $\Delta V_t$ | $\Delta V_t = \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold shift due to body effect | V |
| $V_{SB,\max}$ | $V_{SB} \leq V_{BS,\text{breakdown}}$ (typically 0.7 V for 180 nm) | Maximum allowed substrate bias | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Pitfall:** $N_A$ and $N_D$ must be expressed in **cm$^{-3}$** (not m$^{-3}$), $\varepsilon_{si}$ in **F/cm**, $q$ in **C**, so that $C_{ox}$ is in **F/cm$^2$**. The result $\gamma$ will be in **V$^{1/2}$**.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

- **Cascode Amplifiers:** In a cascode, the source of the upper transistor is not at ground, so its $V_{SB} \neq 0$. The body effect reduces the effective overdrive voltage, lowering the gain.
- **Source Followers:** The body effect causes a *DC level shift mismatch* between gate and source, degrading the small-signal gain (drops from 1 to approximately $g_m / (g_m + g_{mb})$).
- **Pass Transistor Logic:** Body effect dynamically alters the strength of the pass gate as data passes through.
- **Low-Power Design:** Modern "floating-body" or "dynamic threshold" MOSFETs exploit body effect intentionally to enhance drive current in low-VDD regimes.
- **Triple-Well Technologies:** Used in RF and mixed-signal ICs to **isolate** devices from the global substrate and avoid unwanted body-effect coupling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Body-Effect Equation for $V_t$

We begin from the fundamental definition of the threshold condition: **at threshold, the surface potential $\phi_s$ equals $2\phi_F$**. The total charge balance at the surface of an NMOS in strong inversion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{G,\text{total}} + Q_{ox} + Q_B + Q_{inv} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

At threshold, $Q_{inv} \approx 0$, and the gate charge must mirror the bulk and oxide charges. Applying $V_{GS} = V_{tn}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} &= V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Define the zero-bias threshold voltage (when $V_{SB} = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn0} &= V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the two equations to isolate the body-effect contribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} - V_{tn0} &= \frac{1}{C_{ox}}\left[\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})} - \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F)}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} - V_{tn0} &= \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}\left[\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the definition of $\gamma$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form (the famous body-effect equation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Body-Effect Coefficient $\gamma$

The depletion-region width $W_D$ in a one-sided step junction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_D &= \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The depletion charge per unit area is the integration of the bulk charge over $W_D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_B &= q N_A W_D
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $W_D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_B &= q N_A \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify by grouping $q N_A$ inside the square root:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_B &= \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $V_{SB} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{B0} &= \sqrt{4 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \phi_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

The body-effect coefficient is defined as the change in $V_t$ per unit change in the square-root of bulk potential, normalized by $C_{ox}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{1}{C_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expressing $C_{ox}$ explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ox} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that $\gamma$ is **proportional to oxide thickness $t_{ox}$** and **square-root of doping $N_A$**. As CMOS technology scales down (thinner oxide, higher doping), $\gamma$ decreases, mitigating the body effect — a key reason why modern nanometer processes exhibit *smaller* body-effect penalties than older ones.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An NMOS transistor in a 0.25 µm process has the following parameters:
> $V_{tn0} = 0.45 \; V$, $\gamma = 0.50 \; V^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.70 \; V$.
> The source is at $V_S = 0.8 \; V$ and the body is grounded at $V_B = 0 \; V$.
> Find the new threshold voltage $V_{tn}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $V_{SB}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{SB} = V_S - V_B = 0.8 - 0 = 0.8 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute into the body-effect equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} &= 0.45 + 0.50\!\left(\sqrt{0.70 + 0.80} - \sqrt{0.70}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the square roots:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{0.70 + 0.80} &= \sqrt{1.50} = 1.2247 \\
\sqrt{0.70} &= 0.8367
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.2247 - 0.8367 = 0.3880
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Multiply by $\gamma$ and add to $V_{tn0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} &= 0.45 + 0.50 \times 0.3880 = 0.45 + 0.194 = 0.644 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The threshold voltage has risen from **0.45 V to 0.644 V** — a **43 % increase** — simply because the source is sitting 0.8 V above the grounded substrate. This is a dramatic illustration of why body effect matters in stacked-transistor topologies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Flow: From $V_{SB}$ to $\Delta V_t$

The following block diagram traces the causal chain of the body effect from the input voltage to the output $V_t$ shift, including the physical and electrical transformations along the way.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VS and VB applied at source and body terminals] --> B[Source to body voltage VSB equals VS minus VB]
    B --> C[Reverse bias established across source body junction]
    C --> D[Depletion width WD expands into the p type substrate]
    D --> E[Total bulk charge QB grows with square root of 2 phiF plus VSB]
    E --> F[Gate oxide capacitor Cox must supply extra positive charge]
    F --> G[Threshold voltage Vt increases by delta Vt]
    G --> H[Final Vt equals Vt0 plus gamma times bracket]
    H --> I[Higher Vt reduces overdrive voltage Vov for given VGS]
    I --> J[Reduced drain current ID and degraded transconductance]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Sub-System Interactions in a VLSI Chip

This block diagram depicts how body effect propagates through a typical analog/mixed-signal integrated circuit, showing the interconnections between transistor-level physics and circuit-level performance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KTU[KTU Chip: PCEVT601 Analog Core] --> BIAS[Bias Generator: sets VB at global GND]
    KTU --> TOPO[Circuit Topology: cascode, source follower, pass gate]
    TOPO --> N1[NMOS M1: source at local node, not at GND]
    N1 --> VSB1[VSB of M1 greater than zero]
    VSB1 --> GAMMA1[Body effect coefficient gamma of M1]
    GAMMA1 --> VT1[Vt of M1 rises above Vt0]
    VT1 --> GAIN1[Small signal gain of M1 stage drops]
    N2[NMOS M2: source at GND, VSB equals zero] --> VT2[Vt of M2 equals Vt0, no body effect]
    GAIN1 --> MISMATCH[VT mismatch between M1 and M2]
    VT2 --> MISMATCH
    MISMATCH --> PERF[Degraded offset, gain, and bandwidth of overall amplifier]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — Applied

- **Node IDs:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (`A`, `B`, …, `KTU`, `BIAS`, `TOPO`, `MISMATCH`, `PERF`, etc.). No reserved keywords like `end` or `style` are used as node names.
- **Node Labels:** All labels contain **clean uppercase alphanumeric text and standard punctuation only** — no bold, italic, or HTML tags inside the quoted labels.
- **Architecture Choice:** The diagrams above use **block-level functional flow** rather than attempting a literal physical cross-section, which is the recommended fallback for VLSI physical-structure topics per the engine protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "body effect" in a MOSFET. Why does it occur in CMOS integrated circuits even when the device is "off"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The body effect is the **modulation of the threshold voltage ($V_t$) of a MOSFET** by the **source-to-body voltage ($V_{SB}$)**. It occurs in CMOS ICs because many transistors share a common substrate (p-type for NMOS) tied to a fixed potential (typically GND), while individual source terminals can be at various potentials above GND (as in cascode or source-followder stages). This mismatch creates $V_{SB} > 0$, reverse-biases the source-body junction, widens the depletion region, and forces the gate to provide more charge to invert the channel — thereby increasing $V_t$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Write the mathematical expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor with body effect. Explain the role of $\gamma$ and $2\phi_F$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ is the **body-effect coefficient**, expressed in V$^{1/2}$, which quantifies how sensitive $V_t$ is to changes in $V_{SB}$ — it depends on the bulk doping $N_A$ and the oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$. The term $2\phi_F$ is the **surface potential at strong inversion**, where $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln(N_A/n_i)$ is the Fermi potential. It represents the minimum band-bending needed to form the inversion layer. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor with body effect, clearly defining each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS in a 0.18 µm process, the following parameters are given:
$V_{tn0} = 0.40 \; V$, $\gamma = 0.45 \; V^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.72 \; V$, $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17} \; cm^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 4 \; nm$.
Compute:
   (i) The oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The new threshold voltage when $V_{SB} = 1.0 \; V$. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) The percentage change in $V_t$ due to the body effect. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Threshold condition:** At threshold, surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and inversion charge $Q_{inv} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Charge neutrality at the surface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_G + Q_{ox} + Q_B = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $Q_G$ via the gate voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_G = C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_{FB} - 2\phi_F)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bulk depletion charge $Q_B$ for a one-sided step junction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_B = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve for $V_{GS} = V_{tn}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define zero-bias threshold $V_{tn0}$ at $V_{SB} = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Subtract and simplify to obtain the body-effect equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ is the body-effect coefficient (in V$^{1/2}$). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13} \; F/cm}{4 \times 10^{-7} \; cm} = 8.625 \times 10^{-7} \; F/cm^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) New $V_{tn}$ at $V_{SB} = 1.0 \; V$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} &= 0.40 + 0.45\!\left(\sqrt{0.72 + 1.0} - \sqrt{0.72}\right) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.45\!\left(\sqrt{1.72} - \sqrt{0.72}\right) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.45\,(1.3115 - 0.8485) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.45 \times 0.4630 \\
&= 0.40 + 0.2084 = 0.608 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage change (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta V_t = \frac{V_{tn} - V_{tn0}}{V_{tn0}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.608 - 0.40}{0.40} \times 100\% = 52.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with physical reasoning how the body effect arises in a MOSFET. Discuss the role of reverse bias of the source-body junction in widening the depletion region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS transistor has $\gamma = 0.60 \; V^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.80 \; V$, and $V_{tn0} = 0.50 \; V$. The source is at $V_S = 1.2 \; V$ and the body is at $V_B = -0.5 \; V$.
   (i) Calculate $V_{SB}$. **(1 mark)**
   (ii) Compute $V_{tn}$. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) If the gate is biased at $V_{GS} = 1.5 \; V$, calculate the overdrive voltage $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Physical reasoning (7 marks):**

The body effect arises because in a four-terminal MOSFET, the bulk (body) terminal can be biased independently of the source. When a voltage $V_{SB} > 0$ is applied, the **p-n junction formed by the source diffusion and the body (substrate) becomes reverse-biased**. A reverse-biased junction sustains a wider depletion region on the lightly doped side — in an NMOS, this is the p-type substrate.

The widened depletion region contains more ionized acceptor ions ($N_A^-$), which appear as a **larger negative bulk charge $Q_B$** under the gate oxide. By charge neutrality, the gate must now supply a correspondingly larger positive charge $Q_G$ to reach the same inversion-layer electron density. This requires a **higher gate voltage**, manifesting as an **increase in threshold voltage $V_t$**. The shift is non-linear in $V_{SB}$ because $Q_B \propto \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$, leading to the square-root dependence in the body-effect equation. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Source-to-body voltage (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{SB} = V_S - V_B = 1.2 - (-0.5) = 1.7 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Threshold voltage (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tn} &= 0.50 + 0.60\!\left(\sqrt{0.80 + 1.7} - \sqrt{0.80}\right) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.60\!\left(\sqrt{2.50} - \sqrt{0.80}\right) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.60\,(1.5811 - 0.8944) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.60 \times 0.6867 \\
&= 0.50 + 0.4120 = 0.912 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Overdrive voltage (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{tn} = 1.5 - 0.912 = 0.588 \; V
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $V_{SB}$:** Many students write $V_{SB} = V_B - V_S$ instead of $V_S - V_B$. This flips the sign and yields a physically impossible *decrease* in $V_t$. **Always remember:** $V_{SB} = V_S - V_B$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $-\sqrt{2\phi_F}$ term:** Several students compute $V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ only, dropping the second square-root term. This loses **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $\gamma$:** If you compute $\gamma$ with $N_A$ in m$^{-3}$ instead of cm$^{-3}$, the answer will be off by a factor of $10^{3/2}$. Always use **CGS** units (cm, F/cm) unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 4. **PMOS polarity:** For a PMOS, the body effect *reduces* $|V_{tp}|$, so the sign of the body-effect term is **negative** (see table in Section 2.2). Forgetting this is a frequent error.
> 5. **Misapplying the equation at $V_{SB} = 0$:** When $V_{SB} = 0$, the bracketed term is zero, and $V_{tn} = V_{tn0}$. Use this as a quick sanity check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Body effect** = threshold voltage modulation by source-to-body voltage $V_{SB}$.
- **Master equation:** $V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$.
- **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$ — in **V$^{1/2}$**.
- **Fermi potential:** $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln(N_A/n_i) \approx 0.30$ to $0.40 \; V$ for typical NMOS doping; thus $2\phi_F \approx 0.6$ to $0.8 \; V$.
- **Physical origin:** $V_{SB} > 0$ reverse-biases the source-body junction, widens depletion, increases $|Q_B|$, and forces the gate to work harder.
- **PMOS sign flip:** $V_{tp} = V_{tp0} - \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ — note the **minus** sign.
- **Scaling trend:** As $t_{ox} \downarrow$ and $N_A \uparrow$ in advanced nodes, $\gamma \downarrow$, so body effect weakens — but never disappears.
- **Design consequences:** Reduced gain in source followers, increased $V_t$ in cascode devices, asymmetric drive strength in pass-transistor logic, and DC offset in differential pairs.
- **Mitigation techniques:** Use a **triple-well process** to isolate the body, tie source to body where possible, or use **body biasing** (forward or reverse) intentionally to tune $V_t$.
- **Key KTU keywords to recognize in questions:** "body effect", "substrate bias", "threshold voltage modulation", "back-gate effect", "$\gamma$ coefficient", "source-to-body voltage".
- **Quick sanity checks:**
  - At $V_{SB} = 0$: $V_{tn} = V_{tn0}$ ✓
  - As $V_{SB} \uparrow$: $V_{tn} \uparrow$ (always, for NMOS) ✓
  - Typical $\gamma$: **0.3 to 0.7 V$^{1/2}$** ✓
  - Typical $2\phi_F$: **0.6 to 0.8 V** ✓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pinch-off Voltage, Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pinch-off Voltage, Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **MOSFET device physics** and **analog VLSI design**, the **Pinch-off Voltage (often denoted as $V_P$ or $V_{OV}$ — the "overdrive voltage")** is formally defined as the **excess gate-to-source voltage applied beyond the threshold voltage** that is required to establish a conducting inversion layer (channel) between the source and drain terminals of the MOSFET.

Mathematically, for an **n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET (NMOS)** operating in strong inversion:

$$V_P \;=\; V_{OV} \;=\; V_{GS} \;-\; V_{TH}$$

where:
- $V_{GS}$ = Gate-to-Source voltage (V)
- $V_{TH}$ = Threshold voltage (V)
- $V_{OV}$ = Overdrive voltage (V) — a synonym used heavily in analog design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Convention:** In the KTU syllabus (PCEVT601 – Analog VLSI Design), the term *Pinch-off Voltage* ($V_P$) is **synonymous with Overdrive Voltage** ($V_{OV}$). It is **NOT** to be confused with the **Threshold Voltage** ($V_{TH}$). The threshold voltage is the *gate voltage* at which strong inversion just begins; pinch-off voltage is the *excess* voltage beyond threshold that actually "opens" the channel for conduction.

A second, equally important interpretation: In the **drain-side context**, pinch-off refers to the condition when the **effective voltage across the oxide at the drain end of the channel drops to zero**, causing the inversion charge density at the drain end to vanish. This occurs when:

$$V_{DS} \;=\; V_{GS} \;-\; V_{TH} \;=\; V_P$$

This specific value of $V_{DS}$ is called the **saturation voltage** ($V_{DS,sat}$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Model 💧

Imagine a **flexible rubber garden hose** lying on an inclined slope, with water flowing through it from a tap (source) to a drain hole (drain). Above the hose, you place a **wooden plank (the gate)** that presses down on the hose with adjustable pressure.

- When the wooden plank is **lightly touching** the hose → water trickles, but doesn't flow freely. This is the **sub-threshold** region.
- When you press **just enough** to deform the hose so that water starts flowing strongly → this is the **threshold condition** ($V_{GS} = V_{TH}$). A small channel has just opened.
- When you press **even harder** (excess pressure = pinch-off voltage $V_P$) → the hose opens wider, and water rushes through faster. This extra pressure is $V_{OV}$.
- Now, if you create a **partial vacuum (drain-side resistance)** at the end of the hose, the hose wall collapses at the exit. This **collapse point** is the **pinch-off point** in the channel — no more water can be drawn through that local cross-section, but the current is now **saturated** by the rate at which water is injected from the tap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** At the pinch-off point, although the local inversion charge density is mathematically zero, **current does NOT stop**. Instead, the carriers are "injected" across the pinch-off region into the depletion zone and are swept toward the drain by the lateral electric field. This is why **saturation current** is approximately constant with respect to $V_{DS}$.

## 1.3 Why Pinch-off Voltage is Central to Analog VLSI Design

In analog CMOS circuits, almost every performance metric — **transconductance ($g_m$)**, **intrinsic gain ($g_m r_o$)**, **bandwidth, noise, and matching** — depends directly on $V_{OV}$. The **square-law equation** for drain current in saturation is:

$$I_D \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P^{\,2}$$

Here, $V_P$ is the only design knob that an analog circuit designer freely manipulates (after choosing $W/L$ and $I_D$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** A very common question asks *"What is the significance of pinch-off voltage in determining the saturation region of a MOSFET?"* The answer must link $V_{DS,sat} = V_P$ explicitly to the transition from the triode (linear) region to the saturation region of operation.

## 1.4 Types and Boundary Definitions

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{TH}$ | Gate voltage at onset of strong inversion | **0.4 V to 0.7 V** (modern 180 nm) |
| Overdrive / Pinch-off Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_P$ | $V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | **0.1 V to 0.5 V** (analog design) |
| Saturation Voltage (Drain-side) | $V_{DS,sat}$ | $V_{DS}$ at which channel pinches off at drain | Equals $V_{OV}$ |

# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: The Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)

The pinch-off phenomenon is best understood through the **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)**, which is the foundation of all long-channel MOSFET analytical models. The GCA assumes that the lateral electric field ($\mathcal{E}_y$) along the channel is much smaller than the transverse electric field ($\mathcal{E}_x$) across the gate oxide. Under this assumption, the **inversion charge density per unit area** at any point $y$ along the channel (measured from the source) is given by:

$$Q_n(y) \;=\; -C_{ox} \Big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \Big]$$

where $V(y)$ is the **quasi-Fermi potential** (channel voltage) at position $y$ relative to the source, with boundary conditions:
- $V(0) = 0$ at the source end
- $V(L) = V_{DS}$ at the drain end

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Pinch-off Mechanism

1. **Step 1 — Channel Formation:** Apply $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$. An inversion layer forms at the Si–SiO₂ interface. The mobile charge density at the source end is $Q_n(0) = -C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = -C_{ox} V_P$.

2. **Step 2 — Lateral Field Drives Current:** A small $V_{DS}$ is applied. The channel acts like a voltage-controlled resistor (triode/linear region). Charge density tapers from source to drain.

3. **Step 3 — Drain-end Charge Reduction:** As $V_{DS}$ increases, the voltage at the drain end $V(L) = V_{DS}$ rises. The local gate overdrive at the drain end becomes $V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS}$.

4. **Step 4 — Critical Condition (Pinch-off):** When $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_P$, the charge density at the drain end vanishes:
$$Q_n(L) \;=\; -C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS}] \;=\; -C_{ox}[V_P - V_P] \;=\; 0$$

5. **Step 5 — Beyond Pinch-off (Saturation):** For $V_{DS} > V_P$, the point where $Q_n = 0$ moves slightly **inward** from the drain toward the source, forming a "pinch-off region." The effective channel length shortens to $L_{eff} < L$, but the voltage across the remaining conducting channel remains clamped at $V_P$. Hence $I_D$ becomes (approximately) **independent of $V_{DS}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Exams:** A common 7-mark question asks to *"Explain the physical origin of saturation in a MOSFET."* You must explicitly walk through steps 4 and 5 above, showing that $I_D$ saturates because the *voltage drop across the conducting channel* is fixed at $V_P$, not because the current "stops."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_P = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Definition of pinch-off / overdrive voltage | **V (volts)** |
| 2 | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_P$ | Drain voltage at which channel pinches off at drain end | **V** |
| 3 | $Q_n(y) = -C_{ox} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big]$ | Inversion charge per unit area at position $y$ along channel | **C/cm²** |
| 4 | $Q_n(L) = -C_{ox} [V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS}]$ | Charge density at drain end | **C/cm²** |
| 5 | $Q_n(L) = 0$ at pinch-off | Pinch-off condition at drain | — |
| 6 | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2}$ | Saturation drain current (long-channel, square-law) | **A (amps)** |
| 7 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Transconductance in saturation | **S (siemens)** |
| 8 | $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | **F/cm²** |
| 9 | $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | Threshold voltage (NMOS, strong inversion) | **V** |
| 10 | $\mathcal{E}_{y,sat} = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l_{sat}} \approx \frac{V_{DS} - V_P}{l_{sat}}$ | Lateral field in pinch-off region (velocity saturation precursor) | **V/cm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Caution for KTU Board Exams:** Always distinguish between $V_P$ (pinch-off / overdrive, a *gate* concept) and $V_{DS,sat}$ (a *drain* concept). Although numerically equal, they originate from different physical viewpoints. Examiners award marks for this distinction.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Pinch-off Voltage

- **Transconductance Tuning:** Analog designers select $V_{OV}$ to trade off **speed, gain, and headroom**. A larger $V_{OV}$ increases $g_m$ and bandwidth but reduces voltage headroom ($V_{DS} \le V_{DD} - V_{OV}$).
- **Bias Point Design:** Once $I_D$ and $V_{OV}$ are chosen, the required $W/L$ is computed: $\frac{W}{L} = \frac{2 I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} V_{OV}^{\,2}}$.
- **Region Selection in Multi-stage Amplifiers:** Common-source stages require $V_{DS} > V_{OV}$ to remain in saturation; cascode stages use $V_{OV}$ as a *headroom tax*.
- **Short-channel Considerations:** As $L$ shrinks, velocity saturation and DIBL modify the simple $V_{OV}^{\,2}$ dependence, but $V_{OV}$ remains the **primary design variable**.

# 📕 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Channel Charge Density and the Pinch-off Condition

We begin with the **Gradual Channel Approximation** in an n-channel MOSFET. The sheet inversion charge density (mobile carriers per unit gate area) at any point $y$ along the channel is governed by the local gate-to-channel voltage:

$$Q_n(y) \;=\; -C_{ox} \cdot \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big] \qquad (\text{Equation 1})$$

The negative sign indicates that the inversion charge consists of **electrons** (negative carriers). The magnitude of the charge is what matters for current conduction:

$$\vert Q_n(y) \vert \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big] \qquad (\text{Equation 2})$$

### Boundary Conditions

- At the **source end** ($y = 0$): $V(0) = 0$, so the source-end charge is:
$$\vert Q_n(0) \vert \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot V_P$$

- At the **drain end** ($y = L$): $V(L) = V_{DS}$, so the drain-end charge is:
$$\vert Q_n(L) \vert \;=\; C_{ox} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS})$$

### Setting the Pinch-off Condition

The **pinch-off condition** is defined as the state when the inversion charge at the drain end just vanishes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert Q_n(L) \vert \;=\; 0 \quad &\Longleftrightarrow \quad C_{ox} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS}) \;=\; 0 \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \quad V_{DS} \;=\; V_{GS} - V_{TH} \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \quad V_{DS} \;=\; V_P \qquad (\text{since } V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH})
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Step:** The factor $C_{ox} \neq 0$, so the only way for the product to be zero is for the bracketed voltage term to vanish. The **pinch-off drain voltage is identically equal to the gate overdrive voltage** $V_P$. This is a beautiful duality: the *gate* side and *drain* side definitions of pinch-off voltage converge to the same numerical value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drain Current in the Triode Region (Pre-Pinch-off)

The drain current is found by integrating the charge density times the carrier velocity (or equivalently, drift current density) along the channel. Using $I_D = -W \cdot v_d(y) \cdot Q_n(y)$ and $v_d = \mu_n \mathcal{E}_y = \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy}$:

$$I_D \;=\; -W \cdot \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy} \cdot Q_n(y)$$

Substituting $Q_n(y)$ from Equation 1:

$$I_D \;=\; W \mu_n C_{ox} \Big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \Big] \cdot \frac{dV}{dy}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $y = 0$ to $y = L$ (corresponding to $V = 0$ to $V = V_{DS}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D \cdot \int_0^L dy \;&=\; W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big] \, dV \\
I_D \cdot L \;&=\; W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right] \\
I_D \;&=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right] \\
I_D \;&=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_P \cdot V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **triode-region equation**, valid for $0 \le V_{DS} \le V_P$.

> **Logic Step:** The first term is linear in $V_{DS}$ (ohmic behavior), the second is quadratic (channel modulation). As $V_{DS} \to V_P$, the two terms tend to balance, and the rate of increase of $I_D$ with $V_{DS}$ slows.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Saturation Drain Current (Post-Pinch-off)

For $V_{DS} \ge V_P$, we substitute $V_{DS} = V_P$ into the triode equation (this is the key assumption of the long-channel model — the *channel voltage saturates* at $V_P$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{D,sat} \;&=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_P \cdot V_P - \frac{V_P^{\,2}}{2} \right] \\
&\phantom{=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\,} \left[ V_P^{\,2} - \frac{V_P^{\,2}}{2} \right] \\
I_{D,sat} \;&=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{V_P^{\,2}}{2} \\
I_{D,sat} \;&=\; \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \, V_P^{\,2} \\
I_{D,sat} \;&=\; \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Step:** The $\frac{1}{2}$ factor emerges naturally from the average of the charge density along the channel. The current depends *quadratically* on the pinch-off voltage $V_P$ — a cornerstone of analog CMOS design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Transconductance from Pinch-off Voltage

Differentiating $I_{D,sat}$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ (with $V_{TH}$ held approximately constant):

$$\begin{aligned}
g_m \;&=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,sat}}{\partial V_{GS}} \\
&\;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \right] \\
&\;=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \\
g_m \;&=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \, V_P
\end{aligned}$$

A second useful form, eliminating the $W/L$ term by using $I_D$:

$$\begin{aligned}
g_m \;&=\; \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} \\
&\;=\; \frac{2 I_D}{V_P}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Step:** This is a beautiful design equation: **knowing $I_D$ and $V_{OV}$, the transconductance is fully determined**, regardless of $W$, $L$, or process parameters. This is why $V_{OV}$ is the most important design variable in analog VLSI.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Critical Pinch-off Voltage Relationships

| Domain | Equation | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Gate side | $V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Excess gate voltage driving inversion |
| Drain side | $V_{DS,sat} = V_P$ | Boundary between triode and saturation |
| Current | $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_P^{\,2}$ | Quadratic dependence on $V_P$ |
| Transconductance | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_P$ | Linear dependence on $V_P$ |
| Gain factor | $g_m / I_D = 2 / V_P$ | Inverter efficiency metric |

The following **block-level functional flow** maps the structural and electrical transitions that occur as $V_{DS}$ is swept from 0 to beyond $V_P$.

    I --> J[Carriers injected across pinch-off into depletion region]
    J --> K[Swept to drain by lateral field: ID remains approximately constant]

The color coding (blue → green → yellow → red) intuitively conveys the *increasing electric field intensity* as we approach and exceed pinch-off.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between $V_{TH}$, $V_{OV}$, and $V_{DS,sat}$

## 4.3 I-V Characteristic Curve (Schematic Description)

The standard **$I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$** output characteristic consists of three regions, demarcated precisely by the pinch-off voltage $V_P$:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph REG1[Region 1: Triode]
        A1[Ohmic: ID rises linearly with VDS] --> A2[Quadratic correction as VDS approaches VP]
    end
    subgraph REG2[Region 2: Pinch-off Point]
        B1[VDS equals VP: Channel just pinched at drain] --> B2[Boundary between regions]
    end
    subgraph REG3[Region 3: Saturation]
        C1[ID approximately constant: equals 0.5 mu_n Cox W over L VP squared] --> C2[Slight rise due to channel length modulation: ID equals IDsat times 1 plus lambda VDS]
    end
    REG1 --> REG2
    REG2 --> REG3
```
