# 📙 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define the pinch-off voltage of a MOSFET. How is it related to the threshold voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **pinch-off voltage ($V_P$)** of a MOSFET is defined as the excess gate-to-source voltage beyond the threshold voltage required to establish a strong inversion layer in the channel. Mathematically:
$$V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$$
It is also called the **overdrive voltage ($V_{OV}$)**. Equivalently, in the drain-side context, $V_P$ is the drain-to-source voltage at which the inversion charge density at the drain end becomes zero, marking the onset of saturation. Thus $V_{DS,sat} = V_P$. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for saturation link)**

---

### **Q2. What is the significance of pinch-off voltage in determining the operating region of a MOSFET?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The pinch-off voltage $V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ determines the **boundary between the triode (linear) and saturation regions** of MOSFET operation. The region selection rule is:
- If $V_{DS} < V_P$ → **Triode/Linear region** (channel exists throughout, behaves as voltage-controlled resistor).
- If $V_{DS} \ge V_P$ → **Saturation region** (channel pinches off near drain, $I_D$ becomes nearly constant).

Therefore $V_P$ is the **threshold value of $V_{DS}$** that demarcates the two principal operating regions and is critical for analog circuit biasing. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ of an n-channel MOSFET operating in the saturation region, clearly showing the role of the pinch-off voltage $V_P$. Assume long-channel gradual channel approximation. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *An NMOS transistor in a 180 nm process has the following parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \; \mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{TH} = 0.5$ V. It is biased at $V_{GS} = 0.9$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.2$ V. Determine: (i) Pinch-off voltage $V_P$, (ii) Saturation drain current $I_{D,sat}$, (iii) Transconductance $g_m$, (iv) Verify the operating region. [7 marks]*

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

Using the **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)**, the inversion charge density at position $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q_n(y) = -C_{ox} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big]$$

The drain current is found from Ohm's law applied to the channel:

$$I_D = W \mu_n Q_n(y) \frac{dV}{dy}$$

Wait — the correct form using the magnitude of charge and drift velocity $v = \mu_n \mathcal{E}_y$:

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big] \frac{dV}{dy}$$

Separating variables and integrating from source ($V=0$, $y=0$) to drain ($V = V_{DS}$, $y = L$):

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_0^{V_{DS}} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V \big] \, dV$$

$$I_D \cdot L = W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_P \cdot V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right] \quad \text{(Triode equation)}$$

At the **pinch-off point**, $V_{DS} = V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Substituting:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_P \cdot V_P - \frac{V_P^{\,2}}{2} \right] = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P^{\,2}$$

This is the **square-law saturation equation**, showing that saturation drain current depends on the **square of the pinch-off voltage**. **[7 marks: 1 for GCA setup, 2 for integration, 2 for triode equation, 2 for saturation substitution]**

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

Given: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 250 \;\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{TH} = 0.5$ V, $V_{GS} = 0.9$ V, $V_{DS} = 1.2$ V.

**(i) Pinch-off voltage [1.5 marks]:**

$$V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.9 - 0.5 = 0.4 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Saturation drain current [2 marks]:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times (0.4)^2$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2500 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.16 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 200 \; \mu A$$

**(iii) Transconductance [2 marks]:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 0.4 = 1000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/V} = 1 \; mA/V$$

Alternative check: $g_m = 2 I_D / V_P = 2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} / 0.4 = 1000 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V ✓

**(iv) Region verification [1.5 marks]:**

Since $V_{DS} = 1.2$ V and $V_P = 0.4$ V, we have $V_{DS} > V_P$. Hence the transistor operates in the **saturation region**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain the physical mechanism of channel pinch-off in a MOSFET with the help of the gradual channel approximation. Sketch and explain the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ characteristic curve, clearly marking the triode and saturation regions separated by the pinch-off voltage. [7 marks]*

**(b)** *Using the pinch-off voltage concept, derive the expressions for: (i) transconductance $g_m$, and (ii) the ratio $g_m / I_D$ of an NMOS transistor operating in saturation. Why is $g_m / I_D$ considered the most important figure of merit in analog design? [7 marks]*

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Physical mechanism [7 marks]:**

When $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, an n-type inversion layer forms at the Si–SiO₂ interface between source and drain. The sheet charge density (magnitude) at any point $y$ along the channel, under the gradual channel approximation, is:

$$\vert Q_n(y) \vert = C_{ox} \big[ V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(y) \big]$$

At the source ($y = 0$, $V = 0$), the charge is maximum: $C_{ox} V_P$. At the drain ($y = L$, $V = V_{DS}$), the charge reduces to $C_{ox}(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V_{DS})$.

When $V_{DS}$ increases up to $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_P$, the drain-end charge becomes **zero**. This is the **pinch-off condition**. For $V_{DS} > V_P$, the point of zero charge moves inward, and carriers are injected across the pinch-off region into the depletion zone, where the lateral field sweeps them to the drain. Since the voltage across the conducting channel remains clamped at $V_P$, $I_D$ saturates.

**Sketch description** (to be drawn on answer script):
- Plot $I_D$ (y-axis) vs. $V_{DS}$ (x-axis).
- Initially, a steeply rising linear segment (triode region) for $V_{DS} < V_P$.
- A "knee" at $V_{DS} = V_P$, where the curve bends.
- Beyond the knee, an almost flat (slightly rising due to channel length modulation) saturation region for $V_{DS} > V_P$.
- Mark $V_{DS,sat} = V_P$ clearly on the x-axis. **[7 marks: 2 mechanism, 2 GCA, 1 sketch, 2 region identification]**

**Part (b) — Derivation of $g_m$ and $g_m/I_D$ [7 marks]:**

The saturation drain current is:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P^{\,2}$$

**(i) Transconductance [2.5 marks]:**

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_{D,sat}}{\partial V_{GS}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_P$$

Alternative form using $I_D$:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_P}$$

**(ii) $g_m / I_D$ ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\frac{g_m}{I_D} = \frac{2 I_D / V_P}{I_D} = \frac{2}{V_P}$$

**Why $g_m/I_D$ is the most important FOM [2.5 marks]:**

The ratio $g_m / I_D = 2 / V_P$ is **independent of process parameters, device dimensions (W, L), and even temperature** to first order. It represents the **transconductance generated per microampere of bias current** — a direct measure of the transistor's "efficiency" in converting bias current into gain. A designer who fixes $g_m/I_D$ knows the trade-off between speed (high $g_m$) and power (low $I_D$) without needing to know $\mu_n$, $C_{ox}$, or $W/L$. In modern low-power analog design, maximizing $g_m/I_D$ (i.e., minimizing $V_P$) is the central optimization target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusion of $V_{TH}$ and $V_{OV}$:** A very common mistake is to write the saturation current as $\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{TH}^{\,2}$. This loses **2 to 3 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always substitute $V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $\mu_n C_{ox}$ must be in **A/V²** (or $\mu$A/V²), and $V_{OV}$ in volts. Numerical answers in $\mu$A or mA without units lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Region misidentification:** Some students compute $I_{D,sat}$ without verifying that $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. If the device is actually in triode, the triode equation must be used. Always state "Since $V_{DS} > V_{OV}$, the device is in saturation, hence the square-law equation is valid."
> 4. **Channel length modulation:** In real analog design, $I_D$ has a slight $V_{DS}$ dependence ($\lambda$ effect). The basic pinch-off derivation ignores this, but mentioning it briefly in the answer shows depth.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ **Pinch-off Voltage = Overdrive Voltage = $V_{OV} = V_P = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$** — three names, one quantity.
- ✅ **Boundary between triode and saturation** is set by $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_P$.
- ✅ **At pinch-off, the drain-end inversion charge is zero**, but current still flows (carriers injected across the pinch-off region).
- ✅ **Square-law saturation current:** $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_P^{\,2}$.
- ✅ **Transconductance in saturation:** $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_P = 2 I_D / V_P$.
- ✅ **Figure of merit:** $g_m / I_D = 2 / V_P$ — independent of process, $W$, $L$.
- ✅ **$V_P$ is the *only* free design variable** in square-law analog design (after $W/L$ and $I_D$).
- ✅ For NMOS in 180 nm CMOS, typical $V_P$ is in the range **0.15 V to 0.4 V**; for low-power design it can be as low as **0.1 V**.
- ✅ **In saturation, the channel voltage at the drain end is *clamped* at $V_P$**, which is why $I_D$ becomes (approximately) independent of $V_{DS}$.
- ✅ **Long-channel assumption** ($L \ge 1\;\mu m$ in older processes, $\ge 0.25\;\mu m$ in 180 nm) is required for the square-law model to hold. In short-channel devices, velocity saturation modifies the $V_P^{\,2}$ law.
- ✅ **KTU exam vocabulary:** Always use the terms "pinch-off voltage," "overdrive voltage," "saturation voltage," and "effective voltage" — they are all synonymous in the KTU PCEVT601 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regions of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regions of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> The **Regions of Operation** of a MOSFET describe the distinct electrical states (modes) that the transistor can be biased into, governed by the relative magnitudes of the **Gate-to-Source Voltage (V_GS)**, the **Drain-to-Source Voltage (V_DS)**, and the **Threshold Voltage (V_tn)**. For a long-channel NMOS transistor, the four canonical regions are: (i) **Cutoff (Sub-threshold) Region**, (ii) **Triode (Linear / Ohmic) Region**, (iii) **Saturation (Active) Region**, and (iv) **Velocity-Saturation Region** (for short-channel devices).

The transition between these regions is dictated by the formation, pinch-off, and ultimate carrier transport characteristics of the **inversion layer** (the conductive channel) at the Si–SiO₂ interface beneath the gate oxide.

## 1.2 The Governing Voltages (NMOS Reference)

| Symbol | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{GS}$ | Gate–Source Voltage | Creates the channel by field effect |
| $V_{DS}$ | Drain–Source Voltage | Pushes carriers from Drain to Source |
| $V_{tn}$ | Threshold Voltage | Minimum $V_{GS}$ to form a conducting channel |
| $V_{OV}$ | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ — the "effective drive" |
| $V_{SB}$ | Source–Bulk Voltage | Modifies $V_{tn}$ via the **Body Effect** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Variable Water Valve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The MOSFET as a Smart Water Tap:**
> Imagine a **horizontal pipe (the channel)** carrying water (electrons) from a high-pressure end (**Drain**) to a low-pressure end (**Source**). A **rotating knob (the Gate)** above the pipe presses down on it to restrict flow.
> - When the knob is **not pressed enough** → no water flows → **Cutoff Region**.
> - When the knob is **lightly pressed** → water trickles, and the flow is proportional to the pressure difference across the pipe → **Triode Region** (the pipe acts like a **variable resistor** controlled by the knob).
> - When the knob is **pressed firmly** → the pipe becomes a narrow jet, and increasing the downstream pressure (more $V_{DS}$) doesn't change the flow much → **Saturation Region** (the pipe acts like a **constant current source**).
> - In a **very short pipe** at high pressure, water reaches terminal velocity → **Velocity Saturation**.

This analogy maps directly onto how analog designers *select* a region: **Triode = resistor**, **Saturation = current source / amplifier**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is the Heart of Analog VLSI

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU 2024 Scheme Stresses This Module:**
> Every analog block (amplifier, current mirror, op-amp gain stage, mixer) is designed by *deliberately placing* a MOSFET in a specific region. **Mis-biasing a transistor is the #1 cause of analog circuit failure.** Mastering the regions is the prerequisite for Module 3 (Single-Stage Amplifiers) and Module 4 (Differential Pairs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Conditions That Define the Regions

For an **NMOS transistor** (substitute p-type symbols for PMOS), the regions are governed by these inequalities:

### Region I — Cutoff (Sub-Threshold) Region
**Condition:** $V_{GS} < V_{tn}$

**Physics:** The gate voltage is insufficient to invert the p-type substrate. No conducting channel exists between source and drain.

**Current:** $I_D = 0$ (ideally)

**KTU Board Key Point:** Analog designers *avoid* cutoff for active gain stages — a transistor here has **zero transconductance ($g_m = 0$)** and is useless for amplification.

---

### Region II — Triode (Linear / Ohmic) Region
**Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **AND** $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$

**Physics:** A continuous inversion layer connects source to drain. The channel acts as a voltage-controlled resistor. The drain current rises *roughly linearly* with small $V_{DS}$, with a slight quadratic roll-off due to channel-pinching at the drain end.

**Current Equation:**

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

**Small-$V_{DS}$ Approximation (Deep Triode):** When $V_{DS} \ll 2V_{OV}$, the device behaves as a linear resistor:

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})} = \frac{1}{k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}}$$

where $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ is the **process transconductance parameter** (units: A/V²).

**Analog Design Usage:** Used as a **switch** (transmission gate) and as a **voltage-controlled resistor** in automatic gain control (AGC) circuits.

---

### Region III — Saturation (Active / Pinch-Off) Region ⭐
**Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **AND** $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$

**Physics:** The channel is "pinched off" near the drain end. Carriers are injected into the depletion region and swept to the drain by the electric field. The current becomes (ideally) **independent of $V_{DS}$** — the MOSFET behaves as a **voltage-controlled current source**.

**Current Equation (Square-Law):**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^{2} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2}$$

**Including Channel-Length Modulation (CLM):**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

where $\lambda$ is the **channel-length modulation parameter** (units: V⁻¹), typically $0.01$ to $0.1 \text{ V}^{-1}$.

**Transconductance ($g_m$) in Saturation — the Most Quoted Analog Metric:**

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

**Analog Design Usage:** ⭐ **This is the workhorse region for ALL analog amplifiers.** The current source behavior provides the high output impedance needed for voltage gain.

---

### Region IV — Velocity Saturation Region (Short-Channel Devices)
**Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **AND** $V_{DS} \geq V_{DSAT}$ where $V_{DSAT} < V_{OV}$

**Physics:** In deep submicron transistors (channel length < 0.25 µm), the lateral electric field is so high that carriers reach their **scattering-limited velocity (v_sat ≈ 10⁷ cm/s)** before the channel physically pinches off. The current saturates at a *lower* $V_{DS}$ than the long-channel formula predicts.

**Current Equation:**

$$I_D = C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_{tn}) v_{sat}$$

The square-law model **fails** here. Transconductance becomes:

$$g_m = C_{ox} W v_{sat}$$

which is **independent of $V_{GS}$ and $I_D$** — a serious problem for analog designers in nanometer CMOS.

---

## 2.2 Body Effect (Substrate Bias Effect)

When the source is not tied to the bulk (i.e., $V_{SB} > 0$), $V_{tn}$ increases:

$$V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$$

where:
- $V_{tn0}$ = threshold voltage with source tied to bulk
- $\gamma$ = **body-effect coefficient** (typically $0.3$ to $0.7 \text{ V}^{1/2}$)
- $\phi_F$ = **Fermi potential** (≈ $-0.3$ V for n-type substrate in p-well process; often quoted as $\vert \phi_F \vert \approx 0.3$ V)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting the body effect in **cascode stages and current mirrors** (where the source is not at ground) costs 1–2 marks. The body effect *reduces* the effective overdrive and *degrades* $g_m$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every entry is KTU 2024 board-exam relevant.**

| # | Parameter | Equation | Region | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overdrive Voltage | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ | All "ON" regions | V | Also called $V_{eff}$ or $V_{DSAT}$ (long-channel) |
| 2 | Process Transconductance | $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | All | A/V² | $\mu_n C_{ox} \approx 100\text{–}200\,\mu A/V^2$ for typical 0.18 µm CMOS |
| 3 | Conduction Parameter | $k_n = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L)$ | All | A/V² | Sometimes defined without the ½ |
| 4 | Cutoff Condition | $I_D = 0$ | Cutoff | A | When $V_{GS} \le V_{tn}$ |
| 5 | Triode $I_D$ | $I_D = k'_n (W/L)[V_{OV} V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$ | Triode | A | Reduce to linear $I_D \approx k'_n (W/L) V_{OV} V_{DS}$ for small $V_{DS}$ |
| 6 | Triode $R_{on}$ | $R_{on} = 1/[k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}]$ | Deep Triode | Ω | Used in switch design |
| 7 | Saturation $I_D$ (long channel) | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$ | Saturation | A | **Square-law** — heart of analog design |
| 8 | Saturation $I_D$ (with $\lambda$) | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation | A | Includes CLM |
| 9 | $g_m$ (Saturation) | $g_m = k'_n (W/L) V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D} = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$ | Saturation | A/V (or S) | The figure of merit of analog design |
| 10 | $g_{ds}$ (Output Conductance) | $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D$ | Saturation | S | Intrinsic gain: $A_v = g_m / g_{ds} = 2/(\lambda V_{OV})$ |
| 11 | Velocity-Saturated $I_D$ | $I_D = C_{ox} W v_{sat} V_{OV}$ | Vel. Sat. | A | For $L < 0.25\,\mu m$ |
| 12 | Velocity-Saturated $g_m$ | $g_m = C_{ox} W v_{sat}$ | Vel. Sat. | S | **Independent of bias** — design disaster |
| 13 | Body Effect | $V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma [\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}]$ | All | V | Crucial for cascodes |
| 14 | Boundary Triode→Saturation | $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$ | Boundary | V | The "knee" of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve |

> **⚠️ LaTeX Isolation Note:** All absolute-value bars in the table above are written using `\vert ... \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Analog Block | Preferred Region | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Common-Source Amplifier | Saturation | High $g_m$ for voltage gain |
| Common-Gate Amplifier | Saturation | Low input resistance, high bandwidth |
| Differential Pair (input pair) | Saturation | Linear $g_m$ for small-signal amplification |
| Current-Mirror Diode-Connected | Saturation (at $V_{DS} = V_{GS}$) | Acts as a precise current source |
| Transmission Gate (Switch) | Triode (or Cutoff) | Low $R_{on}$ for signal pass |
| AGC / Variable Attenuator | Triode | $R_{on}$ tuned by $V_{GS}$ |
| Cascode / High-Gain Output | Saturation | Boosts output impedance via $g_m r_o$ stacking |
| Sub-threshold (Ultra-Low-Power) | Sub-threshold ($V_{GS} < V_{tn}$) | Exponential $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ for bio-medical IoT |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Drain Current in the Triode Region (from First Principles)

### Setup
Consider an NMOS in the triode region. The inversion layer charge per unit area at a point $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{tn} - V(y) \right]$$

where $V(y)$ is the channel potential at position $y$ (with $V(0) = 0$ at source, $V(L) = V_{DS}$ at drain).

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law in the Channel
The drift current at point $y$ is:

$$I_D = -\mu_n Q_I(y) W \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

The negative sign converts the electron-current convention to the conventional (source-to-drain) current direction. Substituting $Q_I(y)$:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{tn} - V(y) \right] \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

### Step 2 — Separate Variables and Integrate
Multiply both sides by $dy$:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ V_{GS} - V_{tn} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

Integrate from $y = 0$ (source, $V = 0$) to $y = L$ (drain, $V = V_{DS}$):

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{tn} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Left Side
Since $I_D$ is constant along the channel (KCL):

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = I_D \cdot L$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Right Side

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{tn}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $I_D$

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{tn}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the Overdrive Voltage
Let $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$:

$$\boxed{\,I_D = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right] \quad \text{(Triode)}\,}$$

> This is the **complete** KTU board derivation. Marks are awarded for: showing $Q_I(y)$, separating variables, integrating limits, and the final boxed equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Drain Current at the Saturation Boundary

### Step 1 — Start with the Triode Equation

$$I_D = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Saturation Boundary
The boundary occurs when the channel just pinches off at the drain end, i.e., $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$.

### Step 3 — Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$

$$I_{D,sat} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} \cdot V_{OV} - \frac{V_{OV}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Algebraically

$$I_{D,sat} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV}^{\,2} - \frac{V_{OV}^{\,2}}{2} \right] = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{V_{OV}^{\,2}}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2} \quad \text{(Saturation)}\,}$$

> **KTU Note:** This *single* derivation transitions Triode → Saturation. Examiner often asks: "Show that at the edge of saturation, $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$." The above proves it elegantly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Transconductance $g_m$ in Saturation

### Step 1 — Start with the Saturation $I_D$ Equation

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2$$

### Step 2 — Take the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $V_{GS}$
(Assume $V_{DS}$ is held constant, and $V_{tn}$ is constant when source is tied to bulk.)

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{tn})$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn}) = k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of $I_D$ (Alternative Forms)
From $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^{\,2}$, we have:

$$k'_n \frac{W}{L} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}^{\,2}}$$

Substituting:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}^{\,2}} \cdot V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

Alternatively, eliminating $V_{OV}$:

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n (W/L)}}$$

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n (W/L)}} = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

### Step 5 — The Three Equivalent Forms (KTU must-know)

$$\boxed{\,g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design Insight:** $g_m$ scales linearly with $V_{OV}$, with $\sqrt{I_D}$, and with $W$ (for fixed $L$). Doubling the bias current **doubles** $g_m$ (good), but also **doubles** power dissipation (bad) — a fundamental analog design trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Boundary Voltage between Triode and Saturation

To find the exact $V_{DS}$ at which a device leaves triode:

### Step 1 — Differentiate $I_D$ w.r.t. $V_{DS}$ in Triode
The peak of the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve marks the triode-saturation transition.

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} - V_{DS} \right] = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Critical $V_{DS}$

$$V_{DS} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
For any $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$ → triode; for $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$ → saturation. This is the **pinch-off condition** — the drain end of the channel just vanishes.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Region Identification from Bias Conditions

| Given Bias | $V_{GS}$ vs. $V_{tn}$ | $V_{DS}$ vs. $V_{OV}$ | Region | $I_D$ Behavior | Use in Analog |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{GS} = 0$, $V_{DS} = 3\text{ V}$ | $V_{GS} < V_{tn}$ | — | **Cutoff** | $I_D \approx 0$ | OFF switch |
| $V_{GS} = 1.5\text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 0.1\text{ V}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ | $V_{DS} = 0.1 \ll 1.0$ | **Deep Triode** | $I_D \propto V_{DS}$ (resistor) | $R_{on}$ switch |
| $V_{GS} = 1.5\text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 1.2\text{ V}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ | $V_{DS} = 1.2 > 1.0$ | **Saturation** | $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$ (const) | Amplifier |
| $V_{GS} = 1.5\text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 0.9\text{ V}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ | $V_{DS} = 0.9 < 1.0$ | **Triode** | Quadratic in $V_{DS}$ | Variable resistor |
| $V_{GS} = 1.5\text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $V_{DS} = 0.999\text{ V}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ | $V_{DS} \approx V_{OV}$ | **Edge of Saturation** | Boundary point | Used in design margin check |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** An NMOS in 0.18 µm CMOS has $V_{tn} = 0.5$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 300\,\mu A/V^2$, $W = 10\,\mu m$, $L = 0.5\,\mu m$, $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V, $V_{DS} = 0.3$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{OV}$:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn} = 1.0 - 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Region:**

$$V_{DS} = 0.3 \text{ V} < V_{OV} = 0.5 \text{ V} \implies \textbf{Triode}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_D$ (Triode Equation):**

$$I_D = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

$$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20$$

$$I_D = (300 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 20 \cdot \left[ (0.5)(0.3) - \frac{(0.3)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$= (6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \left[ 0.15 - 0.045 \right]$$

$$= (6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.105)$$

$$\boxed{I_D = 0.63 \text{ mA}}$$

**Step 4 — Check What Happens at Saturation Boundary ($V_{DS} = 0.5$ V):**

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2}(6 \times 10^{-3})(0.5)^2 = 0.75 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $g_m$ in Saturation (if $V_{DS}$ were raised to $0.6$ V):**

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = (6 \times 10^{-3})(0.5) = 3 \text{ mA/V} = 3 \text{ mS}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — MOSFET Region Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Apply V_GS and V_DS] --> checkA{V_GS greater than V_tn ?}
    checkA -- No --> regCutoff[REGION 1: CUTOFF]
    checkA -- Yes --> checkB{V_DS less than V_OV ?}
    checkB -- Yes --> regTriode[REGION 2: TRIODE]
    checkB -- No --> checkC{Channel length less than 0.25 micron ?}
    checkC -- No --> regSat[REGION 3: SATURATION]
    checkC -- Yes --> checkD{V_DS greater than V_DSAT ?}
    checkD -- Yes --> regVelSat[REGION 4: VELOCITY SATURATION]
    checkD -- No --> regSat
    regCutoff --> outA[I_D approx 0 Ampere]
    regTriode --> outB[I_D is quadratic in V_DS]
    regSat --> outC[I_D is constant equal to I_DSAT]
    regVelSat --> outD[I_D is linear in V_GS not square law]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Walk through the diamond (decision) boxes. The terminal boxes describe the $I_D$ behavior characteristic of each region.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Region-Dependent Analog Functions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bias[Bias Network V_GS, V_DS] --> mos[MOSFET Device]
    mos --> detect{Region Detector}
    detect -- Cutoff --> f1[OFF Switch Logic Level Shifter]
    detect -- Triode --> f2[Variable Resistor AGC Attenuator Pass Transistor]
    detect -- Saturation --> f3[Current Source Voltage Amplifier Transconductance Stage]
    detect -- Velocity Sat --> f4[High Speed Driver RF Front End]
    f3 --> gm[Metric: g_m high For Voltage Gain]
    f2 --> ron[Metric: R_on low For Switch]
    f1 --> ioff[Metric: I_D zero For Low Leakage]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The same MOSFET, biased into different regions, performs four completely different analog functions. This is the foundation of CMOS analog versatility.

---

## 4.3 $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ Output Characteristic Curves — Schematic View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axis[Axes Setup: X axis is V_DS, Y axis is I_D] --> origin[Origin 0,0]
    origin --> ohmic[Ohmic Region: I_D rises linearly with V_DS at low V_DS]
    ohmic --> pinch[Pinch Off Point: I_D peaks at V_DS equal to V_OV]
    pinch --> sat[Saturation Region: I_D stays constant ideally for V_DS greater than V_OV]
    sat --> clm[Channel Length Modulation: I_D rises slightly with V_DS slope lambda]
    clm --> breakdown[Breakdown: I_D shoots up if V_DS exceeds BV_DSS]
    sat -.-> triode2[Triode curves at higher V_GS sit above the lower V_GS curve]
    triode2 --> family[Family of Curves for V_GS1 less than V_GS2 less than V_GS3]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This represents the classic MOSFET output characteristic. The boundary between Ohmic/Triode and Saturation is the "knee" of each curve, occurring at $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$. For higher $V_{GS}$, the curve sits higher (more current), and the knee moves right.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — The $g_m$ Design Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    goal[Design Goal: High g_m] --> choice1{Choose Knob}
    choice1 -- Higher V_OV --> v1[Pros: g_m increases linearly]
    choice1 --> v1b[Cons: V_GS rises, headroom drops, reliability worsens]
    choice1 -- Higher I_D --> v2[Pros: g_m scales with root I_D]
    choice1 --> v2b[Cons: power dissipation doubles]
    choice1 -- Larger W over L --> v3[Pros: g_m scales linearly with W]
    choice1 --> v3b[Cons: larger area, higher C_gg, lower bandwidth]
    v1 --> trade[Trade Off Triangle]
    v2 --> trade
    v3 --> trade
    trade --> opt[Optimization: choose small V_OV and moderate I_D]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Illustrates the three "knobs" an analog designer can twist to increase $g_m$, and the corresponding penalties. This is a recurring theme in Module 3 (Single-Stage Amplifiers).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List the four regions of operation of an NMOS transistor and state the condition (in terms of $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$, and $V_{tn}$) that defines each region.**

**Model Answer:**

The four regions of operation of an NMOS transistor are:

| # | Region | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutoff** | $V_{GS} < V_{tn}$ |
| 2 | **Triode (Linear)** | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **and** $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$ where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ |
| 3 | **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **and** $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$ |
| 4 | **Velocity Saturation** (short-channel only) | $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ and $V_{DS} \ge V_{DSAT}$ where $V_{DSAT} < V_{OV}$ |

In cutoff, $I_D = 0$ (channel not formed). In triode, the channel is continuous and $I_D$ depends on both $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$. In saturation, the channel is pinched off and $I_D$ is ideally independent of $V_{DS}$ (constant current source). In velocity saturation (sub-0.25 µm devices), the carriers reach $v_{sat}$ before pinch-off, and $I_D$ becomes linear in $V_{GS}$ rather than quadratic. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$ of a MOSFET. Why is it a more useful design parameter than $V_{GS}$ in analog circuits?**

**Model Answer:**

The **overdrive voltage** is defined as:

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$$

It represents the **excess** gate voltage beyond what is required to *just* turn the transistor on. It is more useful than $V_{GS}$ for analog design because:

1. **Process Independence:** $V_{tn}$ varies with temperature, process corner, and body effect, but $V_{OV}$ tracks these shifts automatically.
2. **Direct Control of Drain Current:** In saturation, $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$ — $V_{OV}$ alone (with $W/L$) sets $I_D$.
3. **Direct Control of Transconductance:** $g_m = k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}$ — a designer picks $V_{OV}$ first, then sizes $W/L$ for the desired $I_D$ or $g_m$.
4. **Headroom Indicator:** $V_{OV}$ must be small enough to leave sufficient $V_{DS}$ headroom for the device to *remain* in saturation across the signal swing.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** For an NMOS transistor in saturation, derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ in terms of $V_{GS}$, $V_{tn}$, $\mu_n$, $C_{ox}$, and $W/L$. Clearly state the saturation condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NMOS in 0.18 µm CMOS has the following parameters: $V_{tn} = 0.45$ V, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 280\,\mu A/V^2$, $W = 5\,\mu m$, $L = 0.5\,\mu m$. The device is biased with $V_{GS} = 0.95$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.5$ V.
   (i) Identify the region of operation.
   (ii) Calculate $I_D$ (neglecting $\lambda$).
   (iii) Calculate $g_m$.
   (iv) If $V_{DS}$ is reduced to $0.3$ V, what is the new $I_D$? Justify the region change. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

We begin with the **triode-region** $I_D$ equation, which is derived by integrating the channel-charge density:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

The **saturation boundary** is found by setting $\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{tn}) - V_{DS} \right] = 0$$

This gives the **critical drain voltage** at which the channel just pinches off at the drain end:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{tn} = V_{OV}$$

The **saturation condition** is therefore:

$$V_{DS} \ge V_{OV} \quad \text{(with } V_{GS} > V_{tn} \text{)}$$

Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$ into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV}^{\,2} - \frac{V_{OV}^{\,2}}{2} \right] = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2}$$

For $V_{DS} > V_{OV}$, the additional voltage drops across the depletion region near the drain (where the channel has vanished). The current is limited by the rate at which carriers are injected into the pinch-off point, which depends only on the voltage *at* the pinch-off point (i.e., $V_{OV}$), not on $V_{DS}$. Thus $I_D$ saturates at:

$$\boxed{I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2 \quad \text{(Saturation, neglecting } \lambda\text{)}}$$

[KTU Mark Distribution: 2 marks for triode equation setup, 2 marks for finding the boundary $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$, 2 marks for substitution and simplification, 1 mark for stating the physical interpretation.]

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Region Identification:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn} = 0.95 - 0.45 = 0.50 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 1.5 \text{ V} > V_{OV} = 0.50 \text{ V}$, the device is in **Saturation**. ✓

**(ii) Calculate $I_D$:**

$$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} (280 \times 10^{-6}) (10) (0.50)^2$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \times (2.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{I_D = 0.35 \text{ mA}}$$

**(iii) Calculate $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = (280 \times 10^{-6})(10)(0.50)$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 1.4 \text{ mA/V} = 1.4 \text{ mS}}$$

**Cross-check using $g_m = 2I_D / V_{OV}$:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \times 0.35 \text{ mA}}{0.50 \text{ V}} = 1.4 \text{ mS} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iv) New $I_D$ when $V_{DS} = 0.3$ V:**

Since $V_{DS} = 0.3 \text{ V} < V_{OV} = 0.50 \text{ V}$, the device is **now in Triode**.

Using the triode equation:

$$I_D = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = (280 \times 10^{-6})(10) \left[ (0.50)(0.3) - \frac{(0.3)^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_D = (2.8 \times 10^{-3}) \left[ 0.15 - 0.045 \right]$$

$$I_D = (2.8 \times 10^{-3}) (0.105)$$

$$\boxed{I_D = 0.294 \text{ mA}}$$

The current **decreased** from 0.35 mA to 0.294 mA because the device moved from saturation (where $I_D$ is at its maximum for the given $V_{OV}$) into triode, where $I_D$ is suppressed by the $-V_{DS}^2/2$ channel-pinching term.

[KTU Mark Distribution: 1 mark region ID, 2 marks $I_D$ saturation, 2 marks $g_m$ with two methods, 2 marks triode recalculation + physical justification.]

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ output characteristic diagram, describe the **triode, saturation, and cutoff** regions of an NMOS transistor. Show the locus of the triode-saturation boundary and write the equations for $I_D$ in each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **body effect** in a MOSFET. Derive the expression for $V_{tn}$ as a function of the source-bulk voltage $V_{SB}$. How does the body effect influence the choice of region of operation in a cascode amplifier? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Description with Diagram (7 marks):**

The $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ characteristic of an NMOS transistor consists of a family of curves, each corresponding to a fixed $V_{GS}$. The curves partition into three regions:

**1. Cutoff Region** (below the threshold curve $V_{GS} = V_{tn}$): $I_D = 0$. The transistor is OFF; the channel has not been induced.

**2. Triode Region** (low $V_{DS}$, to the left of the "knee"): For $V_{DS} < V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2} \right]$$

The curves rise approximately linearly from the origin and bend downward as the channel begins to pinch at the drain.

**3. Saturation Region** (high $V_{DS}$, to the right of the "knee"): For $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2} (1 + \lambda V_{DS}) \approx \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^{\,2}$$

The curves are nearly horizontal (constant current). Including channel-length modulation ($\lambda > 0$), they have a small positive slope $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D$.

**Locus of the Triode–Saturation Boundary:**
The boundary is the parabola $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ passing through the "knee" of each curve. It originates at $(0, V_{tn})$ on the $V_{DS}$ axis and rises with slope 1.

**Schematic Diagram (Mermaid — Functional Block Representation of the Curve):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    plot[ID versus VDS Curve Family] --> cutoff_line[Cutoff: ID equals 0 for VGS less than Vtn]
    plot --> triode_zone[Triode Zone: low VDS, ID rises with VDS]
    plot --> sat_zone[Saturation Zone: high VDS, ID is constant]
    triode_zone --> boundary[Knee Curve: VDS equals VOV marks boundary]
    boundary --> sat_zone
    sat_zone --> clm_slope[CLM effect: slight upward slope lambda ID]
    cutoff_line --> vgs_lines[Family of VGS curves stacked vertically]
    triode_zone --> vgs_lines
    sat_zone --> vgs_lines
```

[KTU Mark Distribution: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks region equations, 2 marks boundary locus, 1 mark physical interpretation.]

---

**Part (b) — Body Effect (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation:**
The threshold voltage $V_{tn}$ of a MOSFET depends on the voltage between the source and the bulk (substrate) terminal. When the bulk is tied to ground (the most common configuration for an NMOS in a p-substrate process) and the source is *also* at ground, there is no body effect. However, when the source is at a *higher* potential than the bulk (i.e., $V_{SB} > 0$, as happens in a **cascode** stage), the source-to-bulk junction becomes reverse-biased, the depletion region widens, more charge must be induced at the surface to form the channel, and $V_{tn}$ **increases**.

**Derivation of $V_{tn}(V_{SB})$:**

The general expression for the threshold voltage (with the source at potential $V_S$ and bulk at $V_B$) is:

$$V_{tn} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

where $V_{FB}$ is the flat-band voltage, $\phi_F$ is the Fermi potential, $N_A$ is the substrate doping, and $C_{ox}$ is the gate-oxide capacitance per unit area.

Defining:
- $V_{tn0} = V_{tn}\vert_{V_{SB}=0}$ = zero-bias threshold voltage
- $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ = **body-effect coefficient** (units V$^{1/2}$)

we obtain the compact form:

$$\boxed{\,V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)\,}$$

**Influence on Cascode Region of Operation:**

In a cascode amplifier, the upper transistor (M2) has its **source at a non-zero potential** (the drain of the lower transistor M1). This means $V_{SB2} > 0$ and the threshold of M2 is **elevated** above $V_{tn0}$:

$$V_{tn2}^{eff} = V_{tn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB2} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$$

**Consequences for region selection:**

1. **Reduced Effective Overdrive:** $V_{OV2}^{eff} = V_{GS2} - V_{tn2}^{eff}$ is smaller than the bias voltage suggests. The designer must allocate more headroom.
2. **Risk of Triode Operation:** If $V_{OV2}^{eff}$ is too small, the upper cascode device may **leave saturation** for large output swings, collapsing the high output impedance that motivated the cascode topology.
3. **Reduced $g_m$:** $g_{m2} = k'_n (W/L)_2 V_{OV2}^{eff}$ is reduced, partially offsetting the gain benefit of the cascode.

**Design Rule of Thumb:** To keep M2 in saturation across the entire signal swing, the bias $V_{GS2}$ must be at least $V_{OV2}^{eff} + V_{tn2}^{eff}$ (i.e., the cascode bias must account for the body-induced threshold shift).

[KTU Mark Distribution: 2 marks conceptual explanation, 2 marks derivation of $V_{tn}(V_{SB})$, 2 marks cascode consequence, 1 mark design rule.]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU 2024 Students Lose Marks on "Regions of Operation" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{OV}$ with $V_{DSAT}$:** They are *equal* in long-channel devices but $V_{DSAT} < V_{OV}$ in short-channel (velocity-saturated) devices. State this explicitly when relevant.
> 2. **Forgetting the body effect in $V_{tn}$:** When the source is not grounded (e.g., source follower, cascode, current mirror), the threshold *shifts*. Examiners deliberately set up circuits with $V_{SB} > 0$ to test this.
> 3. **Using the saturation $I_D$ equation when the device is in triode:** Always check the region condition *first* before plugging into any equation. The most common error: writing $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$ when $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the units of $\lambda$ and $\gamma$:** $\lambda$ is in V$^{-1}$ (so $\lambda V_{DS}$ is dimensionless); $\gamma$ is in V$^{1/2}$ (so $\gamma \cdot \sqrt{V}$ is in V). Many students write the body-effect equation with mismatched units and lose a mark.
> 5. **Not stating the region condition explicitly:** Examiners allocate at least 1 mark to *identifying the region* before any calculation. Do this *first* in every problem.
> 6. **Confusing $g_m$ with $g_{ds}$:** $g_m$ is the *transconductance* ($\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}$, large, desired) and $g_{ds}$ is the *output conductance* ($\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS}$, small, parasitic). The intrinsic gain is $g_m / g_{ds}$.
> 7. **Ignoring $\lambda$ in numerical problems when told to "include it":** If the question says "include channel-length modulation," you must multiply $I_D$ by $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet-Point Summary for KTU 2024 ESE**
> - ✅ **Four Regions:** Cutoff, Triode, Saturation, Velocity Saturation (short-channel only).
> - ✅ **Overdrive Voltage:** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ — the "effective knob" of analog design.
> - ✅ **Process Transconductance:** $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \approx 100\text{–}300\,\mu A/V^2$ for typical CMOS.
> - ✅ **Cutoff Condition:** $V_{GS} < V_{tn} \Rightarrow I_D = 0$.
> - ✅ **Triode Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **and** $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$.
> - ✅ **Triode $I_D$:** $k'_n (W/L)[V_{OV} V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$.
> - ✅ **Triode $R_{on}$:** $1/[k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}]$.
> - ✅ **Saturation Condition:** $V_{GS} > V_{tn}$ **and** $V_{DS} \ge V_{OV}$.
> - ✅ **Saturation $I_D$:** $\tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2$ (square law).
> - ✅ **Saturation with CLM:** $\tfrac{1}{2} k'_n (W/L) V_{OV}^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.
> - ✅ **$g_m$ in Saturation (3 forms):** $k'_n (W/L) V_{OV} = 2I_D/V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}$.
> - ✅ **$g_{ds}$ in Saturation:** $\lambda I_D$.
> - ✅ **Intrinsic Voltage Gain:** $A_v = g_m / g_{ds} = 2 / (\lambda V_{OV})$ — *larger* $V_{OV}$ *reduces* gain.
> - ✅ **Body Effect:** $V_{tn}$ increases with $V_{SB}$ via $\gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)$.
> - ✅ **Velocity Saturation (short-channel):** $I_D = C_{ox} W v_{sat} V_{OV}$ and $g_m = C_{ox} W v_{sat}$ (bias-independent — bad for analog).
> - ✅ **Sub-Threshold Region:** $V_{GS} < V_{tn}$ but $I_D \ne 0$ — exponential in $V_{GS}$, used in ultra-low-power biomedical IoT.
> - ✅ **Triode = Variable Resistor / Switch; Saturation = Current Source / Amplifier.** Memorize this mapping.
> - ✅ **Always identify the region FIRST** in any numerical problem (1 mark in ESE).
> - ✅ **Boundary of triode–saturation is the parabola** $V_{DS} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ on the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ plot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearizing Depletion Region Charge, Source-Referenced Simplified All-Region Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Linearizing Depletion Region Charge, Source-Referenced Simplified All-Region Models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Depletion Region Charge ($Q_B$)**: The total ionized-acceptor (or donor) charge residing in the semiconductor depletion layer beneath the gate oxide of a MOSFET. In an NMOS, $Q_B$ is negative and given by the standard 1-D Poisson solution of the surface-potential model. **Body-Effect Coefficient ($\gamma$)**: A technology-dependent parameter, measured in $\mathrm{V^{1/2}}$, that quantifies the sensitivity of the threshold voltage to the source-bulk bias $V_{SB}$. **Linearization of $Q_B$**: The Taylor-series expansion of the nonlinear square-root $Q_B(V_{SB})$ function about a quiescent operating point $V_{SB0}$, producing a constant (DC) component plus a small-signal linear capacitance (the bulk trans-capacitance). **Source-Referenced Model**: A MOSFET formulation in which all internal node voltages and charges are expressed with respect to the source terminal, allowing the substrate (body) node to be either absorbed or treated as a parasitic. **All-Region Model**: A single, continuous analytical expression that smoothly interpolates the terminal charges, currents, and capacitances across **cutoff**, **triode**, and **saturation**, eliminating the discontinuities in the derivatives that plague piece-wise (region-segregated) models. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board-Examiner Insight:** Modern BSIM3/BSIM4/BSIM-CMG models are *always* written as source-referenced, all-region, charge-conserving models. The traditional "Meyer" capacitance formulation is *not* charge-conservative and has been deprecated in industry simulators, though it still appears in KTU theory questions for pedagogical clarity. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of the depletion charge as the **water level in a tilted bathtub**: - The square-root dependence $Q_B \propto \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ resembles the nonlinear shape of water rising against a curved basin wall. - A **linearization** is like replacing a curved arch with a single straight plank tangent to it at the resting point — perfect for tiny ripples, but inaccurate if a large wave hits. - A **source-referenced model** is like labelling all furniture heights in a room from the floor (source) rather than from sea-level (substrate), so the architect does not have to redo every measurement whenever the building site changes elevation. - The **all-region model** is the all-weather umbrella: it works whether it is sunny, raining, or hailing, with no sudden collapse at the boundary between conditions. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in **bold**) | Symbol | Value | Meaning | |--------|-------|---------| | **$q$** | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}}$ | Electron charge | | **$\varepsilon_0$** | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathrm{F/m}}$ | Vacuum permittivity | | **$\varepsilon_{si}$** | $\mathbf{11.7 \,\varepsilon_0 \approx 1.0359 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{F/m}}$ | Silicon permittivity | | **$\varepsilon_{ox}$** | $\mathbf{3.9 \,\varepsilon_0 \approx 3.453 \times 10^{-11}\ \mathrm{F/m}}$ | SiO$_2$ permittivity | | **$kT/q$** | $\mathbf{25.85\ \mathrm{mV}}$ at 300 K | Thermal voltage | | **$n_i$** | $\mathbf{1.45 \times 10^{10}\ \mathrm{cm^{-3}}}$ | Intrinsic carrier density of Si | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, you are expected to *derive* the small-signal bulk capacitance (often labelled $C_{gb,\text{eff}}$ or $C_{bb}$) and to *state*, not necessarily re-derive, the Ward-Dutton 40/60 split. Charge-conservation arguments carry the highest marks in 14-mark derivations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Depletion-Charge Linearization is Needed In every analog transient simulation, the simulator must evaluate the **terminal charges** $Q_G, Q_B, Q_D, Q_S$ as functions of the four terminal voltages. Because $Q_B$ is intrinsically nonlinear in $V_{SB}$, evaluating it at every Newton-Raphson iteration is expensive. The **operating-point / small-signal decomposition** allows the simulator to: 1. Compute the **DC** $Q_{B0}$ once per iteration. 2. Replace the AC variation by a *constant* trans-capacitance $\partial Q_B / \partial V_{SB}$. This is the same mathematical pattern used for **diode junction capacitances** in SPICE. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Linearization - **Step 1.** Identify the nonlinear function: $Q_B(V_{SB})$ derived from depletion approximation. - **Step 2.** Choose an operating point $V_{SB0}$ (the DC bias on the source-body junction). - **Step 3.** Compute the slope $\partial Q_B / \partial V_{SB}$ at $V_{SB0}$; this slope is a true *capacitance* per unit area. - **Step 4.** Express small-signal: $q_b \approx C_{bb} \cdot v_{sb}$. - **Step 5.** Add the DC component $Q_{B0}$ to maintain absolute accuracy. - **Step 6.** Plug the result back into the master charge-neutrality equation $Q_G + Q_B + Q_I = 0$. ### 2.3 Source-Referencing Principle In a *source-referenced* formulation: - $V_S$ is treated as the datum (zero) for **all** voltages. - $V_{SB}$ appears as a *single* variable; the four-terminal matrix collapses to three independent voltages. - The body terminal is moved into a *parasitic network* (junction diodes $C_{sb}, C_{db}$), connected to the package pin. This drastically simplifies Newton-Raphson convergence in SPICE. ### 2.4 All-Region Continuity Trick Piece-wise models suffer $C_{ij}$ discontinuities at the triode–saturation edge. The cure is an **effective drain-source voltage**: $$V_{DS,\text{eff}} \;=\; V_{DS,sat}\!\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{V_{DS}}{V_{DS,sat}}\right)^{\!2}} \;-\; 1\,\right]$$ This expression reduces to $V_{DS}$ for small $V_{DS}$ and to $V_{DS,sat}$ for large $V_{DS}$, with **continuous second derivative** — a property KTU examiners love to test. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Expression | Unit | Validity | |---|----------|------------|------|----------| | 1 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | $\mathrm{V^{1/2}}$ | All regions | | 2 | Bulk depletion charge (per device) | $Q_B = -\,\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | Strong inversion | | 3 | Bulk trans-capacitance density | $\dfrac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}} = -\dfrac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}}$ | $\mathrm{F}$ | Small signal | | 4 | Linearized bulk charge | $Q_B \approx Q_{B0} + C_{bb}\,v_{sb}$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | Small-signal | | 5 | Inversion charge (triode) | $Q_I = -W\,L\,C_{ox}\!\left(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - \dfrac{V_{DS}}{2}\right)$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | Triode | | 6 | Inversion charge (saturation) | $Q_I = -\dfrac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | Saturation | | 7 | Ward-Dutton drain charge | $Q_D = -W\,C_{ox}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\dfrac{L-x}{L}\,dx$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | All-region | | 8 | Ward-Dutton source charge | $Q_S = -W\,C_{ox}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\dfrac{x}{L}\,dx$ | $\mathrm{C}$ | All-region | | 9 | 40 / 60 split (saturation) | $Q_D : Q_S = 2 : 3$ | — | Saturation only | | 10 | Effective $V_{DS}$ (smooth) | $V_{DS,\text{eff}} = V_{DS,sat}\!\left[\sqrt{1 + (V_{DS}/V_{DS,sat})^{2}} - 1\right]$ | $\mathrm{V}$ | All-region | | 11 | Charge neutrality | $Q_G + Q_B + Q_I = 0$ | — | Always | | 12 | Gate-source cap (sat) | $C_{gs} \approx \dfrac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}$ | $\mathrm{F}$ | Saturation | | 13 | Gate-drain cap (sat) | $C_{gd} \approx 0$ | $\mathrm{F}$ | Saturation | | 14 | Gate-bulk cap (cutoff) | $C_{gb} \approx W\,L\,C_{ox}$ | $\mathrm{F}$ | Cutoff | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall to avoid in tables:** Absolute-value bars $\vert x \vert$ must be written as $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` so that the markdown column separator is **not** confused with the math symbol. The same rule applies to set-bars and norm-bars. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Analog/RF IC design**: The 40/60 split directly sets the *gate-drain* feedthrough in switched-capacitor circuits and the *flicker-noise* corner of CMOS LNAs. - **Memory design**: DRAM cell retention time is governed by the sub-threshold $Q_B$ slope; linearization predicts leakage within $\pm 5\%$. - **Mixed-signal simulation**: Source-referenced BSIM models run $\sim 30\%$ faster than the older four-terminal, region-segregated SPICE Level-3 models. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linearization of $Q_B$ about $V_{SB0}$ Starting from the depletion approximation of an NMOS in strong inversion, the total bulk charge per device is $$ Q_B(V_{SB}) \;=\; -\,\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}\;\;W\,L\;\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} $$ Introduce the *body-effect coefficient* $$ \gamma \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A} \;=\; \gamma\,C_{ox} $$ so that $$ Q_B(V_{SB}) \;=\; -\,\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} $$ Apply a first-order Taylor expansion about the operating point $V_{SB} = V_{SB0}$: $$ Q_B(V_{SB}) \;\approx\; Q_B(V_{SB0}) \;+\; \left.\frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}}\right|_{V_{SB0}}\!\!\!\left(V_{SB} - V_{SB0}\right) $$ Compute the partial derivative term by term: $$ \frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}} \;=\; -\,\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\;\frac{1}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} $$ Evaluate at the operating point: $$ \left.\frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}}\right|_{V_{SB0}} \;=\; -\,\frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}} \;\equiv\; -C_{bb} $$ Hence the **linearized bulk charge** is $$ \boxed{\,Q_B \;\approx\; Q_{B0} \;-\; C_{bb}\,(V_{SB} - V_{SB0})\,} $$ where $$ Q_{B0} \;=\; -\,\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}} $$ and the **small-signal bulk trans-capacitance** is $$ C_{bb} \;=\; \frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}} $$ **Engineering interpretation.** $C_{bb}$ is a *constant* (with respect to small-signal $v_{sb}$), and it scales as $1/\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}$. It is the contribution of the bulk depletion region to the gate-bulk and source-bulk capacitances in a small-signal equivalent circuit. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Source-Referenced Charge-to-Capacitance Mapping Begin with the charge-neutrality identity applied to the inversion charge density $q_I(x)$ along the channel: $$ q_I(x) \;=\; -\,C_{ox}\;\bigl[\,V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)\,\bigr], \qquad V(0) = 0,\;\; V(L) = V_{DS} $$ Total inversion charge: $$ Q_I \;=\; -\,W\!\int_{0}^{L}\!q_I(x)\,dx \;=\; -\,W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,dx $$ Apply the **Ward-Dutton charge-partitioning** scheme — the source supplies charge at the source end, the drain at the drain end: $$ Q_S \;=\; -\,W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\frac{x}{L}\,dx $$ $$ Q_D \;=\; -\,W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\frac{L-x}{L}\,dx $$ Adding the two: $$ Q_S + Q_D \;=\; -\,W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,dx \;=\; Q_I $$ Hence **charge conservation is guaranteed by construction**, regardless of the operating region. The small-signal capacitances follow from the Jacobian $$ C_{ij} \;=\; -\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial V_j}\bigg|_{\text{other }V \text{ const}} $$ Carrying out the differentiation for the triode region (with the smooth $V_{DS,\text{eff}}$) yields the celebrated BSIM expressions: $$ C_{gg} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\!\left[\,1 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V_{DS,\text{eff}})^{2}}{(2\,V_{GS}-2V_{TH}-V_{DS,\text{eff}})^{2}}\,\right] $$ $$ C_{gs,\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\!\left[\,1 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})^{2}}{(2\,V_{GS}-2V_{TH}-V_{DS,\text{eff}})^{2}}\,\right] $$ and the cross-capacitances follow from $C_{gd} = C_{gg} - C_{gs,\text{eff}}$. All of these remain **continuous** in $V_{DS}$ across the saturation edge because $V_{DS,\text{eff}}$ is continuous. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: The 40/60 Saturation Rule In saturation the channel pinches off at $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Solving the Ward-Dutton integrals in the limit $V_{DS} \to V_{DS,sat}$: $$ Q_{S,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) $$ $$ Q_{D,\text{sat}} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(in the strict pinch-off model)} $$ However, for **charge conservation** the BSIM approach keeps a small $Q_{D,\text{sat}}$ such that the **2/3 inversion charge** is split as 60 % to source and 40 % to drain: $$ \frac{Q_{D,\text{sat}}}{Q_{S,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{0.4}{0.6} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} $$ Equivalently, $$ Q_{S,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{2}{5}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}), \qquad Q_{D,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{4}{15}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) $$ **Verification of normalization:** $$ Q_{S,\text{sat}} + Q_{D,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{2}{5} - \frac{4}{15} \;=\; -\frac{6+4}{15} \;=\; -\frac{10}{15} \;=\; -\frac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) $$ which equals the saturation $Q_I$, confirming the **40/60 rule**. ## 4.
