Reproducing this flow in an exam sketch scores full marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > *Define the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ of a MOSFET. State its dependence on technology parameters and explain, in one sentence, why the depletion-region charge $Q_B$ is a nonlinear function of $V_{SB}$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The body-effect coefficient is defined as $$ \gamma \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}} \quad [\mathrm{V^{1/2}}] $$ It depends on the channel doping $N_A$ (increases with $\sqrt{N_A}$) and the oxide capacitance $C_{ox}$ (decreases as $t_{ox}$ grows). $Q_B$ is nonlinear in $V_{SB}$ because the depletion width follows $W_d \propto \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$, arising from Poisson's equation under the depletion approximation. (3 marks: 1 mark for expression, 1 for technology dependence, 1 for non-linearity reason.) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *What is meant by a "source-referenced, all-region MOSFET model"? List two advantages it has over the classical Meyer capacitance model.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A source-referenced, all-region model expresses all terminal voltages, charges, and currents with the source as datum, using a single smooth analytical expression that is valid in cutoff, triode, and saturation without piecewise switching. (2 marks) **Advantages:** (i) charge conservation guaranteed by Ward-Dutton partitioning, (ii) continuous derivatives of $C_{ij}$ at the triode–saturation edge, (iii) faster SPICE convergence. (1 mark for any two correct points.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]** **Q.A (a)** Derive the linearized form of the depletion-region charge $Q_B$ about an operating point $V_{SB0}$, and obtain the small-signal bulk trans-capacitance $C_{bb}$. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Write $Q_B$ in standard form:* $$ Q_B(V_{SB}) \;=\; -\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}\;W\,L\;\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} $$ *Step 2 — Introduce $\gamma$ to simplify:* $$ \gamma \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A}}{C_{ox}} \;\Longrightarrow\; Q_B \;=\; -\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} $$ *Step 3 — Apply first-order Taylor expansion:* $$ Q_B(V_{SB}) \;\approx\; Q_B(V_{SB0}) \;+\; \left.\frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}}\right|_{V_{SB0}}\,(V_{SB}-V_{SB0}) $$ *Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative:* $$ \frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}} \;=\; -\frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} $$ *Step 5 — Evaluate at the operating point:* $$ \left.\frac{\partial Q_B}{\partial V_{SB}}\right|_{V_{SB0}} \;=\; -\frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}} \;\equiv\; -C_{bb} $$ *Step 6 — Final result:* $$ \boxed{\,Q_B \;\approx\; Q_{B0} \;-\; C_{bb}\,(V_{SB} - V_{SB0})\,\quad\text{with}\quad C_{bb} = \frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}}\,} $$ (Mark distribution: 2 for writing $Q_B$, 1 for $\gamma$, 2 for Taylor expansion setup, 1 for derivative, 1 for final boxed result.) --- **Q.A (b)** An NMOS transistor has $W = 10\,\mu\mathrm{m}$, $L = 1\,\mu\mathrm{m}$, $t_{ox} = 10\,\mathrm{nm}$, $N_A = 10^{16}\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$, and $V_{SB0} = 0$. Calculate $\gamma$, $Q_{B0}$, and $C_{bb}$. Take $\phi_F = 0.714\,\mathrm{V}$. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$:* $$ C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \;=\; \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} \;=\; 3.453 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{F/m^{2}} $$ *Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$ (convert $N_A = 10^{22}\,\mathrm{m^{-3}}$):* $$ \sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.0359 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^{22}} $$ $$ = \sqrt{3.315 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 5.757 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \gamma \;=\; \frac{5.757 \times 10^{-4}}{3.453 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 0.167\ \mathrm{V^{1/2}} $$ *Step 3 — Compute $Q_{B0}$ (with $V_{SB0}=0$):* $$ Q_{B0} \;=\; -\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L\,\sqrt{2\phi_F} $$ $$ = -0.167 \times 3.453 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{1.428} $$ $$ = -0.167 \times 3.453 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-11} \times 1.195 $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q_{B0} \;\approx\; -6.88\ \mathrm{fC}\,} $$ *Step 4 — Compute $C_{bb}$:* $$ C_{bb} \;=\; \frac{\gamma\,C_{ox}\,W\,L}{2\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}}} \;=\; \frac{0.167 \times 3.453 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-11}}{2 \times 1.195} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C_{bb} \;\approx\; 2.41\ \mathrm{fF}\,} $$ (Mark distribution: 2 for $C_{ox}$, 2 for $\gamma$, 2 for $Q_{B0}$, 1 for $C_{bb}$.) --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Q.B (a)** With the aid of relevant expressions, explain the **Ward-Dutton charge-partitioning** scheme. Show that it guarantees charge conservation and state the **40/60 rule** for saturation. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Define inversion charge density at position $x$:* $$ q_I(x) \;=\; -C_{ox}\,(V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x)) $$ *Step 2 — Write the Ward-Dutton split integrals:* $$ Q_S \;=\; -W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\frac{x}{L}\,dx $$ $$ Q_D \;=\; -W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\frac{L-x}{L}\,dx $$ *Step 3 — Verify charge conservation:* $$ Q_S + Q_D \;=\; -W\,C_{ox}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!(V_{GS}-V_{TH}-V(x))\,\left[\frac{x}{L} + \frac{L-x}{L}\right]dx \;=\; Q_I $$ *Step 4 — Saturation limit:* In saturation, $V_{DS} \to V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS}-V_{TH}$, and the integrals evaluate to $$ Q_{S,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{2}{5}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}), \quad Q_{D,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{4}{15}\,W\,L\,C_{ox}\,(V_{GS}-V_{TH}) $$ *Step 5 — 40/60 rule:* $$ \frac{Q_{D,\text{sat}}}{Q_{I,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{4/15}{2/3} \;=\; \frac{4}{15} \times \frac{3}{2} \;=\; \frac{2}{5} \;=\; 40\% $$ $$ \frac{Q_{S,\text{sat}}}{Q_{I,\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{2/5}{2/3} \;=\; 60\% $$ Hence in saturation, **40 % of the inversion charge is attributed to the drain and 60 % to the source**, ensuring continuity of the $C_{ij}$ matrix. (Mark distribution: 2 for defining $q_I$, 2 for the split integrals, 2 for charge-conservation proof, 1 for 40/60 numbers.) --- **Q.B (b)** An NMOS operating in saturation has $W = 20\,\mu\mathrm{m}$, $L = 0.5\,\mu\mathrm{m}$, $t_{ox} = 5\,\mathrm{nm}$, $V_{GS} = 1.5\,\mathrm{V}$, $V_{TH} = 0.5\,\mathrm{V}$. Using the source-referenced simplified model, calculate the gate-source capacitance $C_{gs}$ and verify that $C_{gd} \approx 0$ in saturation. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$:* $$ C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} \;=\; 6.906 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{F/m^{2}} \;=\; 6.91\ \mathrm{fF/\mu m^{2}} $$ *Step 2 — Apply the saturation source-referenced model:* $$ C_{gs,\text{sat}} \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\,W\,L\,C_{ox} $$ *Step 3 — Substitute values:* $$ C_{gs,\text{sat}} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \times (20 \times 0.5)\ \mu\mathrm{m^{2}} \times 6.91\ \mathrm{fF/\mu m^{2}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C_{gs,\text{sat}} \;\approx\; 46.1\ \mathrm{fF}\,} $$ *Step 4 — Verify $C_{gd} \approx 0$ in saturation:* In saturation the channel pinches off at $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat}$; further increases in $V_{DS}$ do not modify the channel charge, so by the Ward-Dutton Jacobian, $$ C_{gd,\text{sat}} \;=\; -\frac{\partial Q_G}{\partial V_{DS}}\bigg|_{\text{sat}} \;\approx\; 0 $$ (In the *strict* pinch-off model it is exactly zero; in BSIM a small non-zero value is retained for charge conservation, typically $< 5\%$ of $C_{gs}$.) (Mark distribution: 2 for $C_{ox}$, 2 for formula, 2 for numerical answer, 1 for $C_{gd}$ justification.) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $Q_B$** — $Q_B$ is *negative* (it is ionized acceptor charge), but $C_{bb}$ is taken as a *positive magnitude* in small-signal. Mixing signs loses 1 mark. > 2. **Unit inconsistency in $\gamma$** — $\gamma$ has units of $\mathrm{V^{1/2}}$, *not* V. Writing "$\gamma = 0.167\ \mathrm{V}$" is a strict $-1$ mark. > 3. **Mixing $C_{ox}$ in $\mathrm{F/m^{2}}$ and $\mathrm{fF/\mu m^{2}}$** — $1\ \mathrm{F/m^{2}} = 1\ \mathrm{fF/\mu m^{2}}$. Converting incorrectly (e.g., factor of $10^{6}$ or $10^{12}$ errors) is a frequent $-1$ to $-2$ mark slip. > 4. **Ignoring the Ward-Dutton factor** — In Part B, writing $C_{gs} = WLC_{ox}$ in saturation is wrong. The correct factor is $2/3$. Examiners deduct full 2 marks. > 5. **Stating "Meyer model is charge-conserving"** — this is the most common conceptual error and attracts a flat $-1$ mark. > 6. **Not showing the 40/60 derivation** — for 7-mark questions asking for the rule, simply writing "40/60" without showing the integral evaluation loses 3 marks. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Body-effect coefficient** $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}/C_{ox}$ scales as $\sqrt{N_A}/C_{ox}$ and dictates the strength of $V_{SB}$ modulation. - **Bulk charge** $Q_B = -\gamma C_{ox} W L \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ is the *only* nonlinear $V_{SB}$ term in the long-channel charge model. - **Linearization** at $V_{SB0}$ gives a *constant* trans-capacitance $C_{bb} = \gamma C_{ox} W L / (2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB0}})$, valid for $v_{sb} \ll 2\phi_F + V_{SB0}$. - **Charge neutrality** $Q_G + Q_B + Q_I = 0$ is the master constraint that closes the system of three charge equations. - **Source-referenced model** sets $V_S = 0$ as datum; $V_{SB}$ is a single independent variable; body terminal becomes a parasitic. - **All-region model** uses the smooth effective voltage $V_{DS,\text{eff}} = V_{DS,sat}[\sqrt{1+(V_{DS}/V_{DS,sat})^{2}} - 1]$ for derivative continuity. - **Ward-Dutton partitioning** defines $Q_S$ and $Q_D$ as weighted integrals with weights $x/L$ and $(L-x)/L$, *guaranteeing* $Q_S+Q_D = Q_I$. - **40/60 rule** in saturation: $Q_D : Q_S = 2 : 3$, with $|Q_{S,\text{sat}}| = \tfrac{2}{5} WLC_{ox}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$ and $|Q_{D,\text{sat}}| = \tfrac{4}{15} WLC_{ox}(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$. - **Meyer's model** is *not* charge-conservative; BSIM/BSIM-CMG replaced it because charge conservation is mandatory for accurate transient and periodic-steady-state (PSS) simulation. - **Gate-source cap in saturation** is $C_{gs} = (2/3) WLC_{ox}$; **gate-drain cap** $C_{gd} \approx 0$ (strict pinch-off). - **Gate-bulk cap in cutoff** is $C_{gb} = WLC_{ox}$ (the full oxide capacitance appears because there is no inversion layer to shield it). - **Operating-point decomposition** is the universal SPICE technique: a non-linear charge is split into DC (operating point) + small-signal capacitance (linear), evaluated by partial derivatives. - **KCL/KVL on the bulk node** requires the *small-signal* $C_{bb}$ to appear in the small-signal equivalent circuit between source (or drain) and body, *not* between gate and body. - **Continuity of $C_{ij}$ derivatives** is what makes Harmonic-Balance and PSS simulators converge — the all-region smooth $V_{DS,\text{eff}}$ is the *enabler* of this. - **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Always state "charge-conserving Ward-Dutton model" rather than "Meyer model" when the question asks about modern capacitance formulations.

topics.title: Strong Inversion: Complete Strong Inversion Model, Non-Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strong Inversion: Complete Strong Inversion Model, Non-Saturation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Strong Inversion?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Inversion** is the regime of MOSFET operation in which the surface potential under the gate oxide, $\phi_s$, is large enough that the inverted electron (or hole) concentration at the oxide–silicon interface is of the same order of magnitude as, or greater than, the bulk doping concentration $N_A$ (for n-channel devices). The classical strong-inversion boundary is reached when $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = (kT/q) \ln(N_A/n_i)$ is the bulk Fermi potential.

In this regime, a *highly conductive channel* is induced at the surface, and the drain current $I_D$ can be modeled using the classical **Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA)** developed by Shockley, Sah, and Pao.

The condition for strong inversion (NMOS) is:

$$V_{GS} \geq V_{TN} \quad \text{where} \quad V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

The strong-inversion layer can be modeled as a **continuous sheet of mobile charge** $Q_I$ (the inversion charge) drifting from source to drain under the influence of the longitudinal electric field $E_y$.

---

## 1.2 The "Complete" Strong Inversion Model

> [!NOTE]
> The term **Complete Model** distinguishes a high-accuracy expression from the *simple square-law* model taught in introductory digital courses. The complete model includes all major second-order physical effects that the simple model ignores.

The **Complete Strong Inversion Model** is the LEVEL-1 / Allen–Holberg expression for $I_D$ that simultaneously incorporates:

| Symbol | Effect | Physical Origin |
|:---:|---|---|
| $\gamma$ | **Body Effect** | Back-gate bias shifts $V_T$ via depletion charge modulation |
| $\lambda$ | **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** | Effective $L$ shortens at high $V_{DS}$ |
| $\theta$ | **Mobility Degradation** | Vertical field reduces surface mobility |
| $\mu_0$ | **Low-field mobility** | Process-defined constant |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Complete"?** The square-law model (ID = (μCox/2)(W/L)(VGS-VT)²) is mathematically clean but gives 30–50% error against measured silicon. The **Complete Model** is the analytical framework that bridges that gap without resorting to full numerical simulation (e.g., BSIM).

---

## 1.3 What is "Non-Saturation"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Saturation** is the operating region in which the channel extends *all the way* from source to drain — i.e., the channel has **NOT yet pinched off**. It is also called the **Triode** or **Linear** region. The defining inequality (NMOS) is:
> $$0 < V_{DS} < V_{DSAT} \quad \text{where} \quad V_{DSAT} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$$
> Beyond $V_{DSAT}$ the device enters the **Saturation** region (covered in the next section of the syllabus).

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – The Garden Hose**
>
> Imagine a garden hose lying on a tilted plank, with water flowing from the high end (source) to the low end (drain). The plumber pinches the hose partway down the plank (gate controls the opening).
>
> * **Below threshold** — the hose is clamped shut; barely a trickle leaks (subthreshold).
> * **Strong inversion, small $V_{DS}$ (non-saturation)** — the hose is open end-to-end. Doubling the pressure drop ($V_{DS}$) roughly doubles the flow — the device acts like a **linear resistor** whose value is set by the gate (a *voltage-controlled resistor*).
> * **Strong inversion, large $V_{DS}$ (saturation)** — the pinch point moves off the end of the hose; flow becomes *constant* regardless of further pressure increases.
>
> The **Complete Model** refines this picture by recognizing that (a) the rubber hose stretches as pressure increases (channel length modulation), (b) the hose gets stiffer when squeezed harder (mobility degradation), and (c) the surrounding soil pressure (back-gate / body effect) also affects the pinch force.

---

## 1.5 Operating-Point Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete NMOS strong-inversion $I_D$ has the *boundary* $V_{DSAT} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$. On either side of this boundary the **form** of the equation changes — the model is **piecewise continuous** (continuous in $I_D$ but not in $\partial I_D / \partial V_{DS}$).

**NMOS — Non-Saturation (Triode):**
$$0 < V_{DS} < V_{DSAT}$$

**NMOS — Saturation:**
$$V_{DS} \geq V_{DSAT}$$

This module focuses exclusively on the non-saturation branch of the piecewise model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Picture

In strong inversion, the total inversion charge per unit area at any point $y$ along the channel (measuring $y = 0$ at the source) follows from Gauss's law applied to the MOS capacitor at that point:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GB} - V_{FB} - \phi_s(y)] \approx -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{TN}]$$

where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential (with source as reference). The gate overdrive is conventionally defined as $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$, so:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{OV} - V(y)]$$

The drift current (using $I_D = -W \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot v_d(y)$ and the drift velocity $v_d = \mu_n E_y$):

$$I_D = W \mu_n C_{ox}\,[V_{OV} - V(y)]\,\frac{dV}{dy}$$

Because the channel is a series element, $I_D$ is **independent of $y$**. Integrating from $y=0$ (where $V=0$) to $y=L$ (where $V = V_{DS}$) yields the basic triode equation.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

### Step 1 — Bare Triode $I_D$ (no second-order effects)

The textbook **Paired Integration**:

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = \int_0^{V_{DS}} W \mu_n C_{ox} [V_{OV}-V]\, dV$$

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

> **Why this step?** The LHS treats $I_D$ as a constant (it doesn't depend on $y$); the RHS integrates the local Ohm's law along the channel. This is the heart of the **Gradual-Channel Approximation**.

### Step 2 — Define the Transconductance Coefficient

$$\boxed{\,K' = \mu_n C_{ox}\,} \quad \text{with units } A/V^2$$

$$\beta = K' \frac{W}{L} \quad (\text{device-level "gain factor"})$$

The triode equation in compact form:

$$I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Channel-Length Modulation ($\lambda$)

At higher $V_{DS}$ the depletion regions of source and drain intrude, shortening the effective channel length:

$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L \approx L \cdot \frac{1}{1+\lambda V_{DS}}$$

> **Why include $\lambda$?** Even in triode, $\lambda$ makes $I_D$ *slightly super-linear* with $V_{DS}$. This is critical for analog gain: $\partial I_D/\partial V_{DS}$ is the small-signal output conductance $g_{ds}$ that limits the gain of a common-source amplifier.

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})\,}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Mobility Degradation ($\theta$)

The vertical field $E_{ox} \approx V_{OV}/t_{ox}$ scatters carriers at the surface. A first-order correction models the effective mobility as:

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta\,V_{OV}}$$

The parameter $\theta$ (in $V^{-1}$) has typical value **0.1–0.5 V⁻¹** for 0.18 µm–0.5 µm processes.

### Step 5 — Include Body Effect ($\gamma$) Inside $V_{OV}$

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}, \quad V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

> **Why this matters in non-saturation?** The drain is at potential $V_{DS}$ relative to source, but the body is typically tied to ground. So the *local* back-gate bias $V_{SB}(y)$ varies along the channel — for short-channel devices, the **lumped** approximation $V_{SB} = V_S$ is used to keep the model tractable (this is the standard KTU assumption).

### Step 6 — Assemble the Complete Non-Saturation Model

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \frac{\mu_0 C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV}} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right] (1+\lambda V_{DS})\,}$$

Subject to the constraint $V_{DS} < V_{DSAT} = V_{OV}$ (NMOS) — otherwise the equation becomes the *saturation* form.

---

## 2.3 PMOS Equivalents

For a **PMOS** device in non-saturation the symmetry is achieved by replacing every NMOS quantity with its source-referenced dual:

| NMOS Quantity | PMOS Replacement |
|:---:|:---:|
| $V_{GS}$ | $V_{SG}$ |
| $V_{DS}$ | $V_{SD}$ |
| $V_{TN}$ | $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ |
| $V_{OV,N}$ | $V_{OV,P} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert$ |
| $V_{DSAT,N}$ | $V_{SDSAT,P}$ |
| $\mu_n$ | $\mu_p$ |

The non-saturation condition for PMOS:

$$0 < V_{SD} < V_{SDSAT} = V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert$$

And the current equation:

$$I_D = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV,P}} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \left[V_{OV,P}V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^2}{2}\right] (1+\lambda V_{SD})$$

The current flows from source (high potential) to drain (low potential) into the device terminal labeled D.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of equations you must memorize for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for this sub-topic. Every Part-B (14-mark) question on Complete Strong Inversion is solved using these expressions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Threshold with body effect | Always |
| 2 | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ | Effective gate overdrive | Always |
| 3 | $V_{DSAT} = V_{OV}$ | Pinch-off / saturation boundary | Region selection |
| 4 | $K' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | Process transconductance (A/V²) | Sizing |
| 5 | $\beta = K'\,(W/L)$ | Device transconductance parameter | Hand-calculations |
| 6 | $\mu_{eff} = \mu_0/(1+\theta V_{OV})$ | Field-dependent mobility | DC accuracy |
| 7 | $I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | **Complete non-saturation $I_D$** | Core equation |
| 8 | $r_{DS} = 1/\left[\beta\,(V_{GS}-V_{TN}-V_{DS})(1+\lambda V_{DS}) + I_D\lambda\right]$ | Small-signal channel resistance | Switch / resistor use |
| 9 | $I_D^{sat} = \dfrac{\beta\,V_{OV}^2}{2}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation form (for boundary check) | Continuity verification |
| 10 | $V_{DSAT} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 I_D}{\beta K'_{sat}/K'}}$ (velocity-sat. limit) | $V_{DSAT}$ w/ velocity saturation | Optional, advanced |

> **Note on notation:** $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$; $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$; $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}/C_{ox}$ (NMOS) — all are process parameters, not design variables.

---

## 2.5 Why Engineers Care: Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this region matters in Analog VLSI Design:**
> 1. **CMOS Switches & Transmission Gates** — operated deep in non-saturation; small $r_{ON}$ is critical for sample-and-hold settling.
> 2. **Triode-Load Active Loads** — small-signal gain of a triode-loaded CS stage is $-g_m \cdot r_{ds,triode}$ (typically much smaller than the saturation-load case).
> 3. **Voltage-Controlled Resistors (VCR)** — linearizing circuits exploit the quadratic $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ relation for tunable resistors in automatic gain control (AGC).
> 4. **Current Mirrors with Low $V_{DSAT}$** — wide-swing current mirrors operate the diode-connected device at the **edge of non-saturation** ($V_{DS} = V_{DSAT}$) to minimize headroom.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bare Non-Saturation $I_D$ from the Gradual-Channel Approximation

**Starting point — Gauss's law on the MOS capacitor at channel position $y$:**

The charge induced on the gate at position $y$ balances the sum of inversion charge, depletion charge, and any fixed-oxide charge. In strong inversion the depletion charge is clamped at its maximum (the **depletion approximation**), so any extra gate voltage beyond $V_{TN}$ appears as extra **inversion** charge:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GB}(y) - V_{FB} - 2\phi_F] - Q_B$$

For an n-channel device biased at $V_{GB} = V_{GS}$ and local channel potential $V(y) = V_{GB}(y) - V_{GB}$... (after algebra) the inversion sheet charge is:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V(y)] \equiv -C_{ox}[V_{OV} - V(y)]$$

> **Logic:** The bulk is at $V_S$ (= 0 in the source-referenced frame). The surface potential is locked at $2\phi_F$ in strong inversion. Any further gate-bump above $V_{TN}$ therefore produces an inversion charge exactly equal to $C_{ox}$ times the *excess* overdrive — a textbook linear capacitor law.

**Drift current at slice $y$:**

$$dI(y) = -\mu_n Q_I(y) \cdot E_y(y) \cdot W \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W [V_{OV}-V(y)]\,\frac{dV}{dy}\,dy$$

But the channel is a series resistance path — the *same* current flows at every cross-section:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{OV} - V(y)]\,\frac{dV}{dy}$$

> **Logic:** Because the gate is at DC and no current flows vertically, KCL demands that the lateral current entering slice $y$ equals the current leaving slice $y+dy$. The result is that $I_D$ is *constant* with $y$.

**Separation of variables:**

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{OV} - V]\,dV$$

**Integrate from source to drain:**

$$\int_0^L I_D \, dy = \int_0^{V_{DS}} \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{OV} - V]\,dV$$

The LHS integrates trivially because $I_D$ is constant in $y$:

$$I_D L = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\left[V_{OV} V - \frac{V^2}{2}\right]_0^{V_{DS}}$$

**Evaluate the RHS:**

$$I_D L = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Divide both sides by $L$:**

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\,}$$

**Define $\beta = \mu_n C_{ox} W/L$** to obtain the compact form:

$$I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

> **Sanity check (limits):**
> * If $V_{DS} \to 0$: $I_D \to \beta V_{OV} V_{DS}$ — pure **Ohm's law**, $R_{on} = 1/(\beta V_{OV})$.
> * If $V_{DS} \to V_{DSAT}$: $I_D \to \beta V_{OV}^2/2$ — matches the **saturation** equation continuously. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclusion of Channel-Length Modulation (CLM)

**Physical model:** The drain and source p–n junctions have depletion widths $x_{dD}$ and $x_{dS}$ that grow with reverse bias. The effective channel shrinks:

$$L_{eff} = L - x_{dD} - x_{dS} \approx L\left(1 - \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

For first-order (small $\Delta L/L$):

$$L_{eff} = \frac{L}{1 + \lambda V_{DS}}$$

**Substitute into the basic triode result:**

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L_{eff}}\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(1+\lambda V_{DS})\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})\,}$$

> **Why this matters in non-saturation:** Without CLM, the channel would be a *perfect* linear resistor; with CLM, the small-signal conductance at any bias point is:
> $$g_{ds}^{triode} = \left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}}\right|_{V_{GS}} = \beta(V_{OV}-V_{DS})(1+\lambda V_{DS}) + \lambda\beta\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$
> The second term is the CLM contribution and gives the device a finite Early effect even in triode.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inclusion of Mobility Degradation

**Vertical field at the surface:**

The oxide field perpendicular to the channel is approximately:

$$E_{ox} \approx \frac{V_{GS} - V_{TN}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{V_{OV}}{t_{ox}}$$

Empirically (from the Matthiessen-rule fit), the effective mobility drops as:

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta(V_{GS}-V_{TN})} = \frac{\mu_0}{1+\theta V_{OV}}$$

**Substitute $\mu_n \to \mu_{eff}$ in the triode result:**

$$I_D = \frac{\mu_0 C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV}} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

> **Insight:** Mobility degradation makes $I_D$ *sub-linear* in $V_{OV}$ at high overdrives. For $V_{OV} \ll 1/\theta$, the equation reduces to the textbook square law; for $V_{OV} \gg 1/\theta$, $I_D$ grows only as $\sqrt{V_{OV}}$ — velocity-limited behavior.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Small-Signal Channel Resistance $r_{DS}$ in Non-Saturation

**Start from complete non-saturation $I_D$:**

$$I_D = \frac{\beta}{(1+\theta V_{OV})}\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

Differentiate with respect to $V_{DS}$ at constant $V_{GS}$ (so $V_{OV}$ is also constant in this derivative):

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \frac{\beta}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\left\{(V_{OV}-V_{DS})(1+\lambda V_{DS}) + \lambda\left[V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\right\}$$

Expand the bracket:

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\beta}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\left\{V_{OV} - V_{DS} + \lambda V_{OV} V_{DS} - \lambda V_{DS}^2 + \lambda V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{\lambda V_{DS}^2}{2}\right\}$$

$$\boxed{\,g_{ds} = \frac{\beta}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\left[(V_{OV}-V_{DS}) + 2\lambda V_{OV} V_{DS} - \frac{3}{2}\lambda V_{DS}^2\right]\,}$$

Take the reciprocal to get the small-signal resistance:

$$\boxed{\,r_{DS} = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{(1+\theta V_{OV})}{\beta\left[(V_{OV}-V_{DS}) + 2\lambda V_{OV} V_{DS} - \tfrac{3}{2}\lambda V_{DS}^2\right]}\,}$$

**Limiting case** — for $\lambda = 0$ and $\theta = 0$ (ideal long-channel):

$$r_{DS}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{\beta(V_{OV}-V_{DS})} = \frac{1}{K'(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TN}-V_{DS})}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** For $V_{DS} \ll V_{OV}$, the device is a near-perfect voltage-controlled resistor with $R_{on} \approx 1/(\beta V_{OV})$. As $V_{DS} \to V_{DSAT}$, $R_{on} \to \infty$ — exactly the transition into saturation.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical:** Consider an n-channel MOSFET with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \;\mu A/V^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_{T0} = 0.7\;V$, $\gamma = 0.4\;V^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.7\;V$, $V_{SB} = 0$, $\theta = 0.1\;V^{-1}$, $\lambda = 0.05\;V^{-1}$. Compute $I_D$ and $r_{DS}$ at $V_{GS} = 1.5\;V$, $V_{DS} = 0.4\;V$.

**Step 1 — Body effect (with $V_{SB}=0$):**
$$V_{TN} = 0.7 + 0.4(\sqrt{0.7+0}-\sqrt{0.7}) = 0.7 + 0 = 0.7\;V$$

**Step 2 — Overdrive:**
$$V_{OV} = 1.5 - 0.7 = 0.8\;V$$

**Step 3 — Check region:**
$$V_{DSAT} = V_{OV} = 0.8\;V, \quad V_{DS} = 0.4 < 0.8 \Rightarrow \textbf{non-saturation} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\beta$:**
$$\beta = 50\;\mu A/V^2 \times 10 = 500\;\mu A/V^2$$

**Step 5 — Mobility degradation factor:**
$$1 + \theta V_{OV} = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.8 = 1.08$$

**Step 6 — Bare triode term:**
$$V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} = 0.8 \times 0.4 - \frac{0.4^2}{2} = 0.32 - 0.08 = 0.24\;V^2$$

**Step 7 — Channel-length modulation factor:**
$$1 + \lambda V_{DS} = 1 + 0.05 \times 0.4 = 1.02$$

**Step 8 — Assemble $I_D$:**
$$I_D = \frac{500\;\mu A/V^2}{1.08} \times 0.24 \times 1.02 = \frac{500 \times 0.2448}{1.08} = 113.3\;\mu A$$

**Step 9 — Small-signal $g_{ds}$:**
$$g_{ds} = \frac{500\;\mu}{1.08}\left[(0.8-0.4) + 2(0.05)(0.8)(0.4) - 1.5(0.05)(0.16)\right] \;\mu A/V$$
$$g_{ds} = 462.96\;\mu \left[0.4 + 0.032 - 0.012\right] = 462.96 \times 0.420 = 194.4\;\mu A/V$$

**Step 10 — Small-signal resistance:**
$$r_{DS} = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{194.4\;\mu A/V} = 5.14\;k\Omega$$

> **Verification (rough estimate using ideal long-channel formula):**
> $r_{DS}^{ideal} = 1/[\beta(V_{OV}-V_{DS})] = 1/[500\;\mu \times 0.4] = 5\;k\Omega$ — close agreement. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Region Selection Logic for Strong Inversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Device in Strong Inversion: VGS greater than VTN"] --> nodeB["Compute Overdrive: VOV = VGS minus VTN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute VDSAT = VOV"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Compare VDS and VDSAT"}
    nodeD -->|"VDS less than VDSAT"| nodeE["NON SATURATION TRIODE REGION"]
    nodeD -->|"VDS equals VDSAT"| nodeF["PINCH OFF BOUNDARY"]
    nodeD -->|"VDS greater than VDSAT"| nodeG["SATURATION REGION"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["ID = beta times VOV times VDS minus VDS squared over 2 times 1 plus lambda VDS"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Device behaves as voltage controlled resistor"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["Small signal rDS = 1 over gds"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["ID equals beta VOV squared over 2 times 1 plus lambda VDS"]
    nodeG --> nodeL["ID = beta VOV squared over 2 times 1 plus lambda VDS"]
    style nodeE fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38b00,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of the Complete Strong-Inversion Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUTS"]
        nodeVGS["VGS gate to source"]
        nodeVDS["VDS drain to source"]
        nodeVSB["VSB body to source bias"]
        nodeDIM["W and L device geometry"]
        nodePROC["Process parameters: mu0, Cox, VT0, gamma, theta, lambda"]
    end
    subgraph CALC["COMPUTATION"]
        nodeVT["VT = VT0 + gamma times bracket sqrt 2phiF plus VSB minus sqrt 2phiF"]
        nodeVOV["VOV = VGS minus VT"]
        nodeMU["mu_eff = mu0 over 1 plus theta VOV"]
        nodeBETA["beta = mu_eff times Cox times W over L"]
        nodeCORE["ID = beta times bracket VOV times VDS minus VDS squared over 2 bracket times 1 plus lambda VDS"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS"]
        nodeIDout["ID drain current"]
        nodeGDS["gds small signal conductance"]
        nodeRDS["rDS small signal resistance"]
        nodeREGION["Operating region label"]
    end
    nodeVGS --> nodeVT
    nodeVSB --> nodeVT
    nodePROC --> nodeVT
    nodeVT --> nodeVOV
    nodeVGS --> nodeVOV
    nodePROC --> nodeMU
    nodeVOV --> nodeMU
    nodeMU --> nodeBETA
    nodeDIM --> nodeBETA
    nodePROC --> nodeBETA
    nodeBETA --> nodeCORE
    nodeVDS --> nodeCORE
    nodeVOV --> nodeCORE
    nodePROC --> nodeCORE
    nodeCORE --> nodeIDout
    nodeCORE --> nodeGDS
    nodeGDS --> nodeRDS
    nodeVDS --> nodeREGION
    nodeVOV --> nodeREGION
    style nodeCORE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    style nodeREGION fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ Curve in Strong Inversion

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Cutoff: VGS less than VTN
    Cutoff --> Triode: VGS equals VTN
    Triode --> Triode: VDS increases from 0 to VDSAT
    Triode --> Saturation: VDS equals VDSAT
    Saturation --> Saturation: VDS increases above VDSAT
    note right of Triode
        ID = beta bracket VOV VDS minus VDS squared over 2 bracket
        times 1 plus lambda VDS
        Approximately linear at small VDS
    end note
    note right of Saturation
        ID equals beta VOV squared over 2 times 1 plus lambda VDS
        Idsat reached at boundary
    end note
    style Triode fill:#cce5ff
    style Saturation fill:#ffd6d6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Equivalent Small-Signal Circuit in Non-Saturation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeG["Gate port"] -->|"vg input small signal"| nodeGm["Current source gm times vgs"]
    nodeG --> nodeCGS["Cgs"]
    nodeGm --> nodeD["Drain node"]
    nodeD --> nodeRDS["rds small signal channel resistance"]
    nodeRDS --> nodeS["Source node"]
    nodeS --> nodeGSS["GND small signal"]
    nodeD --> nodeCDB["Cdb to bulk"]
    nodeCDB --> nodeB["Bulk node"]
    nodeB --> nodeBSS["GND small signal"]
    nodeS --> nodeCSB["Csb to bulk"]
    nodeCSB --> nodeB
    style nodeRDS fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    style nodeGm fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
```

> **Reading the schematic:** The non-saturation MOSFET is modeled by a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with the small-signal channel resistance $r_{DS}$. The key feature is that $r_{DS}$ is **small** in triode — orders of magnitude smaller than in saturation. This is why triode-load amplifiers have much lower gain than saturation-load amplifiers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part-A (3 marks) tests **recall + one-step application**. Answers should fit in **3–5 lines** of a main-answer booklet (≈ 60–80 words). No derivations, but every symbol used must be defined.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the non-saturation (triode) region of operation of a MOSFET. State the condition for a device to be biased in this region.**

**Model Answer:**

> The non-saturation region — also called the **triode** or **linear** region — is the regime of MOSFET operation in strong inversion in which the drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ is **insufficient** to pinch off the channel at the drain end. The defining condition is:
> $$0 < V_{DS} < V_{DSAT}, \quad \text{where} \quad V_{DSAT} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$$
> In this region, an unbroken inversion layer exists from source to drain, and the device behaves approximately as a **voltage-controlled resistor** whose small-signal resistance $r_{DS}$ is set by the gate overdrive. The complete drain-current expression is:
> $$I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS}), \quad \text{with} \quad \beta = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the four second-order effects included in the "complete" strong-inversion model and give one physical reason why each is significant.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Body Effect ($\gamma$):** Source–body bias widens the depletion region, raising $V_{TN}$ and reducing $I_D$.
> 2. **Channel-Length Modulation ($\lambda$):** Reverse-biased drain–body depletion region shortens the effective channel, making $I_D$ depend weakly on $V_{DS}$.
> 3. **Mobility Degradation ($\theta$):** Strong vertical gate field scatters surface carriers, reducing the effective mobility at high $V_{OV}$.
> 4. **Velocity Saturation (optional, $V_{DSAT,c}$):** Carriers reach $v_{sat}$ at high lateral fields, clamping the drain current.
> Together these effects reduce the **30–50%** error of the ideal square law to a few percent relative to measured silicon.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part-B carries **14 marks** per question with a *choice* between two questions (Module Internal Choice). The pattern below reproduces that structure: sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks, with **complete worked solutions**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the complete expression for the drain current $I_D$ of an n-channel MOSFET operating in the non-saturation (triode) region, starting from the gradual-channel approximation. Your derivation should clearly show how the body effect, channel-length modulation, and mobility degradation modify the basic triode equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $0.5\;\mu m$ NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\;\mu A/V^2$, $W = 10\;\mu m$, $L = 1\;\mu m$, $V_{T0} = 0.7\;V$, $\gamma = 0.4\;V^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.6\;V$, $\theta = 0.1\;V^{-1}$, $\lambda = 0.04\;V^{-1}$. The source–body is tied to ground. The device is biased at $V_{GS} = 1.5\;V$ and $V_{DS} = 0.3\;V$.
  (i) Verify that the device operates in the non-saturation region.  
  (ii) Compute $I_D$ using the complete model.  
  (iii) Compute the small-signal channel resistance $r_{DS}$ at this bias. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Inversion charge (1 mark)**

In strong inversion, the inversion sheet charge at a position $y$ along the channel, where the local channel potential is $V(y)$, is given by:

$$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V(y)\right] = -C_{ox}\left[V_{OV} - V(y)\right]$$

Here $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ is the effective overdrive, and $V_{TN}$ includes the body effect:

$$V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

**Step 2: Drift-current expression (1 mark)**

$$I_D = \mu_{eff}\,C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{OV}-V(y)]\,\frac{dV}{dy}$$

**Step 3: Mobility degradation (1 mark)**

The vertical field at the surface reduces the carrier mobility to:

$$\mu_{eff} = \frac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta\,V_{OV}}$$

**Step 4: Integration (2 marks)**

Separating variables and integrating from source ($V=0$) to drain ($V=V_{DS}$):

$$\int_0^L I_D\,dy = \mu_{eff} C_{ox} W \int_0^{V_{DS}}[V_{OV} - V]\,dV$$

$$I_D L = \mu_{eff} C_{ox} W \left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5: Channel-length modulation (1 mark)**

Substituting the effective length $L_{eff} = L/(1+\lambda V_{DS})$:

$$I_D = \mu_{eff} C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

**Step 6: Final compact form (1 mark)**

Defining $\beta = \mu_0 C_{ox} (W/L)/(1+\theta V_{OV})$:

$$\boxed{\,I_D = \frac{\mu_0 C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\cdot\frac{W}{L}\cdot\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right](1+\lambda V_{DS})\,}$$

Subject to $V_{DS} < V_{DSAT} = V_{OV}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**(i) Region verification (2 marks):**

Since $V_{SB} = 0$:

$$V_{TN} = 0.7 + 0.4(\sqrt{0.6+0} - \sqrt{0.6}) = 0.7 + 0 = 0.7\;V$$

$$V_{OV} = 1.5 - 0.7 = 0.8\;V$$

$$V_{DSAT} = V_{OV} = 0.8\;V$$

$$V_{DS} = 0.3\;V < 0.8\;V \Rightarrow \textbf{Non-Saturation} \;\checkmark$$

**(ii) Compute $I_D$ (3 marks):**

$$\beta_0 = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} = 50\;\mu A/V^2 \times \frac{10}{1} = 500\;\mu A/V^2$$

Mobility factor:
$$1 + \theta V_{OV} = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.8 = 1.08$$

Triode core:
$$V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} = (0.8)(0.3) - \frac{0.09}{2} = 0.24 - 0.045 = 0.195\;V^2$$

CLM factor:
$$1 + \lambda V_{DS} = 1 + 0.04 \times 0.3 = 1.012$$

Final:
$$I_D = \frac{500}{1.08} \times 0.195 \times 1.012 = 463.0 \times 0.1973 = 91.4\;\mu A$$

**(iii) Compute $r_{DS}$ (2 marks):**

$$g_{ds} = \frac{500\;\mu}{1.08}\left[(0.8-0.3) + 2(0.04)(0.8)(0.3) - 1.5(0.04)(0.09)\right] \;\mu A/V$$

$$g_{ds} = 462.96\;\mu \times [0.5 + 0.0192 - 0.0054] = 462.96\;\mu \times 0.5138 = 237.9\;\mu A/V$$

$$r_{DS} = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{237.9\;\mu A/V} \approx 4.2\;k\Omega$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain with the aid of a small-signal equivalent circuit, why a MOSFET biased in the non-saturation region is *not* a good amplifying device. Show that the small-signal channel resistance in non-saturation is:

$$r_{DS} = \frac{1}{\beta(V_{GS}-V_{TN}-V_{DS})}$$

for an ideal long-channel device ($\lambda = 0$, $\theta = 0$). State the approximation used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An n-channel MOSFET is to be used as a switch (transmission gate element) with a maximum allowable ON-resistance of $1\;k\Omega$. The device has $K' = 100\;\mu A/V^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.7\;V$, and the available power supply is $V_{DD} = 3.3\;V$. Ignoring body effect, mobility degradation, and CLM, find the minimum $W/L$ ratio required. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Amplifier Critique (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition of small-signal $r_{DS}$ in non-saturation (2 marks):**

For an ideal long-channel MOSFET in non-saturation with $V_{DS} \ll 2 V_{OV}$, the triode $I_D$ is approximately linear:

$$I_D \approx \beta (V_{GS} - V_{TN}) V_{DS} = \beta V_{OV} V_{DS}$$

This is Ohm's law with:

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{\beta V_{OV}} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TN})}$$

**Step 2: Finite $V_{DS}$ correction (1 mark):**

For non-zero $V_{DS}$, the exact (still long-channel) form is:

$$I_D = \beta\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{DS}$:

$$g_{ds} = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} = \beta(V_{OV} - V_{DS}) = \beta(V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V_{DS})$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,r_{DS} = \frac{1}{\beta(V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V_{DS})}\,}$$

**Step 3: Why it's a poor amplifier (2 marks):**

The voltage gain of a common-source stage with a triode load of resistance $R_L$ is:

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot (r_{DS} \parallel R_L)$$

In non-saturation, $r_{DS}$ is **very small** (typically $100\;\Omega$–$1\;k\Omega$), orders of magnitude smaller than the saturation-region $r_{DS}$ (which can be tens of $k\Omega$). With a finite $R_L$, the parallel combination is dominated by the small $r_{DS}$, giving $|A_v| \ll 1$. The device cannot provide the high output-impedance needed for gain.

**Step 4: Small-signal equivalent (1 mark):**

The non-saturation small-signal model is a **voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with a low-value $r_{DS}$**. Because $r_{DS} \ll 1/g_m$, the input-referred loading dominates and the stage does not amplify.

**Step 5: Approximation used (1 mark):**

We have used the **long-channel, no-body-effect, no-mobility-degradation, no-CLM** simplification (i.e., $\lambda = 0$, $\theta = 0$, $\gamma$ ignored, $V_{SB} = 0$). The result is a *closed-form* expression but under-predicts the true $r_{DS}$ when second-order effects are non-negligible.

#### Part (b) — Switch Sizing (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the design constraint (1 mark):**

Maximum ON-resistance:
$$R_{on,max} = 1\;k\Omega$$

**Step 2: Apply the design formula (2 marks):**

The ideal triode resistance at small $V_{DS}$:

$$R_{on} = \frac{1}{K'(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TN})}$$

Setting $R_{on} \leq R_{on,max}$:

$$K'\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN}) \geq \frac{1}{R_{on,max}}$$

**Step 3: Determine $V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ (1 mark):**

A transmission-gate switch sees the gate at $V_{DD} = 3.3\;V$ and the source at ground (worst case):

$$V_{OV,max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN} = 3.3 - 0.7 = 2.6\;V$$

**Step 4: Solve for $W/L$ (2 marks):**

$$\frac{W}{L} \geq \frac{1}{K'\,(V_{DD}-V_{TN})\,R_{on,max}} = \frac{1}{100\;\mu A/V^2 \times 2.6\;V \times 1\;k\Omega}$$

$$\frac{W}{L} \geq \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.6 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{0.26} \approx 3.85$$

**Step 5: Engineering safety margin (1 mark):**

In practice, designers use a safety factor of 2× to absorb body effect, mobility degradation, and process variation:

$$\boxed{\,\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{design} \geq 8\,}$$

(For $L = 0.5\;\mu m$, this gives $W \geq 4\;\mu m$ — a typical transmission-gate device.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Complete Strong Inversion Model, Non-Saturation" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\lambda$ factor in triode.** Many students write $I_D = \beta[V_{OV}V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$ and lose **2–3 marks** in 14-mark derivations. CLM is *still present* in triode; the channel is just not pinched off. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Writing the saturation equation in triode territory.** If $V_{DS} > V_{DSAT}$ and the student blindly applies the triode form, the answer is **physically wrong**. Always check $V_{DS} < V_{DSAT}$ first. (Lose 3–4 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring body effect when source is not at bulk.** If the problem says "source tied to ground but body at $-1\;V$," you MUST use $V_{SB} = +1\;V$ in the $V_{TN}$ formula. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing up NMOS and PMOS sign conventions.** $V_{GS}$ vs $V_{SG}$, $V_{TN}$ vs $|V_{TP}|$, $V_{DS}$ vs $V_{SD}$ — examiners specifically look for this. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to state the non-saturation *inequality*.** Even if the equation is correct, omitting the constraint $V_{DS} < V_{DSAT}$ is a -1 mark deduction in KTU valuation key.
> 6. **Wrong order of operation in $\mu_{eff}$:** The mobility factor $(1+\theta V_{OV})$ goes in the **denominator** of $\beta$, not multiplying $I_D$ directly. Many students make this algebraic slip and end up with a *larger* $I_D$ than the bare-square-law predicts, which is unphysical. (Lose 1–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Complete Strong Inversion Model, Non-Saturation**

- [x] **Strong Inversion Definition** — surface potential $\phi_s \geq 2\phi_F$; equivalently $V_{GS} \geq V_{TN}$.
- [x] **Complete Model = Ideal Triode × Mobility × CLM × Body effect**:
  $$I_D = \underbrace{\frac{\mu_0 C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV}}}_{\text{mobility}}\cdot\underbrace{\frac{W}{L}}_{\text{geometry}}\cdot\underbrace{\left[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]}_{\text{triode core}}\cdot\underbrace{(1+\lambda V_{DS})}_{\text{CLM}}$$
- [x] **Non-Saturation Condition:** $0 < V_{DS} < V_{DSAT}$ with $V_{DSAT} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$.
- [x] **Body Effect Formula:** $V_{TN} = V_{T0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ — *never* omit when $V_{SB} \neq 0$.
- [x] **Threshold $V_{TN}$** uses $V_{SB}$ (source-to-body), **not** $V_{DB}$ (drain-to-body). Examiners test this distinction.
- [x] **Small-signal $r_{DS}$ (ideal long-channel, $\lambda = \theta = 0$):** $r_{DS} = 1/[\beta(V_{GS} - V_{TN} - V_{DS})] = 1/[\beta(V_{OV} - V_{DS})]$.
- [x] **Complete-model $g_{ds}$ formula** (used for analog gain):
  $$g_{ds} = \frac{\beta}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\left[(V_{OV}-V_{DS}) + 2\lambda V_{OV}V_{DS} - \tfrac{3}{2}\lambda V_{DS}^2\right]$$
- [x] **PMOS Mirror Convention:** swap $V_{GS} \to V_{SG}$, $V_{DS} \to V_{SD}$, $V_{TN} \to |V_{TP}|$, $\mu_n \to \mu_p$.
- [x] **Continuity Check:** at the boundary $V_{DS} = V_{DSAT}$, $I_D^{triode} = \beta V_{OV}^2 / 2 \cdot (1+\lambda V_{DSAT}) = I_D^{sat}$. The model is **continuous** (not differentiable) at this point.
- [x] **Engineering Use-Cases for Non-Saturation:**
  1. CMOS analog switches / transmission gates.
  2. Triode-loaded amplifiers (low-gain stages).
  3. Voltage-controlled resistors (AGC, automatic gain).
  4. Wide-swing cascode current mirrors (diode-connected at $V_{DS} = V_{DSAT}$).
- [x] **Symbol Cheat-Sheet:**
  - $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$
  - $\beta = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)$
  - $K' = \mu_n C_{ox}$
  - $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TN}$
  - $V_{DSAT} = V_{OV}$
  - $r_{DS} = 1/g_{ds}$
- [x] **Always state the region inequality** in any numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for the absence of the boundary condition.
- [x] **Final equation to memorize for ESE** (the "one-liner" that captures the entire sub-topic):
  $$\boxed{\,I_D^{triode,complete} = \frac{\mu_0 C_{ox}}{1+\theta V_{OV}}\,\frac{W}{L}\,\bigl[V_{OV}V_{DS} - \tfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\bigr](1+\lambda V_{DS}),\quad V_{DS} < V_{OV}\,}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate-Drain Connected Loads: CS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gate-Drain Connected Loads: CS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Common Source (CS) amplifier with a Gate-Drain Connected Load** is a single-stage CMOS amplifier topology in which the load device M2 has its **gate terminal tied to its drain terminal** (i.e., $V_{G2} = V_{D2}$). This connection forces M2 to behave as a **diode-connected MOSFET**, always biased in the **saturation region** (because $V_{DS2} = V_{GS2} \ge V_{DS,sat}$). The driving transistor M1 is the common-source amplifying device, and M2 acts as a non-linear, small-signal resistive active load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate-Drain Connected Load = Diode-Connected MOSFET Load.** This is the simplest form of an *active load* used in CMOS analog integrated circuits, replacing large, area-consuming resistive or poly-load elements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a water tap (M1, the driver) controlling the flow of water into a flexible balloon (M2, the load). Normally, the balloon would need a separate elastic string to push back. But here, the balloon is *pinched at its own outlet* and tied to its own neck — so it auto-regulates its own stiffness depending on how much water is being pushed in.

- The **tap** (M1) controls current by its gate voltage.
- The **self-pinched balloon** (M2) presents a resistance to the output node automatically.
- The output voltage develops at the joint between the tap and the balloon (the common node = output).

This self-adjusting behaviour eliminates the need for a separate bias voltage for the load, dramatically simplifying the circuit.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Threshold Voltage** ($V_{TH}$): typically **0.4 V to 0.7 V** for modern CMOS processes.
- **Process Transconductance Parameter**: $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (typical: **100–250 µA/V²**).
- **Channel Length Modulation Parameter** ($\lambda$): typically **0.01 V⁻¹ to 0.1 V⁻¹**.
- **Thermal Voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx$ **26 mV** at room temperature (**300 K**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A diode-connected MOSFET is the *only* two-terminal MOSFET configuration that **always operates in saturation** under normal bias, which is why it is a preferred active load in low-voltage, low-power design modules.

## 1.4 Symbol and Structure Preview

$$
\text{Gate-Drain Connected MOSFET} \;\Rightarrow\; V_{GS} = V_{DS} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Device is always in saturation}
$$

The load M2 therefore behaves as a **voltage-controlled current source in saturation**, whose small-signal resistance is set entirely by $g_{m2}$ and $r_{o2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Bias Condition of M2 (Load)**
Since the gate is tied to the drain, $V_{DS2} = V_{GS2}$. For the device to be ON, we need $V_{GS2} > V_{TH2}$. The condition $V_{DS2} = V_{GS2} > V_{GS2} - V_{TH2} = V_{DS,sat}$ is automatically satisfied whenever the device is conducting. Hence M2 is **unconditionally in saturation**.

**Step 2 — DC Bias Current**
The DC drain current of M1 and M2 are equal (series stack):
$$
I_{D1} = I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2}\, k_n' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\!2} (V_{GS2} - V_{TH2})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})
$$

> The same current must flow through both transistors, so M1 must supply exactly the current demanded by M2 at its $V_{GS2}$. This is a *self-biasing* feedback mechanism.

**Step 3 — Output Node DC Voltage**
The DC output voltage equals $V_{GS2}$ (since gate-drain tied), so:
$$
V_{OUT,DC} = V_{GS2}
$$

**Step 4 — Small-Signal Resistance of the Diode-Connected M2**
Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the drain/gate node. The small-signal current is:
$$
i_x = g_{m2}\, v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} = g_{m2}\, v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}
$$
Therefore the small-signal resistance seen looking into the drain of a diode-connected MOSFET is:
$$
r_{d} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A diode-connected MOSFET behaves as a small-signal resistance approximately equal to $\mathbf{1/g_m}$ — *not* $r_o$. This is a critical distinction from an ideal current-source load (which presents $\infty$ small-signal resistance).

**Step 5 — Voltage Gain of CS Stage**
For the CS amplifier with M1 as driver and M2 as diode-connected load:
$$
A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out}
$$
where the output resistance is:
$$
R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{d2} = r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; \left(\frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2}\right)
$$

For a typical first-order design where $1/g_{m2} \ll r_{o1}, r_{o2}$:
$$
A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_1 \cdot k_n'}{(W/L)_2 \cdot k_p'}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** The gain depends only on the *device size ratio* — not on the bias current or absolute dimensions! This is extremely useful for low-power design where the bias current cannot be freely increased.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Drain Current (M1) | $I_{D1} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_n' (W/L)_1 (V_{GS1}-V_{TH1})^2$ | A | Saturation, $\lambda$ neglected for DC |
| 2 | DC Drain Current (M2) | $I_{D2} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p' (W/L)_2 (V_{GS2}-V_{TH2})^2$ | A | Always in saturation ($V_{DS2}=V_{GS2}$) |
| 3 | Current Continuity | $I_{D1} = I_{D2}$ | A | Series stack at output node |
| 4 | DC Output Voltage | $V_{OUT,DC} = V_{GS2}$ | V | Because gate and drain of M2 are tied |
| 5 | Small-Signal Resistance of Diode-Connected MOSFET | $r_d = 1/g_m \;\vert\vert\; r_o \approx 1/g_m$ | $\Omega$ | Tied gate-drain config |
| 6 | Transconductance of M1 | $g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_{D1}}$ | A/V | Saturation formula |
| 7 | Output Resistance of CS Stage | $R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; (1/g_{m2}) \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}$ | $\Omega$ | Diode-load topology |
| 8 | Small-Signal Voltage Gain | $A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ | V/V (dimensionless) | When $1/g_{m2} \ll r_{o1}, r_{o2}$ |
| 9 | Gain in Terms of Device Ratios | $A_v \approx -\sqrt{ \frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2} }$ | V/V | For matched $g_{m1} = g_{m2}$ ideally use larger $(W/L)_1$ |
| 10 | Input Resistance | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $\Omega$ | MOSFET gate is insulated |
| 11 | Output Resistance (approximate) | $R_{out} \approx 1/g_{m2}$ | $\Omega$ | Ignoring $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ (first-order) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-stage op-amp first stages** in op-amp IP libraries (e.g., Miller-compensated op-amps).
- **Source followers and level shifters** where a small, well-defined load is required.
- **Bias current mirrors** that drive subsequent stages.
- **Low-power analog front-ends** in IoT/medical ICs where device-ratio-based gain is desirable.
- **Compact gain stages** in analog multipliers, OTAs, and comparators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** The gate-drain connected load is *compact and self-biasing*, but its **gain is fundamentally limited** (typically 5–20 V/V) because $1/g_m$ is small. For higher gain, designers escalate to **current-source loads** (which present $\infty$ AC resistance, gain $\propto g_m r_o$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Small-Signal Resistance of Diode-Connected MOSFET

**Given:** A MOSFET with gate tied to drain. Apply a small-signal test source $v_x$ at the common node and measure the resulting current $i_x$ entering the drain.

**Step (a):** Identify the small-signal terminal voltages. Since gate and drain are joined at the test source:
$$
v_{gs2} = v_x, \quad v_{ds2} = v_x
$$

**Step (b):** Write the small-signal drain current expression (saturation small-signal model):
$$
i_{d2} = g_{m2}\, v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}}
$$

**Step (c):** Substitute the terminal voltages:
$$
i_x = g_{m2}\, v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} = v_x \left( g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)
$$

**Step (d):** The equivalent small-signal resistance is the ratio of applied voltage to drawn current:
$$
r_d = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}}
$$

**Step (e):** Factor the denominator to recognise the parallel form:
$$
r_d = \frac{1}{g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2}
$$

**Step (f):** Apply the typical design approximation $g_{m2} \gg 1/r_{o2}$ (i.e., $g_{m2} r_{o2} \gg 1$):
$$
\boxed{\, r_d \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $1/g_{m2}$ is a *large-signal, small-geometry* resistance on the order of hundreds of ohms to a few kilo-ohms in typical sub-micron CMOS. It is **process and bias dependent** — not a fixed value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of CS Stage with Gate-Drain Connected Load

**Given:** M1 (NMOS, common source) driving M2 (PMOS, gate-drain connected) load. Input applied to M1's gate, output taken at the common drain node.

**Step (a):** Identify the small-signal output node equation using KCL (no current flows into an ideal voltmeter/load):
$$
g_{m1}\, v_{gs1} + \frac{v_{ds1}}{r_{o1}} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} + g_{m2}\, v_{gs2} = 0
$$

**Step (b):** For the diode-connected load M2, the gate is tied to the drain, so $v_{gs2} = v_{ds2} = v_{out}$. Substitute:
$$
g_{m1}\, v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\, v_{out} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}} = 0
$$

**Step (c):** Collect $v_{out}$ terms on one side:
$$
g_{m1}\, v_{in} = -v_{out} \left( \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)
$$

**Step (d):** Solve for the gain $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$:
$$
A_v = -\frac{g_{m1}}{\dfrac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}}
$$

**Step (e):** Express the denominator as the parallel combination $R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; (1/g_{m2}) \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}$:
$$
\boxed{\, A_v = -g_{m1}\left[\,r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2}\,\right] \,}
$$

**Step (f):** If $g_{m2} r_{o2} \gg 1$ and $g_{m2} r_{o1} \gg 1$ (a condition met in most analog designs), then $1/g_{m2}$ dominates the parallel combination, giving:
$$
A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}
$$

**Step (g):** Express the gain in terms of device dimensions, assuming both transistors carry the same bias current $I_D$ and using $g_m = \sqrt{2 k' (W/L) I_D}$:
$$
A_v \approx -\frac{\sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_D}}{\sqrt{2 k_p' (W/L)_2 I_D}} = -\sqrt{ \frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2} }
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The intrinsic gain of a CS stage with a gate-drain connected load is **set entirely by device-size ratios**, not by absolute device sizes or by the bias current. This is the classic signature of a *ratioed* analog design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Input and Output Resistances

**Step (a):** Input resistance — Gate of M1 is insulated by the gate oxide. No DC current flows into the gate. Therefore:
$$
R_{in} = \infty
$$

**Step (b):** Output resistance — Set the input source to zero ($v_{in} = 0$). Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output node and measure $i_x$:
$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\, v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} = v_x \left( \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right)
$$

Therefore:
$$
R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** Unlike an ideal current-source load where $R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}$ (very large), the gate-drain connected load gives $R_{out} \approx 1/g_{m2}$ (small to moderate). This is the central trade-off the KTU examiner loves to test.

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Practice Calculation)

**Given:** A CS amplifier with M1 (NMOS) and M2 (PMOS, gate-drain connected). Process parameters: $k_n' = 200\,\mu A/V^2$, $k_p' = 50\,\mu A/V^2$, $V_{TH,n} = 0.5\,V$, $V_{TH,p} = -0.5\,V$, $\lambda_n = \lambda_p = 0.05\,V^{-1}$. Device sizes: $(W/L)_1 = 20$, $(W/L)_2 = 10$. Bias current $I_D = 100\,\mu A$.

**Step 1:** Compute $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$:
$$
g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 200\mu \cdot 20 \cdot 100\mu} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.895\, mA/V
$$

$$
g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 k_p' (W/L)_2 I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 50\mu \cdot 10 \cdot 100\mu} = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.316\, mA/V
$$

**Step 2:** Approximate gain (ignoring $r_o$):
$$
A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\frac{0.895}{0.316} \approx -2.83\, V/V
$$

**Step 3:** Verify using the device-ratio formula:
$$
A_v \approx -\sqrt{\frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{200\mu \cdot 20}{50\mu \cdot 10}} = -\sqrt{8} \approx -2.83\, V/V
$$

Both methods agree, confirming the result. The numerical gain is modest (≈ -2.83 V/V) — typical of gate-drain connected loads.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`

**Q:** What is a gate-drain connected MOSFET? Why is it always biased in saturation?

**Model Answer:**
A gate-drain connected (diode-connected) MOSFET is a configuration in which the gate terminal is electrically shorted to the drain terminal. Since the device terminals then satisfy $V_{DS} = V_{GS}$, the condition for saturation, $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, is automatically satisfied whenever the device conducts ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$). Hence the device is **unconditionally biased in the saturation region** as long as the gate-source voltage exceeds the threshold. This makes it an excellent active load for analog CS amplifiers.

---

### Question 2 (Definition/Property — 3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`

**Q:** Derive the small-signal resistance of a diode-connected MOSFET and state the typical design approximation.

**Model Answer:**
Apply a small-signal test voltage $v_x$ at the common gate-drain node. The total current entering the node is:
$$
i_x = g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} = g_m v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_o} = v_x \left(g_m + \frac{1}{r_o}\right)
$$
The small-signal resistance is:
$$
r_d = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_m + 1/r_o} = \frac{1}{g_m} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_o
$$
Since typically $g_m r_o \gg 1$, the approximation $r_d \approx 1/g_m$ is used in first-order hand analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, students frequently **omit the body effect** when M2 is a PMOS in a separate n-well, and **forget to include $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$** in the output resistance expression. Always write the **full parallel combination** $R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; (1/g_{m2}) \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}$ and then simplify — partial marks are awarded for the complete expression.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** For a CS amplifier with a gate-drain connected PMOS load (M1: NMOS driver, M2: PMOS load), derive the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ in terms of $g_{m1}, g_{m2}, r_{o1}, r_{o2}$. Clearly state any approximations used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **gate-drain connected load** with the **ideal current-source load** for a CS amplifier. Discuss gain, output swing, bandwidth, and process sensitivity in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $A_v$:**

The small-signal output node KCL (with gate and drain of M2 tied):
$$
g_{m1}\, v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\, v_{out} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}} = 0
$$
Solving for $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$:
$$
A_v = -\frac{g_{m1}}{\dfrac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}} = -g_{m1}\left[\,r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2}\,\right]
$$
Using the design approximation $g_{m2} r_{o2} \gg 1$ and $g_{m2} r_{o1} \gg 1$:
$$
\boxed{\,A_v \approx -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2}}\,}
$$

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Gate-Drain Connected Load | Ideal Current-Source Load |
|---|---|---|
| Small-Signal Load Resistance | $1/g_{m2}$ (small to moderate) | $\infty$ (ideal) |
| Voltage Gain | Moderate ($\approx g_{m1}/g_{m2}$, typ. 5–20) | High ($\approx g_{m1} r_{o1} r_{o2}/(r_{o1}+r_{o2})$) |
| Output Voltage Swing | Larger (swing limited by $V_{GS2}$ drop) | Smaller (headroom needed for current source) |
| Bandwidth | Higher (smaller $R_{out}$ → smaller $R C$ time constant) | Lower (larger $R_{out}$ → larger $R C$ time constant) |
| Process Sensitivity | Sensitive to $V_{TH}$ mismatch | Less sensitive to $V_{TH}$ of M2 |
| Power Consumption | Lower bias current possible | May require higher bias current for same $g_m$ |
| Layout Area | Small (no extra bias circuit) | Larger (requires bias generation) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** A CS amplifier uses M1 with $(W/L)_1 = 50/0.5$ and a gate-drain connected M2 with $(W/L)_2 = 10/0.5$. Given $k_n' = 180\,\mu A/V^2$, $k_p' = 60\,\mu A/V^2$, $\lambda = 0.04\,V^{-1}$ for both, and a bias current of $I_D = 200\,\mu A$, calculate: (i) $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$, (ii) the small-signal output resistance $R_{out}$, (iii) the voltage gain $A_v$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the gain of a CS stage with a gate-drain connected load is **independent of the bias current** in the first-order approximation. What practical limitation does this impose? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Computation:**

(i) Compute transconductances:
$$
g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 180\mu \cdot 100 \cdot 200\mu} = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.683\, mA/V
$$

$$
g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 k_p' (W/L)_2 I_D} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 60\mu \cdot 20 \cdot 200\mu} = \sqrt{4.8 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.693\, mA/V
$$

(ii) Compute $r_{o1}, r_{o2}$ and $R_{out}$:
$$
r_{o1} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.04 \cdot 200\mu} = 125\, k\Omega
$$

$$
\frac{1}{g_{m2}} = \frac{1}{0.693\, mA/V} \approx 1.443\, k\Omega
$$

$$
R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\Big\vert\vert\; \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \;\Big\vert\vert\; r_{o2} = 125\,k \;\Big\vert\vert\; 1.443\,k \;\Big\vert\vert\; 125\,k \approx 1.426\, k\Omega
$$

(iii) Voltage gain:
$$
A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out} = -2.683\,mA/V \cdot 1.426\,k\Omega \approx -3.83\, V/V
$$

Verification using the ratio formula:
$$
A_v \approx -\sqrt{\frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{180\mu \cdot 100}{60\mu \cdot 20}} = -\sqrt{15} \approx -3.87\, V/V
$$

The slight discrepancy is because the ratio formula ignores $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ — the full expression gives a more accurate answer.

**(b) Bias-Current Independence and Practical Limitation:**

The first-order gain $A_v \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ contains the expressions:
$$
g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_D}, \quad g_{m2} = \sqrt{2 k_p' (W/L)_2 I_D}
$$
The bias current $I_D$ appears in *both* $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$. When the ratio is taken, $I_D$ **cancels out**:
$$
A_v \approx -\frac{\sqrt{2 k_n' (W/L)_1 I_D}}{\sqrt{2 k_p' (W/L)_2 I_D}} = -\sqrt{\frac{k_n' (W/L)_1}{k_p' (W/L)_2}}
$$

Therefore, the gain depends only on the device-size ratio and process transconductance parameters — *not* on the bias current.

**Practical Limitation:** The maximum achievable gain is fundamentally bounded by the *device sizing ratio*. To obtain a high gain (e.g., > 50 V/V) using this topology alone, the designer would need impractically large $(W/L)_1$ or impractically small $(W/L)_2$. This is why **higher-gain stages** typically use **current-source loads** (gain $\propto g_m r_o$) or **cascode topologies** (gain $\propto g_m r_o^2$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points — Gate-Drain Connected Loads: CS**

- ☐ **Gate-drain connection = diode-connected MOSFET**, **always in saturation** ($V_{DS} = V_{GS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$).
- ☐ **Small-signal resistance** of diode-connected MOSFET: $r_d = 1/g_m \;\vert\vert\; r_o \approx 1/g_m$.
- ☐ **Voltage gain of CS stage**: $A_v = -g_{m1}\left[r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; (1/g_{m2}) \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}\right] \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$.
- ☐ **First-order gain** depends on **device-size ratios only** — independent of bias current.
- ☐ **Input resistance** is **infinite** (MOSFET gate is insulated).
- ☐ **Output resistance** is approximately $1/g_{m2}$ — *small* compared to a current-source load.
- ☐ **Trade-off**: Simplicity and self-biasing vs. **modest gain** (typ. 5–20 V/V).
- ☐ **Use case**: First stages of op-amps, bias current mirrors, level shifters, low-power front-ends.
- ☐ **Comparison with current-source load**: Current-source load gives *higher* gain but *smaller* output swing and *more complex* biasing.
- ☐ **Always state** the full parallel combination for $R_{out}$ before approximating — examiners award marks for completeness.
- ☐ **Body effect**: When M2 is a PMOS in a separate well tied to its source, $V_{TH,p}$ is *constant*; but if the well is tied to $V_{DD}$, $V_{TH,p}$ shifts with $V_{OUT}$ (subtle effect, mention in higher-mark derivations).
- ☐ **Memorise** the three core equations: (1) $r_d \approx 1/g_m$, (2) $R_{out} = r_{o1} \;\vert\vert\; (1/g_{m2}) \;\vert\vert\; r_{o2}$, (3) $A_v \approx -\sqrt{k_n' (W/L)_1 / [k_p' (W/L)_2]}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** in Analog VLSI Design refers to the behavior of a MOSFET amplifier circuit as a function of the input signal frequency. It characterizes how the magnitude of the voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ and the phase shift $\phi$ vary with the angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ of the applied small-signal input.

In the context of single-stage and multi-stage CMOS amplifiers, the **frequency response** is fundamentally dictated by the intrinsic and extrinsic capacitances of the MOSFET, namely the **gate-to-source capacitance** ($C_{gs}$), **gate-to-drain capacitance** ($C_{gd}$), **drain-to-body capacitance** ($C_{db}$), and **source-to-body capacitance** ($C_{sb}$). As frequency increases, these parasitic capacitances introduce **poles** and **zeros** in the transfer function, which roll-off the gain and shift the phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition — Pole**: A frequency $p$ at which the transfer function magnitude tends to infinity (i.e., the denominator becomes zero). Equivalently, the frequency at which the short-circuit transadmittance of the network becomes infinite.
> 
> **Key Definition — Zero**: A frequency $z$ at which the transfer function magnitude drops to zero (i.e., the numerator becomes zero). Equivalently, the frequency at which the open-circuit transadmittance becomes zero.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Audio Bass-Treble Knob Analogy**
> Imagine a Hi-Fi speaker system with bass, mid, and treble knobs. The **low-frequency (bass)** signals correspond to slowly varying signals. At low frequency, the MOSFET's internal capacitors act like **open circuits** (since $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ is huge), so the amplifier delivers its full DC gain. The **high-frequency (treble)** signals correspond to rapidly varying signals. At high frequency, the capacitors act like **short circuits**, shunting the signal away from the output and reducing the gain.
> 
> Thus, the frequency response is essentially a "filter" the amplifier inherently applies to the signal — a **low-pass filter** in the simplest case, where the gain is maximum at DC and rolls off at higher frequencies.

**Why does this happen physically?** Every MOSFET has internal charge storage at its terminals (gate oxide capacitance, junction depletion capacitances). These capacitances must be charged and discharged through the surrounding circuit resistances, and this charging/discharging takes a finite time. As frequency increases, there is less time to charge the capacitors to the required voltage, so the output signal amplitude decreases.

### 1.3 Standard Frequency Response Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following metrics are universally used to quantify frequency response in KTU 2024 Scheme Analog VLSI examinations:

- **DC Gain ($A_0$)**: The mid-band or low-frequency gain of the amplifier, where $\omega \to 0$.
- **3-dB Bandwidth ($\omega_H$ or $f_H$)**: The frequency at which the gain drops to $\frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot A_0$. The "3-dB" refers to the $20 \log_{10}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}) \approx -3\,\text{dB}$ drop.
- **Lower Cut-off Frequency ($\omega_L$)**: For amplifiers with coupling/ bypass capacitors, the frequency below which gain falls.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**: $GBW = A_0 \cdot \omega_H$ — a constant for a single-pole amplifier.
- **Unity-Gain Frequency ($\omega_T$)**: The frequency at which the open-loop gain falls to unity (1). For a single-pole system, $\omega_T = A_0 \cdot \omega_H = GBW$.
