<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The High-Frequency MOSFET Small-Signal Model

At high frequencies, the simple small-signal model (which treats the MOSFET as a pure voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$) is **incomplete**. We must include the device's internal capacitances, leading to the **High-Frequency Small-Signal Model**.

The intrinsic MOSFET contributes four capacitances:
- $C_{gs}$ — gate-to-source (oxide capacitance, mostly)
- $C_{gd}$ — gate-to-drain (oxide capacitance, mostly)
- $C_{db}$ — drain-to-body (junction depletion capacitance)
- $C_{sb}$ — source-to-body (junction depletion capacitance)

When the source and body are tied together (a common configuration in n-well processes), $C_{sb}$ becomes shorted out and is removed from the AC equivalent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller Effect in MOSFETs**: Since the gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ appears between the input node (gate) and the output node (drain), and the drain voltage is an **inverted and amplified** version of the gate voltage, the current through $C_{gd}$ is much larger than expected. This effectively **multiplies** $C_{gd}$ by a factor of $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ when viewed from the input, known as the **Miller Multiplier**.

### 2.2 The Common-Source (CS) Amplifier Frequency Response — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1**: Identify the small-signal equivalent circuit. The CS stage consists of the MOSFET, a drain resistor $R_D$ (or active load), and a current source biasing the source terminal to ground.

**Step 2**: Replace the MOSFET with its high-frequency model: a transconductance source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $C_{gd}$ at the output, plus $C_{gs}$ from gate to source, and $C_{db}$ from drain to body.

**Step 3**: Identify the input node (gate) and output node (drain). Note that the source is at AC ground.

**Step 4**: Apply the **Miller Approximation**: Because the gain from gate to drain is large and negative ($A_v = -g_m R_D$), $C_{gd}$ appears as:
$$C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D) \quad \text{(at the input)}$$

$$C_{M2} = C_{gd} \cdot \frac{g_m R_D + 1 - 1}{g_m R_D} \approx C_{gd} \quad \text{(at the output)}$$

**Step 5**: The input pole is determined by the total capacitance at the gate node and the source resistance $R_S$ driving it:
$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S \cdot (C_{gs} + C_{M1})}$$

**Step 6**: The output pole is determined by the total capacitance at the drain and the output resistance $R_D \parallel r_o$:
$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{(R_D \parallel r_o) \cdot (C_{db} + C_{M2})}$$

**Step 7**: The dominant pole is the lower of $\omega_{p,in}$ and $\omega_{p,out}$.

### 2.3 The Source Follower (Common-Drain) Frequency Response

The source follower has a gain close to unity ($A_v \approx 1$) from gate to source. Because the Miller multiplier on $C_{gd}$ is $(1 - A_v) \approx 0$, the Miller effect is **negligible**, and the source follower exhibits a much higher bandwidth than the CS stage.

The dominant pole is given by:
$$\omega_H = \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{gd} + C_{sb} \cdot (1 + g_m R_S) \cdot \text{loading}}$$

In many texts, the simplified dominant-pole expression is:
$$\omega_H \approx \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{sb} \cdot (1 + g_m R_S)}$$

### 2.4 The Common-Gate (CG) Amplifier Frequency Response

The CG stage has a low input impedance ($1/g_m$) and a high output impedance. It exhibits excellent high-frequency response because there is **no Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$. The dominant pole is:
$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{(R_D \parallel r_o)(C_{gd} + C_{db})}$$

### 2.5 The Cascode Amplifier Frequency Response

The cascode (CS + CG stacked) reduces the Miller effect on $C_{gd1}$ of the input device because the gain from the gate of $M_1$ to the drain of $M_1$ is small (approximately $-1$, since $M_2$ shields the drain of $M_1$).

The dominant pole is approximately:
$$\omega_H \approx \frac{1}{R_S(C_{gs1} + 2C_{gd1}) + (R_D \parallel r_{o,cascode})(C_{db1} + C_{gs2} + C_{gd2} + C_{db2})}$$

where $r_{o,cascode} \approx g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1}$ is the very high output resistance of the cascode.

### 2.6 The Differential Amplifier Frequency Response

For a symmetric differential pair with resistive loads, the differential half-circuit behaves exactly like a CS stage. Therefore, the differential 3-dB frequency is identical to the CS stage formula. The CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) at high frequency is also analyzed by examining the common-mode half-circuit.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the most important formulas for Frequency Response, aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCEVT601 Module 4.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Stage** |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Midband Gain (CS) | $A_{v0} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $g_m$ = transconductance | Common-Source |
| Midband Gain (CD) | $A_{v0} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $R_S$ = source resistance | Source Follower |
| Midband Gain (CG) | $A_{v0} = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $R_D$ = drain resistance | Common-Gate |
| Miller Input Cap. | $C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$ | $A_v$ = gain from gate to drain | Any inverting stage |
| Miller Output Cap. | $C_{M2} = C_{gd} \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$ | Reduces to $C_{gd}$ when $\vert A_v\vert \gg 1$ | Any inverting stage |
| CS Input Pole | $\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S (C_{gs} + C_{M1})}$ | $R_S$ = source resistance | Common-Source |
| CS Output Pole | $\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{(R_D \parallel r_o)(C_{db} + C_{M2})}$ | Output pole | Common-Source |
| CG Dominant Pole | $\omega_H = \frac{1}{(R_D \parallel r_o)(C_{gd} + C_{db})}$ | No Miller effect | Common-Gate |
| Cascode Dominant Pole | $\omega_H \approx \frac{1}{R_S C_{gs1} + (R_D \parallel r_{o,cascode})C_L}$ | $C_L$ = total load cap. | Cascode |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBW = \vert A_{v0}\vert \cdot \omega_H$ | Constant for single-pole system | All |
| Unity-Gain Frequency | $\omega_T = \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{gd}}$ | Intrinsic device limit | All |
| Short-Circuit $\tau$ Method | $\omega_H \approx \sum_i \frac{1}{R_{i0} C_i}$ | $R_{i0}$ = resistance seen by $C_i$ | General |
| Open-Circuit $\tau$ Method | $\omega_H \approx \sum_i \frac{1}{R_{i\infty} C_i}$ | More accurate generalization | General |

### 2.8 Short-Circuit and Open-Circuit Time Constants Method

For a circuit with multiple capacitors, an elegant analytical method avoids the need to compute the full transfer function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-Circuit Time Constants (OCTC) Method** by Hosur-Lewis: The dominant pole of an amplifier can be estimated as:
> $$\omega_H \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{R_{i\infty} C_i}$$
> where $R_{i\infty}$ is the resistance seen at the terminals of capacitor $C_i$ when **all other capacitors are replaced by open circuits** (i.e., the original operating point is preserved).

> [!NOTE]
> **Short-Circuit Time Constants (SCTC) Method** by Pstar Gray: An upper bound on $\omega_H$ is given by:
> $$\omega_H \geq \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i0} C_i \right)^{-1}$$
> where $R_{i0}$ is the resistance seen at the terminals of $C_i$ when **all other capacitors are replaced by short circuits** (i.e., the high-frequency limit).

The exact dominant pole $\omega_H$ lies between these two estimates. If only one capacitor dominates, both methods yield the same answer.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Relevance**: Frequency response analysis is at the heart of every analog IC design:
> - **RF Front-Ends**: Low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) and mixers in cell phones require explicit $\omega_T$ optimization.
> - **Op-Amps**: The gain-bandwidth product (GBW) sets the maximum slew rate and settling time of operational amplifiers.
> - **Data Converters**: Sample-and-hold circuits and ADC drivers demand $-3\,\text{dB}$ bandwidths in the gigahertz range.
> - **SerDes Links**: High-speed serial links require explicit analysis of poles/zeros to meet eye-diagram masks.
> - **Filters**: Continuous-time $G_m$-$C$ and active-RC filters are designed entirely in the frequency domain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Common-Source High-Frequency Transfer Function

**Starting Point**: Consider a CS stage with the MOSFET replaced by its high-frequency small-signal model, driven by a source resistance $R_S$ with load resistance $R_D$ at the drain (ignoring $r_o$ for simplicity). Include $C_{gs}$ at the input and $C_{gd}$ between gate and drain, plus $C_{db}$ at the output.

**Step 1 — Write KCL at the Output Node (Drain):**

The current entering the drain node from the controlled source is $g_m v_{gs}$, the current through $R_D$ is $-V_{out}/R_D$, the current through $C_{gd}$ is the current flowing from gate to drain, and the current through $C_{db}$ is $-V_{out} \cdot sC_{db}$.

$$g_m v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(V_{in} - V_{out}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_D} + sC_{db} V_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the Input Node (Gate):**

The current entering the gate from the source $V_{in}$ through $R_S$ is $(V_{in} - V_{gs})/R_S$, and the current leaving through $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ is $sC_{gs} v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(v_{gs} - V_{out})$.

$$\frac{V_{in} - v_{gs}}{R_S} = sC_{gs} v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(v_{gs} - V_{out})$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{in} = v_{gs}\left[1 + sR_S(C_{gs} + C_{gd})\right] - sR_S C_{gd} V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{out}$ in terms of $v_{gs}$ from the output KCL:**

$$g_m v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(V_{in} - V_{out}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_D} + sC_{db} V_{out}$$

Wait, $V_{in}$ is the gate voltage itself, so $V_{in} = v_{gs}$ approximately (since the gate draws no DC current). Let us denote the gate voltage as $V_g = v_{gs}$ since the source is at AC ground.

$$g_m v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(v_{gs} - V_{out}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_D} + sC_{db} V_{out}$$

Collecting $V_{out}$ on one side:

$$v_{gs}(g_m + sC_{gd}) = V_{out}\left[\frac{1}{R_D} + s(C_{gd} + C_{db})\right]$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{v_{gs}(g_m + sC_{gd})}{\frac{1}{R_D} + s(C_{gd} + C_{db})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Miller Approximation for Simplicity:**

If the gain is large in magnitude, $V_{out} \approx -g_m R_D \cdot v_{gs}$ at low frequencies, and the current through $C_{gd}$ is dominated by the output-side voltage swing. This gives the Miller multiplier.

The total input capacitance is:

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 5 — Input Pole Frequency:**

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_S \cdot [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]}$$

**Step 6 — Output Pole Frequency:**

$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{R_D \cdot (C_{gd} + C_{db})}$$

**Step 7 — Approximate Transfer Function:**

Assuming the input pole is dominant (typically true for high-gain CS stages):

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}(s) \approx \frac{-g_m R_D}{1 + s/\omega_{p,in}}$$

where $\omega_{p,in} \ll \omega_{p,out}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Source Follower Dominant Pole

Consider a source follower with the source connected to $R_S$ to ground, body tied to ground, gate driven by $V_{in}$ through $R_S$ source resistance (use $R_G$ to avoid confusion: let source resistance be $R_{sig}$). Drain is at AC ground.

The capacitors involved are $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{sb}$.

**Step 1 — Output KCL at the Source Node:**

$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + s(C_{gs} + C_{sb})V_{out} - sC_{gd}(V_{in} - V_{out})$$

Wait, let us be careful with signs. The current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source. At the source node, the current $g_m v_{gs}$ is entering (from the MOSFET), and the currents leaving are through $R_S$, $C_{sb}$, and $C_{gd}$ (toward the gate).

$$g_m v_{gs} - sC_{gd}(v_{gs} - V_{in}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sC_{sb} V_{out} + sC_{gs} V_{out}$$

Note: $v_{gs} = V_{in} - V_{out}$ since the source voltage is $V_{out}$ and gate is $V_{in}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $v_{gs} = V_{in} - V_{out}$:**

$$g_m (V_{in} - V_{out}) - sC_{gd}(V_{in} - V_{out} - V_{in}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sC_{sb} V_{out} + sC_{gs} V_{out}$$

$$g_m (V_{in} - V_{out}) + sC_{gd} V_{out} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sC_{sb} V_{out} + sC_{gs} V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Collect $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$ terms:**

$$g_m V_{in} - g_m V_{out} + sC_{gd} V_{out} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sV_{out}(C_{sb} + C_{gs})$$

$$g_m V_{in} = V_{out}\left[g_m + \frac{1}{R_S} + s(C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd})\right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}(s) = \frac{g_m}{g_m + \frac{1}{R_S} + s(C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd})}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the low-frequency gain denominator:**

The low-frequency (DC) gain is $A_{v0} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}(s) = \frac{A_{v0}}{1 + sR_S(C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd})/(1 + g_m R_S)}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Pole Frequency:**

$$\omega_H = \frac{1 + g_m R_S}{R_S (C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd})}$$

For $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$\omega_H \approx \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: The source follower bandwidth is much higher than the CS stage because the numerator factor $g_m$ (large) appears in the pole frequency, whereas the CS stage has the input resistance $R_S$ (which is typically small or comparable to $1/g_m$) in the pole denominator.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cascode Frequency Response (Dominant Pole Approximation)

For a cascode stage with $M_1$ (CS) driving $M_2$ (CG), the gain of $M_1$ from gate to its drain is approximately:
$$A_{v1} \approx -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m2}} = -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} \approx -1$$

(for matched devices with equal bias currents).

**Step 1 — Miller Effect on $C_{gd1}$:**

Since $\vert A_{v1} \vert \approx 1$, the Miller multiplier is:
$$C_{M1} = C_{gd1}(1 - A_{v1}) = C_{gd1}(1 + 1) = 2C_{gd1}$$

This is a **dramatic reduction** compared to the CS stage where the multiplier was $(1 + g_m R_D)$ (could be 10-100x).

**Step 2 — Total Input Capacitance:**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{gs1} + 2C_{gd1}$$

**Step 3 — Input Pole:**

$$\omega_{p,in} = \frac{1}{R_{sig} (C_{gs1} + 2C_{gd1})}$$

**Step 4 — Output Pole:**

The output node sees the cascode output resistance $R_{out,cascode} = (g_{m2} r_{o2}) r_{o1} \parallel R_D \approx R_D$ in parallel with the total output capacitance.

$$\omega_{p,out} = \frac{1}{R_{out,cascode} (C_{db1} + C_{gs2} + C_{gd2} + C_{db2} + C_L)}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pole:**

The dominant pole is the lower of $\omega_{p,in}$ and $\omega_{p,out}$. For typical biasing, the cascode exhibits bandwidth comparable to the CG stage — significantly higher than the CS stage, while retaining the high output resistance and gain of a cascode.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Amplifier Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following comparison matrix is a high-yield study aid for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) questions on amplifier frequency response.**

| **Topology** | **Midband Gain** | **Miller Multiplier on $C_{gd}$** | **Approx. $\omega_H$** | **GBW** | **Best Use Case** |
|--------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------|---------|-------------------|
| **CS with $R_D$** | $-g_m R_D$ | $(1 + g_m R_D)$ — large | $\frac{1}{R_{sig} C_{gs}(1 + g_m R_D C_{gd}/C_{gs})}$ | $\frac{g_m}{C_{gd}}$ | High gain, inverting |
| **CD (Source Follower)** | $\frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $\approx 1$ (small) | $\frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{sb}}$ | $\approx \frac{g_m}{C_{gs}}$ | Buffer, level shifter |
| **CG** | $g_m R_D$ | $1$ (no inversion) | $\frac{1}{R_D(C_{gd} + C_{db})}$ | $\frac{g_m}{C_{gd} + C_{db}}$ | High-freq, low $Z_{in}$ |
| **Cascode** | $-g_{m1} R_{out,cascode}$ | $\approx 2$ (reduced) | $\frac{1}{R_{sig}(C_{gs1} + 2C_{gd1})}$ | $\frac{g_{m1}}{2C_{gd1}}$ | High gain + high BW |
| **Diff. Pair** | $-g_m R_D$ | Same as CS | Same as CS half-circuit | Same as CS | Balanced signaling |
