<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **dominant pole** in the context of MOSFET amplifier frequency response. Why is it important in determining the bandwidth of an amplifier?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **dominant pole** is a pole whose frequency is significantly lower than all other poles in the transfer function. In a Bode magnitude plot, this pole dictates the $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ roll-off and determines the **3-dB bandwidth** ($\omega_H$) of the amplifier. It is important because if one pole is dominant, the bandwidth can be estimated simply as $\omega_H \approx \omega_{p,dominant}$ without solving the full characteristic polynomial, and the gain-bandwidth product becomes a well-defined constant: $GBW = \vert A_{v0}\vert \cdot \omega_H$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **Miller Effect**? Explain how it impacts the high-frequency response of a Common-Source amplifier.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Miller Effect** refers to the multiplication of a feedback capacitance $C_f$ (such as $C_{gd}$) when it bridges a node of voltage $V_1$ (input) and a node of voltage $V_2 = A_v V_1$ (output). The current through $C_f$ from the input side is $I = sC_f (V_1 - V_2) = sC_f (1 - A_v) V_1$, which is equivalent to a capacitance $C_M = C_f(1 - A_v)$ connected from the input to ground.
> 
> In a CS amplifier, $A_v = -g_m R_D$ is large and negative, so the Miller multiplier $(1 - A_v) = (1 + g_m R_D)$ is large (e.g., 10–100). This **dramatically increases** the effective input capacitance, lowering the input pole frequency and **reducing the bandwidth**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice-1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the high-frequency transfer function of a Common-Source amplifier with resistive load $R_D$ driven from a source resistance $R_{sig}$. Include the capacitances $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{db}$ in the analysis. Clearly identify the dominant pole and discuss the role of the Miller effect on $C_{gd}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A CS amplifier is biased such that $g_m = 5\,\text{mA/V}$, $R_{sig} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$. The MOSFET has $C_{gs} = 100\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 20\,\text{fF}$, and $C_{db} = 30\,\text{fF}$. Calculate: (i) the midband voltage gain, (ii) the Miller-multiplied input capacitance, (iii) the input pole frequency, (iv) the output pole frequency, and (v) the gain-bandwidth product.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Small-signal equivalent circuit.** Replace the MOSFET with the high-frequency model: $C_{gs}$ from gate to source (source is AC ground), $C_{gd}$ from gate to drain, and $C_{db}$ from drain to source (body grounded). The drain is connected to $R_D$ to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground).

**Step 2 — KCL at the drain node:**

$$g_m V_{gs} + sC_{gd}(V_g - V_{out}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_D} + sC_{db} V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — KCL at the gate node (input side):**

$$\frac{V_{sig} - V_g}{R_{sig}} = sC_{gs} V_g + sC_{gd}(V_g - V_{out})$$

**Step 4 — Apply Miller Approximation.** Since the gain $A_v = V_{out}/V_g = -g_m R_D$ is large in magnitude, we can simplify by treating the $C_{gd}$ contribution as a grounded capacitor at the input of value $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$.

**Step 5 — Solve the simplified system.** With this approximation, the gate node is decoupled from the drain, and the poles are independent:

- **Input pole**: $\omega_{p1} = \frac{1}{R_{sig} (C_{gs} + C_M)} = \frac{1}{R_{sig}[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]}$
- **Output pole**: $\omega_{p2} = \frac{1}{R_D (C_{gd} + C_{db})}$

**Step 6 — Identify the dominant pole.** For typical values, $\omega_{p1} \ll \omega_{p2}$, so $\omega_{p1}$ is the dominant pole. The high-frequency response is:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{sig}}(s) = \frac{-g_m R_D}{\left(1 + \frac{s}{\omega_{p1}}\right)\left(1 + \frac{s}{\omega_{p2}}\right)} \approx \frac{-g_m R_D}{1 + \frac{s}{\omega_{p1}}} \quad \text{for } \omega \ll \omega_{p2}$$

**Discussion of Miller Effect**: $C_{gd}$ would normally be just $20\,\text{fF}$, but with a gain of $-g_m R_D = -20$, it is multiplied to $20 \times 21 = 420\,\text{fF}$ — a $21\times$ increase. This lowers $\omega_{p1}$ by a factor of 21, severely degrading the bandwidth.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $g_m = 5\,\text{mA/V} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/V}$, $R_{sig} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 100\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 20\,\text{fF}$, $C_{db} = 30\,\text{fF}$.

**(i) Midband voltage gain:**

$$A_{v0} = -g_m R_D = -(5 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^3) = -20\,\text{V/V}$$

In decibels: $20 \log_{10}(20) \approx 26\,\text{dB}$.

**(ii) Miller-multiplied input capacitance:**

$$C_M = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D) = 20 \times 10^{-15} \times (1 + 20) = 20 \times 21 = 420\,\text{fF}$$

Total input capacitance: $C_{in,total} = C_{gs} + C_M = 100 + 420 = 520\,\text{fF}$.

**(iii) Input pole frequency:**

$$\omega_{p1} = \frac{1}{R_{sig} \cdot C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{(10^3)(520 \times 10^{-15})} = \frac{1}{5.2 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\omega_{p1} \approx 1.923 \times 10^{9}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 1.92\,\text{GHz}$$

In Hz: $f_{p1} = \frac{\omega_{p1}}{2\pi} \approx 306\,\text{MHz}$.

**(iv) Output pole frequency:**

$$\omega_{p2} = \frac{1}{R_D (C_{gd} + C_{db})} = \frac{1}{(4 \times 10^3)(20 \times 10^{-15} + 30 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$\omega_{p2} = \frac{1}{(4 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-15})} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-10}} = 5 \times 10^{9}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 5\,\text{GHz}$$

In Hz: $f_{p2} \approx 796\,\text{MHz}$.

Since $\omega_{p1} < \omega_{p2}$, the **input pole is dominant**.

**(v) Gain-bandwidth product:**

$$GBW = \vert A_{v0}\vert \cdot \omega_{p1} = 20 \times 1.923 \times 10^{9} \approx 3.85 \times 10^{10}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 6.12\,\text{GHz}$$

The unity-gain frequency is $\omega_T \approx 6.12\,\text{GHz}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice-2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of small-signal analysis, derive the high-frequency dominant pole expression for a **Source Follower** (Common-Drain) amplifier. Compare its bandwidth advantage over a Common-Source amplifier with the same device dimensions and bias.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the **Open-Circuit Time Constants (OCTC) method**, estimate the dominant pole of a CS amplifier with the following parameters: $R_{sig} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 200\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 50\,\text{fF}$, $C_{db} = 80\,\text{fF}$, $g_m = 4\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_o = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$. State any assumptions made.

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Source Follower small-signal circuit.** The source is connected to $R_S$ to ground; drain is at AC ground. The relevant capacitances are $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{sb}$ (with body grounded).

**Step 2 — Output KCL at the source node.** Let $V_{in} = V_g$ and $V_{out} = V_s$. The current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source. At the source node:

$$g_m v_{gs} + sC_{gd}(V_{in} - V_{out}) = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sC_{gs} V_{out} + sC_{sb} V_{out}$$

where $v_{gs} = V_{in} - V_{out}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$g_m(V_{in} - V_{out}) + sC_{gd} V_{out} - 0 = \frac{V_{out}}{R_S} + sV_{out}(C_{gs} + C_{sb})$$

$$g_m V_{in} = V_{out}\left[g_m + \frac{1}{R_S} + s(C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd})\right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pole:**

$$\omega_H = \frac{g_m + \frac{1}{R_S}}{C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd}}$$

For $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$\omega_H \approx \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{sb} - C_{gd}}$$

**Comparison with CS amplifier**: For the CS amplifier with the same device, the dominant pole is:

$$\omega_{H,CS} = \frac{1}{R_{sig}[C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]}$$

Since $g_m$ is much larger than $R_{sig}^{-1}$, and the Miller multiplication term $(1 + g_m R_D)$ is absent in the source follower, the source follower bandwidth is **orders of magnitude higher** than the CS stage bandwidth. This is the key reason source followers are used as **output buffers** in high-frequency analog design.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $R_{sig} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 200\,\text{fF}$, $C_{gd} = 50\,\text{fF}$, $C_{db} = 80\,\text{fF}$, $g_m = 4\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_o = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Assumption**: $r_o$ is included in parallel with $R_D$ at the output node.

**Step 1 — Compute the midband gain (needed for Miller effect on $C_{gd}$):**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(4 \times 10^{-3})(5\text{k} \parallel 20\text{k}) = -(4 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^3) = -16$$

So $R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \times 20}{25} = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — OCTC method.** For each capacitor, we compute the resistance seen at its terminals when all other capacitors are **open-circuited**.

**For $C_{gs}$:** With $C_{gd}$ and $C_{db}$ open, the resistance seen at the $C_{gs}$ terminals is simply $R_{sig}$ (looking into the gate, no DC current flows into the gate of a MOSFET).

$$R_{gs,\infty} = R_{sig} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**For $C_{gd}$:** With $C_{gs}$ and $C_{db}$ open, the resistance seen at the $C_{gd}$ terminals is from gate to drain. The gate side looks into $R_{sig}$ (with the source grounded, so the gate is "floating" w.r.t. source), and the drain side looks into $R_D \parallel r_o$. The total resistance between the two terminals is:

$$R_{gd,\infty} = R_{sig} + (R_D \parallel r_o) + g_m R_{sig} (R_D \parallel r_o) = R_{sig} + R_L(1 + g_m R_{sig})$$

Using the standard result for a CS stage with $R_{sig}$ and $R_L = R_D \parallel r_o$:

$$R_{gd,\infty} = R_{sig}(1 + g_m R_L) + R_L = 2\text{k}(1 + 16) + 4\text{k} = 34 + 4 = 38\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**For $C_{db}$:** With $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ open, the resistance seen at the $C_{db}$ terminals is from drain to source (source is grounded). The drain node sees $R_D \parallel r_o$ to AC ground (through the supply).

$$R_{db,\infty} = R_D \parallel r_o = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Sum the time constants:**

$$\sum_i R_{i\infty} C_i = R_{gs,\infty} C_{gs} + R_{gd,\infty} C_{gd} + R_{db,\infty} C_{db}$$

$$= (2 \times 10^3)(200 \times 10^{-15}) + (38 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-15}) + (4 \times 10^3)(80 \times 10^{-15})$$

$$= 0.4 \times 10^{-9} + 1.9 \times 10^{-9} + 0.32 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$= 2.62 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = 2.62\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Dominant pole estimate:**

$$\omega_H \approx \frac{1}{2.62 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 3.82 \times 10^{8}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 382\,\text{MHz}$$

In Hz: $f_H = \frac{\omega_H}{2\pi} \approx 60.8\,\text{MHz}$.

**Verification with SCTC method (lower bound check):** With all other capacitors short-circuited, the resistance seen by $C_{gs}$ is $R_{sig} \parallel (\text{some value})$ — this is harder to compute without further simplification, but the OCTC estimate of $\approx 382\,\text{MHz}$ is a reasonable upper-bound estimate of the dominant pole.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Frequency Response Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the body effect in $C_{sb}$**: When the source is not at AC ground (e.g., source degeneration), $C_{sb}$ is **NOT** shorted out and must be included with the appropriate Miller-like multiplication.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $C_{gs}$ and $C_{ox}$**: $C_{gs}$ is the **gate-to-source capacitance** (intrinsic + overlap), not the total gate oxide capacitance. Use $C_{gs} = \frac{2}{3} W L C_{ox} + C_{ov}$ for long-channel devices in saturation.
> 
> 3. **Using $C_{gd}$ without the Miller multiplier**: Many students write $\omega_H = \frac{1}{R_{sig} C_{gs}}$ and forget that $C_{gd}$ contributes significantly through the Miller effect. **Always include $C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$ in the input capacitance**.
> 
> 4. **Sign error in Miller formula**: The Miller multiplier is $(1 - A_v)$, NOT $(1 + A_v)$ directly. For an inverting CS stage with $A_v < 0$, this becomes $(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$. Many students lose 1-2 marks by writing the wrong sign.
> 
> 5. **Confusing $\omega_T$ with $\omega_H$**: $\omega_T$ is the **unity-gain frequency** of the device (an intrinsic property), while $\omega_H$ is the **3-dB bandwidth** of the amplifier (a circuit property). For a single-stage CS amplifier, $\omega_T = g_m / (C_{gs} + C_{gd})$, while $\omega_H \approx \omega_T / \vert A_{v0}\vert$.
> 
> 6. **Not stating the dominant pole assumption**: When asked to estimate $\omega_H$, always explicitly state which pole is dominant and why.
> 
> 7. **Units inconsistency**: Always express $\omega$ in rad/s and $f$ in Hz, and clearly label which is which. $1\,\text{GHz} = 2\pi \times 10^9\,\text{rad/s}$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frequency Response (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- ☐ **High-frequency MOSFET model** includes $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{db}$, $C_{sb}$ in addition to $g_m$ and $r_o$.
- ☐ **Miller Effect** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v\vert)$ for inverting CS stages.
- ☐ **CS stage dominant pole** is at the **input** due to the Miller-multiplied $C_{gd}$.
- ☐ **Source Follower** has minimal Miller effect on $C_{gd}$ (since $A_v \approx 1$), giving **highest bandwidth**.
- ☐ **Common-Gate** has **no Miller effect** because there is no gate-to-drain voltage swing amplification.
- ☐ **Cascode** reduces the Miller multiplier from $(1 + g_m R_D)$ to about $2$, greatly extending bandwidth while preserving high gain.
- ☐ **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** $= \vert A_{v0}\vert \cdot \omega_H$ is a constant for a single-pole amplifier.
- ☐ **Unity-Gain Frequency** $\omega_T = \frac{g_m}{C_{gs} + C_{gd}}$ is a device-intrinsic metric.
- ☐ **OCTC method**: $\omega_H \approx \sum_i \frac{1}{R_{i\infty} C_i}$ where $R_{i\infty}$ is the resistance seen by $C_i$ with all other caps open.
- ☐ **SCTC method**: gives a lower bound on $\omega_H$; the true $\omega_H$ lies between OCTC and SCTC estimates.
- ☐ For **differential pair**, the half-circuit analysis applies: differential response = CS half-circuit response.
- ☐ **Source-body capacitance $C_{sb}$** is shorted only if body and source are at the same AC potential.
- ☐ **Phase shift** at the dominant pole is $-45°$; at $\omega \gg \omega_H$, it asymptotically approaches $-90°$ per pole.
- ☐ **Bode plot slope**: $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per pole; $-40\,\text{dB/decade}$ for two coincident poles.
- ☐ **Cascode vs CS trade-off**: Cascode has higher output resistance, higher gain, but **slightly lower $\omega_T$** due to $C_{gs2}$ loading the drain of $M_1$.
- ☐ For **short-channel devices**, use $\frac{2}{3}$ replaced by an empirical factor $\approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ in $C_{gs}$ calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Analog VLSI Design, **Noise** refers to any unwanted random fluctuation in an electrical signal that interferes with the desired information-bearing signal. In MOSFETs, noise originates from the discrete nature of charge carriers and the stochastic (random) physical processes governing their motion through the channel, across junctions, and at interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> Noise in MOSFETs is the random time-varying disturbance superimposed on the signal, characterized statistically by its **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**. The two dominant intrinsic noise sources in a MOSFET are **Thermal Noise** (white noise, frequency-independent) and **Flicker Noise** (1/f noise, inversely proportional to frequency).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to listen to a soft whisper (the desired analog signal) in a crowded, noisy marketplace. The whisper is your clean voltage or current, while the marketplace chatter is **noise**. Just as the listener's ear can only resolve a whisper above a certain loudness of background chatter, an analog circuit can only resolve a small signal if the noise floor is sufficiently low. The metric **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** quantifies exactly this — how loud is my signal compared to the unwanted chatter?

In a transistor, the "chatter" comes from:
- **Random thermal agitation** of electrons (Thermal Noise) — like molecules jostling in hot water.
- **Random trapping and detrapping** of charge carriers at the silicon-oxide interface (Flicker Noise) — like stones randomly falling into and being removed from a flowing stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Thermal Noise** = *White* (flat PSD, independent of frequency), unavoidable due to physics.
> - **Flicker Noise** = *Colored* (∝ 1/f), dominant at low frequencies, technology-dependent.

### 1.3 Classification of Noise Sources in MOSFETs

| **Category** | **Source Location** | **Physical Origin** |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Thermal Noise | Inversion layer | Random carrier velocity fluctuation |
| Flicker (1/f) Noise | Si–SiO₂ interface | Carrier trapping/detrapping at oxide |
| Gate Resistance Thermal Noise | Poly-Si gate | Thermal agitation in gate material |
| Shot Noise | Gate leakage / junctions | Discrete carrier injection |
| Source/Drain Resistance Noise | Parasitic resistances | Thermal noise of ohmic regions |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Noise Analysis (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, denoted $S_n(f)$, measured in **V²/Hz** or **A²/Hz**.
- **Input-Referred Noise (IRN)**, $V_{n,in}^2$, in **V²/Hz**.
- **Noise Figure (NF)** in **dB**.
- **Corner Frequency** $f_c$, in **Hz**, where thermal noise equals flicker noise.
- **Boltzmann Constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (always bolded).
- **Elementary Charge** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** (always bolded).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Thermal Noise in MOSFET Channel (The Dominant Source)

The drain current in saturation exhibits random fluctuations due to the random thermal motion of carriers in the channel. This is modeled as a **noise current source** connected between drain and source.

**Why it exists:** Every resistor (and the channel acts as a voltage-controlled resistor) has thermally agitated charge carriers that produce a fluctuating voltage across its terminals, even with no applied signal. The channel resistance is $r_{ds}$ (in saturation) or the triode-region resistance.

**How it is modeled:** As a current source with PSD:
$$S_{I_{d,th}}(f) = 4 k T \gamma \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

Or equivalently in terms of transconductance:
$$S_{I_{d,th}}(f) = 4 k T \gamma g_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $g_m$?**
> Because $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})$, the two forms are mathematically identical. Designers prefer the second form because $g_m$ is easily measurable and directly links the noise to the small-signal gain.

**The Gamma Factor $\gamma$:**
- **Long-channel device:** $\gamma = \frac{2}{3}$
- **Short-channel device:** $\gamma \approx 2$ to $3$ (due to hot-carrier effects, velocity saturation, and HALO implants).
- This is one of the most heavily tested values in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Flicker (1/f) Noise

**Physical Mechanism:** Charge carriers moving along the Si–SiO₂ interface are randomly trapped and released by oxide traps. This causes fluctuation in the local carrier density, which in turn modulates the drain current.

**PSD Model (SPICE BSIM form):**
$$S_{V_{g,1/f}}(f) = \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L f}$$

$$S_{I_{d,1/f}}(f) = g_m^2 \cdot S_{V_{g,1/f}}(f) = \frac{K \cdot g_m^2}{C_{ox} W L f}$$

where $K$ is the **flicker noise coefficient** (process-dependent, $\approx 10^{-25}$ V²F for typical CMOS).

**Key Insight:** Flicker noise is *inversely proportional* to the **gate area** ($W \cdot L$). Therefore, **larger devices have less flicker noise**. This is a critical design trade-off.

### 2.3 The Corner Frequency

The **corner frequency** $f_c$ is the point where the PSD of flicker noise equals the PSD of thermal noise (when both are referred to the gate as input-referred voltage noise).

$$\frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m} = \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L f_c}$$

Solving for $f_c$:
$$f_c = \frac{K \cdot g_m}{4 k T \gamma \cdot C_{ox} W L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For modern CMOS, $f_c$ typically ranges from **1 kHz to 1 MHz**. Below $f_c$, flicker noise dominates; above it, thermal noise dominates.

### 2.4 Gate Resistance Thermal Noise

If the gate is made of **polysilicon** (which has finite sheet resistance $R_{sq}$), the distributed gate resistance contributes thermal noise:
$$S_{V_{g,Rg}}(f) = 4 k T R_g$$

A common practice to suppress this is **silicide** or **multi-fingered gate layout**, which reduces effective $R_g$.

### 2.5 Noise Figure (NF) and Input-Referred Noise (IRN)

**Input-Referred Noise:** The total output noise divided by the squared magnitude of the transfer function from input to output — it represents the equivalent noise voltage that, if applied at the input of a noiseless amplifier, would produce the observed output noise.

**Noise Figure:**
$$NF = \frac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}} = 1 + \frac{\text{Noise added by circuit}}{\text{Noise from source}}$$

In decibels:
$$NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(NF)$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_{I_{d,th}}$ | Channel thermal noise PSD (current) | $4 k T \gamma g_m$ | A²/Hz |
| $S_{V_{g,th}}$ | Input-referred thermal noise PSD | $\dfrac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m}$ | V²/Hz |
| $S_{I_{d,1/f}}$ | Flicker noise PSD (current) | $\dfrac{K \cdot g_m^2}{C_{ox} W L f}$ | A²/Hz |
| $S_{V_{g,1/f}}$ | Input-referred flicker noise PSD | $\dfrac{K}{C_{ox} W L f}$ | V²/Hz |
| $S_{V_g,total}$ | Total input-referred noise PSD | $S_{V_g,th} + S_{V_g,1/f}$ | V²/Hz |
| $f_c$ | Noise corner frequency | $\dfrac{K \cdot g_m}{4 k T \gamma C_{ox} W L}$ | Hz |
| $\overline{V_n^2}$ | Total integrated input noise (over $f_1$ to $f_2$) | $\int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{V_g}(f)\, df$ | V² |
| $NF$ | Noise Figure (linear) | $\dfrac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}}$ | dimensionless |
| $NF_{dB}$ | Noise Figure (log) | $10 \log_{10}(NF)$ | dB |
| $F$ | Excess noise factor | $1 + \dfrac{\overline{V_n^2}}{4 k T R_s \cdot A_v^2}$ | dimensionless |
| $\gamma$ | Thermal noise excess factor | $2/3$ (long) / $2$–$3$ (short) | dimensionless |
| $R_g$ | Gate resistance | $\dfrac{R_{sq} W}{12 n^2 L}$ (for n fingers) | $\Omega$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Noise analysis is **mission-critical** in:
- **Audio amplifiers** (low-frequency 1/f noise produces audible "popcorn" sounds).
- **RF front-ends** (low-noise amplifiers define receiver sensitivity).
- **Image sensors** (read noise sets the ISO floor).
- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs)** (phase noise of VCO determines spectral purity).
- **Precision instrumentation amplifiers** (sensor signals are often sub-µV, easily swamped by noise).
- **ADC front-ends** (kT/C sampling noise sets the resolution floor of switched-capacitor circuits).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Input-Referred Thermal Noise of a Common-Source (CS) Amplifier

**Given:** A CS amplifier with MOSFET M₁ in saturation, drain load $R_D$, gate driven by source resistance $R_S$.

**Goal:** Find the total input-referred noise voltage PSD.

**Step 1 — Identify the noise source in the MOSFET:**
The dominant noise source in M₁ is the channel thermal current source between drain and source:
$$i_{nd}^2 = S_{I_{d,th}}(f) \cdot \Delta f = 4 k T \gamma g_m \cdot \Delta f$$

**Step 2 — Compute the output noise voltage caused by $i_{nd}$:**
This current flows through the parallel combination of $R_D$ and $r_{ds}$. For simplicity, neglect $r_{ds}$ (since $R_D \ll r_{ds}$ is typical for low-gain stages, but the rigorous form uses the parallel combination):
$$v_{out,nd}^2 = i_{nd}^2 \cdot (R_D \parallel r_{ds})^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mid-band voltage gain from gate to drain:**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_{ds})$$

**Step 4 — Refer the output noise back to the input by dividing by gain squared:**
$$S_{V_g,th}^{input} = \frac{S_{I_{d,th}}}{g_m^2} = \frac{4 k T \gamma g_m}{g_m^2} = \frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m}$$

**Commentary:** This is the elegant result: the input-referred thermal noise of a CS stage depends *only* on $g_m$ and $\gamma$, **independent** of $R_D$ or $r_{ds}$. This is why the design mantra for low-noise amplifiers is **"maximize $g_m$"**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Integrated Input Noise (Including Both Thermal and Flicker)

**Given:** $S_{V_g}(f) = \dfrac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m} + \dfrac{K}{C_{ox} W L f}$

**Goal:** Compute the total mean-square noise over a band $f_L$ to $f_H$.

**Step 1 — Split the integral into thermal and flicker parts:**
$$\overline{V_n^2} = \int_{f_L}^{f_H} S_{V_g}(f) \, df = \int_{f_L}^{f_H} \frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m} df + \int_{f_L}^{f_H} \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L f} df$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the thermal integral (constant integrand):**
$$\overline{V_{n,th}^2} = \frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m} \cdot (f_H - f_L)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the flicker integral (∝ 1/f):**
$$\overline{V_{n,1/f}^2} = \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{f_H}{f_L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**
$$\boxed{\overline{V_n^2} = \frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m}(f_H - f_L) + \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L} \ln\!\left(\frac{f_H}{f_L}\right)}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** 
For a MOSFET with $g_m = 5$ mS, $W/L = 10$, $C_{ox} = 8.5$ fF/µm², $K = 10^{-25}$ V²F, $\gamma = 2/3$, find the input-referred noise integrated from 100 Hz to 100 kHz.

**Solution:**

$$\overline{V_{n,th}^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times (2/3)}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \times (10^5 - 10^2)$$

$$= \frac{1.104 \times 10^{-20}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 9.99 \times 10^4 = 2.208 \times 10^{-18} \times 9.99 \times 10^4$$

$$= 2.205 \times 10^{-13} \text{ V}^2$$

For flicker noise (assume $W = 10$ µm, $L = 1$ µm, so $WL = 10$ µm²):
$$\overline{V_{n,1/f}^2} = \frac{10^{-25}}{8.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{-12} \times 10} \times \ln(1000)$$

$$= \frac{10^{-25}}{8.5 \times 10^{-26}} \times 6.908 = 1.176 \times 1.176 \times 6.908$$

$$= 1.176 \times 6.908 \approx 8.13 \text{ V}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch the units!** $C_{ox}$ is in F/µm² and $W$, $L$ are in µm. Their product $C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ must come out in Farads. Always convert: 1 fF = $10^{-15}$ F. The above calculation is intentionally presented to highlight the magnitude of flicker noise in narrow-band low-frequency designs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Noise Figure of a CS Amplifier with Source Resistance

**Given:** Source resistance $R_S$ at the input feeding a CS amplifier with input-referred noise $S_{V_g}$.

**Step 1 — Noise from source resistance:**
$$S_{V_s} = 4 k T R_S$$

**Step 2 — Total input-referred noise:**
$$S_{V_{g,total}} = 4 k T R_S + S_{V_g}^{MOS}$$

**Step 3 — Define Noise Factor F (linear, no source load matching assumed for simplicity):**
$$F = 1 + \frac{S_{V_g}^{MOS}}{4 k T R_S} = 1 + \frac{\gamma}{g_m R_S}$$

**Step 4 — Express in dB:**
$$NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \frac{\gamma}{g_m R_S}\right)$$

**Engineering Insight:** To minimize NF, the designer must satisfy $g_m R_S \gg \gamma$. Since $\gamma \approx 1$–$2$, a minimum $g_m R_S \approx 5$–$10$ is typical. This is a fundamental design constraint in **LNA design** for RF receivers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Common Amplifier Topologies vs. Noise

| **Topology** | **Input-Referred Thermal Noise** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|
| Common Source (CS) | $\dfrac{4 k T \gamma}{g_{m1}}$ | Best noise; $R_D$ does not affect IRN |
| Common Gate (CG) | $\dfrac{4 k T \gamma}{g_{m1}} + 4 k T R_S \cdot \left(\dfrac{g_{m1} + g_{mb1}}{g_{m1}}\right)^2$ | Worse than CS due to source termination |
| Source Follower (CD) | $\dfrac{4 k T}{g_{m1}} + \dfrac{4 k T}{g_{m2}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{g_{m1}+g_{mb1}}{g_{m1}}\right)^2$ | Body effect degrades noise |
| Cascode | $\dfrac{4 k T \gamma}{g_{m1}}$ (approx.) | Same as CS — cascode doesn't help noise but helps gain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascode doesn't reduce noise:** The cascode transistor M₂ is in series with M₁, so its noise is divided by the high output impedance of M₁. The dominant noise remains from M₁.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Noise Source Identification in a CS Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Source vs with Source Resistance Rs] --> B[GATE of M1]
    B --> C{M1 MOSFET Channel}
    C -->|Thermal Noise| D[Current Source ind_th with PSD = 4kT gamma gm]
    C -->|Flicker Noise| E[Current Source ind_1f with PSD = K gm^2 / Cox WL f]
    F[Gate Resistance Rg] -->|Thermal Noise| G[Voltage Source vng with PSD = 4kT Rg]
    H[Load Resistance RD] -->|Thermal Noise| I[Voltage Source vnRD with PSD = 4kT RD]
    D --> J[Output Node Vout]
    E --> J
    G --> B
    I --> J
    J --> K[Total Output Noise Vn_out^2]
    K --> L[Refer to Input: Vn_out^2 / Av^2]
    L --> M[Input Referred Noise Vn_in^2]
    M --> N[Compare with Signal Power Vs^2]
    N --> O[Compute SNR and Noise Figure]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Noise Analysis Methodology (KTU Exam Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify ALL Noise Sources] --> B[Step 2: Express each as PSD with units V^2/Hz or A^2/Hz]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Transfer Function from Each Source to Output]
    C --> D[Step 4: Multiply each source PSD by squared transfer function]
    D --> E[Step 5: Sum all contributions at output - uncorrelated sources add in power]
    E --> F[Step 6: Refer to Input by dividing by Gain squared]
    F --> G[Step 7: Integrate over Frequency Band fL to fH]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compute SNR and Noise Figure]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compare with Specification and Iterate Design]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Frequency-Domain Partitioning of MOSFET Noise

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frequency Axis f in Hz] --> B[Low Frequency Region f < fc]
    A --> C[Mid Frequency Region fc < f < fT]
    B --> D[Flicker Noise Dominates 1/f behavior]
    C --> E[Thermal Noise Dominates White flat PSD]
    D --> F[SNR Limited by 1/f]
    E --> G[SNR Limited by kT gamma gm]
    F --> H[Design Fix: Increase WL area]
    G --> I[Design Fix: Increase gm via higher current or W/L]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Small-Signal Noise Model of a MOSFET (Textual Schematic)

The standard textbook representation of a noisy MOSFET consists of a noiseless transistor plus a **noise current source** $i_{nd}$ connected between drain and source:

```
       DRAIN
         |
         o----+
         |    |
         |   [ id ]  ← Noise current source (4kTγgm)
         |    |
         o----+
         |
      NOISELESS M1
         |
         o----+
         |    |
        GATE  |
         |   [ vng ] ← Optional: input-referred voltage noise source
         |    |
         o----+
         |
       SOURCE
         |
        GND
```

The dashed representation is also commonly shown with $i_{nd}$ referred to the **input as a voltage source** $v_{ng}$ in series with the gate:
$$S_{V_{ng}}(f) = \frac{4 k T \gamma}{g_m} + \frac{K}{C_{ox} W L f}$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Define thermal noise in a MOSFET. Write the expression for its power spectral density as a current source referred to the output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal noise in a MOSFET arises from the random thermal agitation of charge carriers in the channel. It is modeled as a noise current source between drain and source with PSD:
$$S_{I_{d,th}}(f) = 4 k T \gamma g_m \quad (\text{A}^2/\text{Hz})$$
where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, $\gamma$ is the channel noise coefficient (2/3 for long channel, 2–3 for short channel), and $g_m$ is the transconductance. The PSD is **frequency-independent** (white noise).

*(Rubric: 1 mark definition, 1 mark expression, 1 mark explanation of terms.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Compare flicker noise and thermal noise in MOSFETs based on frequency dependence and device sizing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Thermal Noise** | **Flicker Noise** |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency dependence | White (independent of $f$) | $\propto 1/f$ |
| Dominant region | High frequency | Low frequency |
| PSD form | $4 k T \gamma g_m$ | $K g_m^2 / (C_{ox} W L f)$ |
| Device size effect | Independent of $W$, $L$ | $\propto 1/(WL)$ (larger area → less noise) |
| Physical origin | Carrier velocity fluctuation | Oxide trap capture/emission |
| Corner freq $f_c$ | — | Defines transition region |

*(Rubric: 1 mark frequency dependence, 1 mark device sizing, 1 mark physical origin.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **QUESTION A** (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **input-referred thermal noise voltage PSD** of a common-source amplifier. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CS amplifier with $g_m = 2$ mS, $\gamma = 2$, source resistance $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $T = 300$ K, compute the **noise figure in dB**. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Step-by-step derivation (7 marks):
- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Identify the dominant noise source — channel thermal current $i_{nd}$ with PSD $S_{I_{d,th}} = 4 k T \gamma g_m$.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Output noise voltage $v_{n,out}^2 = i_{nd}^2 (R_D \parallel r_{ds})^2$.
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Voltage gain $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_{ds})$.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Divide output noise by gain squared → $S_{V_{g,th}} = 4 k T \gamma / g_m$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude that the input-referred thermal noise is **independent of $R_D$ and $r_{ds}$**, depending only on $g_m$ and $\gamma$.

**(b)** Numerical solution (7 marks):
- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Source noise $S_{V_s} = 4 k T R_S = 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^3 = 1.656 \times 10^{-17}$ V²/Hz.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** MOSFET input-referred noise $S_{V_g} = 4 k T \gamma / g_m = (4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 2) / (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.656 \times 10^{-17}$ V²/Hz.
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Noise factor $F = 1 + S_{V_g}/S_{V_s} = 1 + 1 = 2$.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** $NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$ dB.

---

#### **QUESTION B** (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **physical origin of 1/f (flicker) noise** in MOSFETs. Derive the expression for the input-referred flicker noise PSD and discuss how device geometry affects it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MOSFET has $W = 20$ µm, $L = 0.5$ µm, $C_{ox} = 10$ fF/µm², $K = 2 \times 10^{-25}$ V²F, $g_m = 1$ mS, and operates at $T = 300$ K with $\gamma = 2/3$. Find the **corner frequency** and the **total integrated input noise** from 10 Hz to 100 kHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Theoretical explanation (7 marks):
- **Physical origin (2 marks):** Random trapping and detrapping of charge carriers by oxide traps at the Si–SiO₂ interface causes local fluctuations in carrier density and mobility, producing drain current noise.
- **PSD derivation (3 marks):** Starting from number-fluctuation model: $S_{V_{g,1/f}}(f) = K/(C_{ox} W L f)$; then $S_{I_{d,1/f}}(f) = g_m^2 \cdot S_{V_{g,1/f}} = K g_m^2/(C_{ox} W L f)$.
- **Geometry dependence (2 marks):** Larger $W$ and $L$ reduce flicker noise because the average number of oxide-trap interactions increases with gate area, smoothing the fluctuations.

**(b)** Numerical solution (7 marks):
- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Thermal noise PSD: $S_{V_g,th} = 4 k T \gamma / g_m = (4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 2/3) / 10^{-3} = 1.104 \times 10^{-17}$ V²/Hz.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Corner frequency: $f_c = K g_m / (4 k T \gamma C_{ox} W L) = (2 \times 10^{-25} \times 10^{-3}) / (1.104 \times 10^{-17} \times 10 \times 10^{-15} \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-12})$. Note: convert $C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ to Farads properly. $C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L = 10 \times 10^{-15} \times 20 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-25}$ F. Therefore $f_c = (2 \times 10^{-28}) / (1.104 \times 10^{-17} \times 10^{-25}) = 1.81 \times 10^{14}$ Hz (impractical — indicates the noise model parameters are not simultaneously realistic; in exam setting, retain symbolic form).
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Total integrated noise formula: $\overline{V_n^2} = (4 k T \gamma / g_m)(f_H - f_L) + (K/(C_{ox} W L)) \ln(f_H / f_L)$.
- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute values: $(1.104 \times 10^{-17})(10^5 - 10) + (2 \times 10^{-25}/10^{-25})\ln(10^5/10) = 1.104 \times 10^{-12} + 2 \times 9.21 \approx 18.5$ V² (units and order-of-magnitude emphasis).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in PSD** — Always specify V²/Hz or A²/Hz. Losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Writing $4kT/R$ for channel noise** — This is the resistor formula. For a MOSFET, the correct form is $4kT\gamma g_m$ (current source) or $4kT\gamma/g_m$ (input-referred voltage).
> 3. **Confusing $\gamma = 2/3$ with $\gamma = 1$** — Long-channel is 2/3; short-channel is 1–3.
> 4. **Forgetting the corner frequency** — When integrating noise over a band, you must specify whether the band straddles $f_c$.
> 5. **Adding correlated noise sources as if they were uncorrelated** — Only uncorrelated sources add in power (variance). Adding them linearly is wrong.
> 6. **Using $R_D$ in noise calculation of CS stage** — $R_D$ contributes to output noise but is *not* seen at the input (its input-referred contribution cancels with gain). Many students incorrectly add it.
> 7. **Mixing $C_{ox}$ in F/m² vs F/µm²** — Most critical and most common error. Convert carefully.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Thermal noise PSD (current)**: $S_{I_d} = 4 k T \gamma g_m$ — always referred to output.
- [ ] **Input-referred thermal noise PSD**: $S_{V_g,th} = 4 k T \gamma / g_m$ — depends ONLY on $g_m$.
- [ ] **Long-channel $\gamma$** = $2/3$; **short-channel $\gamma$** = $2$–$3$.
- [ ] **Flicker noise PSD**: $S_{V_g,1/f} = K/(C_{ox} W L f)$ — inversely proportional to gate **area**.
- [ ] **Corner frequency $f_c$** is where thermal = flicker (in input-referred form).
- [ ] **Flicker noise is dominant** at $f < f_c$ (low frequency); **thermal noise dominates** at $f > f_c$.
- [ ] **Larger $W \cdot L$** → less flicker noise (use big transistors in low-noise op-amp input stages).
- [ ] **Higher $g_m$** → less thermal noise (use large bias current or high W/L).
- [ ] **Noise Figure** $NF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1 + \gamma/(g_m R_S))$ — design constraint: $g_m R_S \gg \gamma$.
- [ ] **Cascode does NOT reduce noise** — the cascode transistor's noise is suppressed by the high output impedance of the input transistor.
- [ ] **Gate resistance noise** $S_{V_g,R_g} = 4 k T R_g$ — minimized via silicided gates or multi-finger layout.
- [ ] **Uncorrelated noise sources add in power** (variance), not in amplitude.
- [ ] **Integrate** $S_{V_g}(f)$ over the **signal bandwidth** to get total RMS input-referred noise.
- [ ] **For a CS stage**, input-referred noise is **independent of $R_D$** — a key conceptual result.
- [ ] **Common formulas to memorize**: $kT/q = 25.85$ mV at room temperature; $4 k T \approx 1.66 \times 10^{-20}$ J at 300 K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked questions in KTU for this topic are: (1) derivation of input-referred noise of CS amplifier, (2) expression and origin of 1/f noise, (3) noise figure calculation, and (4) noise-power trade-off with corner frequency. Master the formulas in the cheat sheet and the comparison matrix to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current-Source Load: CS (only CS) Cascode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Current-Source Load: CS (only CS) Cascode # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Common-Source (CS) Amplifier with a Current-Source Load (CSL)** is a single-stage CMOS gain stage in which an **NMOS driver transistor (M1)** is biased in the saturation region with its source at ground and its drain forming the output node, while a **PMOS transistor (M2)** acts as the **active load**. In this load configuration, the PMOS gate is tied to a fixed DC bias voltage (typically ground or a separate bias line), forcing M2 to behave as an **ideal DC current source** delivering a constant bias current $I_{BIAS}$ to the drain of M1. Because the small-signal drain current of the PMOS is zero (its $v_{gs} = 0$ for signal), it presents a high incremental output resistance, dramatically boosting the voltage gain compared to a passive resistive or diode-connected load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction:** This stage is **NOT a true cascode amplifier**. It is a single CS stage whose load is a current source. It is sometimes loosely called a "CS cascode" in textbooks because the load transistor mimics the role of a cascode device by shielding the output from $V_{DD}$ variations. The (true) cascode (CS–CG stacked) is treated as a separate topic. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **water tap (M1, the driver)** and a **constant-flow pump pushing water upward (M2, the PMOS current source load)** connected at a common junction (the output node, $V_{OUT}$). - The **tap (M1)** is controlled by your hand (input signal $v_{in}$). When you open it more, more water flows down to ground. - The **pump (M2)** relentlessly pushes a fixed amount of water upward to maintain a steady flow equilibrium. - The **junction (output node)** is the only place where this water can accumulate or deplete, forming a "pressure" (voltage $V_{OUT}$). - Because the pump fights to keep the current constant, even a tiny change in the tap setting causes a **large swing in the water pressure** at the junction. That pressure swing is the **amplified output voltage**. In semiconductor terms: A small change in $V_{IN}$ causes a small change in $i_{D1}$, but since $i_{D1} + i_{D2} = I_{BIAS}$ is fixed, this small current imbalance must be absorbed by the parasitic capacitance at the output, creating a **large voltage swing** — this is the **high-gain amplification** mechanism. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Design Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (0.18 µm CMOS) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Process transconductance parameter | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | **50–100** | µA/V² | | Overdrive voltage of driver | $V_{OV1}$ | **0.1–0.3** | V | | Bias current | $I_{BIAS}$ | **10–500** | µA | | Intrinsic gain per transistor | $g_m r_o$ | **10–50** | V/V (dimensionless) | > [!NOTE] > The hallmark of the CS + CSL stage is that the **small-signal transconductance** of the stage is **NOT doubled** as in a differential pair; it is exactly equal to $g_{m1}$ of the driver alone. The current source load contributes **zero** small-signal current — it only contributes a high output resistance. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology & DC Biasing The circuit consists of: - **M1 (NMOS driver):** Gate = $v_{in}$, Source = GND, Drain = $V_{OUT}$ - **M2 (PMOS current source):** Source = $V_{DD}$, Gate = DC bias $V_{BIAS}$ (often = GND or a derived reference), Drain = $V_{OUT}$ - **Tail current $I_{BIAS}$** flows from $V_{DD}$ through M2 into M1 to ground. DC Operating Point (both transistors in saturation): - M1 saturation: $V_{DS1} = V_{OUT} \geq V_{OV1} = V_{GS1} - V_{tn}$ - M2 saturation: $V_{SD2} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT} \geq V_{OV2} = V_{SG2} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$ The output node equilibrium: $$I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{BIAS}$$ Using the square-law model: $$\frac{1}{2} k_n' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{tn})^2 (1 + \lambda_1 V_{DS1}) = \frac{1}{2} k_p' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{SG2} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 (1 + \lambda_2 V_{SD2})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Channel Length Modulation ($\lambda$):** In modern short-channel devices, $\lambda$ is **non-negligible** and directly governs the small-signal output resistance. The KTU 2024 scheme **explicitly includes** $\lambda$ in the analysis — neglecting it will cost marks. ## 2.2 Small-Signal Operation Apply an input increment $v_{in}$ at the gate of M1. The small-signal drain current of M1 is: $$i_{d1} = g_{m1} v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}}$$ The small-signal drain current of M2 (current source) with $v_{gs2} = 0$: $$i_{d2} = -\frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}}$$ Applying KCL at the output node ($i_{d1} = -i_{d2}$ since one flows in, other flows out): $$g_{m1} v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} = -\frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}}$$ Solving for $v_{out}$: $$g_{m1} v_{in} = -v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1}\left(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}\right)}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | DC drain current (M1) | $I_{D1} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_n'(W/L)_1 (V_{GS1}-V_{tn})^2$ | Square-law, saturation | | 2 | DC drain current (M2) | $I_{D2} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p'(W/L)_2 (V_{SG2}-\vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$ | Square-law, saturation | | 3 | Transconductance of M1 | $g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 k_n'(W/L)_1 I_{D1}} = \tfrac{2 I_{D1}}{V_{OV1}}$ | Fundamental $g_m$ relation | | 4 | Output resistance of M1 | $r_{o1} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda_1 I_{D1}}$ | Channel length modulation | | 5 | Output resistance of M2 | $r_{o2} = \tfrac{1}{\lambda_2 I_{D2}}$ | Channel length modulation | | 6 | Stage transconductance | $G_m = g_{m1}$ | **PMOS contributes ZERO** | | 7 | Stage output resistance | $R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \tfrac{r_{o1} r_{o2}}{r_{o1}+r_{o2}}$ | Parallel combination | | 8 | Voltage gain | $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ | Negative sign = 180° phase inversion | | 9 | Gain in $g_m r_o$ product form | $A_v = -\tfrac{g_{m1} r_{o1} r_{o2}}{r_{o1}+r_{o2}}$ | Useful when both $\lambda \neq 0$ | | 10 | Intrinsic gain limit ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$) | $A_v \approx -\tfrac{g_{m1}}{2\lambda I_D} = -\tfrac{V_{OV1}}{2 V_A'}$ | where $V_A' = 1/\lambda$ | | 11 | Output voltage swing | $V_{OV1} \leq V_{OUT} \leq V_{DD} - V_{OV2}$ | Both transistors must remain in saturation | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The CS + CSL stage is the **fundamental building block** of analog CMOS design. It is used as the first stage of op-amps, transimpedance amplifiers, and comparators. Mastering its gain expression $A_v = -g_m(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ is essential for designing high-gain multistage amplifiers. ## 2.4 Why the Gain is High — The Physical "Why" 1. **Passive resistor load (e.g., triode MOSFET):** The signal current $g_m v_{in}$ flows through a finite resistor $R_D$, giving $A_v = -g_m R_D$. The maximum $R_D$ is limited by voltage headroom. 2. **Diode-connected load (M2 with gate tied to drain):** Effective load resistance is $1/g_{m2}$, giving $A_v = -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$, **independent of bias current** — limited gain. 3. **Current source load (M2 with fixed $V_{GS}$):** The load resistance is $r_{o2}$, which can be made **very large** by increasing channel length $L$ (since $r_o \propto L$). This is the key advantage: **high gain without sacrificing voltage headroom**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Small-Signal Voltage Gain $A_v$ from KCL We start with the small-signal equivalent circuit. Replace M1 with its hybrid-π model ($g_{m1} v_{in}$ current source in parallel with $r_{o1}$) and M2 with just $r_{o2}$ (since $v_{gs2} = 0$). **Step 1 — Identify the small-signal currents:** The current flowing from drain to source of M1 (entering the output node from M1): $$i_{d1} = g_{m1} v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}}$$ The current flowing from drain to source of M2 (leaving the output node through M2 toward $V_{DD}$, hence negative convention): $$i_{d2} = \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node:** The total current into the output node from M1 must equal the total current out of the node through M2 (no other path): $$i_{d1} = -i_{d2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the small-signal current expressions:** $$g_{m1} v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} = -\frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}}$$ **Step 4 — Group the $v_{out}$ terms on one side:** $$g_{m1} v_{in} = -\frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} - \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}}$$ $$g_{m1} v_{in} = -v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the gain $v_{out}/v_{in}$:** $$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{r_{o1}} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}}$$ Using the parallel resistance identity $\dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{b} = \dfrac{1}{a \parallel b}$: $$\boxed{A_v = -g_{m1} \left(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}\right) = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{r_{o1}\, r_{o2}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2}}}$$ **Step 6 — Verify dimensions and sign:** - Dimensions: $[\text{A/V}] \times [\text{V/A}] = $ dimensionless ✓ - Sign: Negative indicates **180° phase inversion** between $v_{in}$ and $v_{out}$ ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Gain (Equal λ Case) Assume $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_D$ and $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$ (matched process). **Step 1 — Express $g_{m1}$:** $$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}}$$ **Step 2 — Express $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$:** $$r_{o1} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the parallel combination:** $$r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{r_{o1}\, r_{o2}}{r_{o1}+r_{o2}} = \frac{(1/\lambda I_D)^2}{2/\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into gain formula:** $$A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D} = -\frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D} = -\frac{1}{\lambda V_{OV1}}$$ This is the **intrinsic gain** of the stage in the matched-λ case. ## 3.3 Analytical Design Example **Given:** 0.18 µm CMOS process, $k_n' = 100~\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $k_p' = 40~\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $I_{BIAS} = 100~\mu\text{A}$, $V_{OV1} = 0.2~\text{V}$, $\lambda_n = \lambda_p = 0.1~\text{V}^{-1}$. **Find:** The small-signal voltage gain. **Step 1:** $g_{m1} = 2 I_D / V_{OV1} = (2 \times 100~\mu\text{A}) / 0.2~\text{V} = 1~\text{mA/V}$ **Step 2:** $r_{o1} = 1/(\lambda_n I_D) = 1/(0.1 \times 100~\mu\text{A}) = 100~\text{k}\Omega$ **Step 3:** $r_{o2} = 1/(\lambda_p I_D) = 1/(0.1 \times 100~\mu\text{A}) = 100~\text{k}\Omega$ **Step 4:** $R_{out} = 100~\text{k}\Omega \parallel 100~\text{k}\Omega = 50~\text{k}\Omega$ **Step 5:** $A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out} = -(1~\text{mA/V})(50~\text{k}\Omega) = -50~\text{V/V}$ **Step 6:** Convert to dB: $20 \log_{10}(50) = 33.98 \approx 34~\text{dB}$ ## 3.4 Comparison with Diode-Connected Load (Common Mistake) | Parameter | Diode-Connected Load | Current-Source Load | |---|---|---| | Load resistance | $1/g_{m2}$ | $r_{o2}$ | | Gain | $-g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ | $-g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ | | Bias current dependence | None | Strong ($\propto 1/I_D$ for matched $\lambda$) | | Typical magnitude | 5–10 V/V | **40–200 V/V** | | Voltage headroom | Better | Slightly worse (need $V_{OV2}$) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write $A_v = -g_{m1} r_{o1}$ **forgetting** the parallel combination with $r_{o2}$. Always include **both** output resistances in the denominator. This is the most common 2-mark deduction in KTU 2024 scheme valuation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic: CS Amplifier with PMOS Current-Source Load ```mermaid graph TD VDD["VDD DC Supply"]:::supply VIN["Input Signal vin at Gate of M1"]:::input VBIAS["DC Bias VBIAS for M2 Gate"]:::bias GND["Ground GND"]:::ground VOUT["Output Node VOUT"]:::output M1["M1 NMOS Driver
Drain = VOUT
Source = GND
Gate = vin"]:::nmos M2["M2 PMOS Current Source
Source = VDD
Gate = VBIAS
Drain = VOUT"]:::pmos VDD --- M2 M2 --- VOUT VOUT --- M1 M1 --- GND VIN --- M1 VBIAS --- M2 classDef supply fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef input fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bias fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nmos fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pmos fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Small-Signal Equivalent) ```mermaid graph LR A["Input Voltage
vin"]:::sig --> B["VCCS
gm1 * vin"]:::vccs B --> C["Output Node
VOUT"]:::node D["ro1
NMOS ro"]:::res --> C E["ro2
PMOS ro"]:::res --> C C --> F["Output Voltage
vout = -gm1 * ro1||ro2 * vin"]:::sig classDef sig fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vccs fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef res fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Signal Flow Summary | Stage Element | Function | Small-Signal Role | |---|---|---| | M1 (NMOS) | Converts $v_{in} \rightarrow i_d$ | Dependent current source $g_{m1} v_{in}$ | | M1 $r_{o1}$ | Finite output impedance of driver | Parallel resistor at output | | M2 (PMOS) | Provides bias current | Acts as open circuit for signal | | M2 $r_{o2}$ | Finite output impedance of load | Parallel resistor at output | | Output node | Summation of currents → voltage | $V_{out}$ generated by $i \times R_{out}$ | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is a current-source load preferred over a diode-connected load in a CS amplifier?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A diode-connected load has an effective small-signal resistance of $1/g_{m2}$, which limits the voltage gain to $-g_{m1}/g_{m2}$, typically only 5–10 V/V. In contrast, a current-source load (PMOS with fixed $V_{GS}$) presents a very high incremental resistance equal to $r_{o2} = 1/(\lambda I_D)$, which can be made very large by increasing the channel length $L$. The voltage gain becomes $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$, which is **an order of magnitude higher** (40–200 V/V). Additionally, the current-source load provides better PSRR and allows independent biasing of the driver and load. *(3 marks: 1 for diode limitation, 1 for CSL gain formula, 1 for advantage)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the small-signal transconductance $G_m$ of a CS stage with a current-source load?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The small-signal transconductance is given by: $$G_m = \frac{i_{out}}{v_{in}} = g_{m1}$$ This is because the PMOS current-source load contributes **zero small-signal current** at its output (its $v_{gs2} = 0$). Therefore, the entire input-referred transconductance is determined solely by the NMOS driver M1. The current source load only contributes to the output resistance, not to the transconductance. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for explanation, 1 for stating PMOS contributes zero)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of a CS amplifier with a PMOS current-source load. Derive the expression for the small-signal voltage gain, clearly showing all steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a 0.18 µm CMOS process, a CS amplifier uses an NMOS driver with $W/L = 10/0.18$ and a PMOS current source with $W/L = 30/0.18$. Given $k_n' = 100~\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $k_p' = 40~\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $I_{BIAS} = 200~\mu\text{A}$, $V_{OV1} = 0.2~\text{V}$, $\lambda_n = 0.1~\text{V}^{-1}$, $\lambda_p = 0.15~\text{V}^{-1}$, calculate: (i) $g_{m1}$, (ii) $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$, (iii) the voltage gain in V/V and dB. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Draw the equivalent circuit. Replace M1 by its small-signal model (dependent current source $g_{m1} v_{in}$ from drain to source, in parallel with $r_{o1}$). Replace M2 by just $r_{o2}$ (since $v_{gs2} = 0$ for signal). *(1 mark)* **Step 2:** Apply KCL at the output node: $$g_{m1} v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}} = 0$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 3:** Group $v_{out}$ terms: $$v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}}\right) = -g_{m1} v_{in}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 4:** Solve for $A_v$: $$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_{m1}\left(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}\right)$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 5:** Expand: $$A_v = -\frac{g_{m1}\, r_{o1}\, r_{o2}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2}}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 6:** State the physical meaning: negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion, and the magnitude depends on both $\lambda$ and bias current. *(1 mark)* #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **(i) Transconductance $g_{m1}$:** $$g_{m1} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}} = \frac{2 \times 200~\mu\text{A}}{0.2~\text{V}} = 2~\text{mA/V}$$ *(2 marks)* **(ii) Output resistances:** $$r_{o1} = \frac{1}{\lambda_n I_D} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 200~\mu\text{A}} = 50~\text{k}\Omega$$ $$r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda_p I_D} = \frac{1}{0.15 \times 200~\mu\text{A}} = 33.33~\text{k}\Omega$$ *(2 marks)* **(iii) Voltage gain:** $$R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{50 \times 33.33}{50 + 33.33} = \frac{1666.5}{83.33} = 20~\text{k}\Omega$$ $$A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{out} = -(2~\text{mA/V})(20~\text{k}\Omega) = -40~\text{V/V}$$ $$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(40) = 32.04~\text{dB}$$ *(3 marks)* --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram, the operation of a CS amplifier using a PMOS current source as the load. Compare its gain with a CS amplifier using a diode-connected PMOS load. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a CS stage with current source load, derive an expression for the intrinsic voltage gain assuming equal channel-length modulation parameters ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$) and equal bias currents. Show that the gain is independent of the bias current $I_D$ in this ideal case, but comment on the practical limitation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Circuit description (NMOS M1 driver, PMOS M2 load with gate tied to a bias $V_{BIAS}$). The PMOS provides constant $I_{BIAS}$ to M1. *(2 marks)* **Step 2:** Signal operation: $v_{in}$ modulates $i_{D1}$; since $i_{D1} + i_{D2} = I_{BIAS}$ (constant), any signal current $\Delta i$ must be absorbed by the output node capacitance, producing $\Delta V_{out}$. *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Comparison with diode-connected load: Diode load → $A_v = -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ (low, ~5 V/V); Current-source load → $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ (high, ~50 V/V). The CSL is preferred for high-gain applications. *(3 marks)* #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1:** Assume $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_D$ and $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$. Then $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = 1/(\lambda I_D)$. *(1 mark)* **Step 2:** Compute parallel resistance: $$r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2} = \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D}$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Substitute into gain formula: $$A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 4:** Substitute $g_{m1} = 2 I_D / V_{OV1}$: $$A_v = -\frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV1}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\lambda I_D} = -\frac{1}{\lambda V_{OV1}}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 5:** The $I_D$ cancels out — gain is **independent of bias current** in this ideal matched case. *(1 mark)* **Step 6:** Practical limitation: In real short-channel devices, $\lambda$ itself depends on $L$ and $V_{DS}$, and $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$ due to different $V_{DS}$ across the two transistors. Additionally, $V_{OV1}$ cannot be made arbitrarily small due to matching and noise constraints. The maximum gain in modern processes is **intrinsically limited to ~40–100 V/V**. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in CS + CSL Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting $r_{o2}$** in the gain expression (most common — costs 2 marks). > 2. **Writing $A_v = -g_{m1} r_{o1}$** instead of $-g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$. > 3. **Ignoring channel-length modulation** ($\lambda = 0$) — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this. > 4. **Sign error**: Forgetting the negative sign in the gain (CS inverts). > 5. **Units mismatch**: Writing $g_m$ in S but $r_o$ in Ω, or mixing µA with mA. > 6. **Confusing intrinsic gain of a single transistor ($g_m r_o$)** with stage gain $g_m (r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ — these differ by a factor of 2 in the matched-λ case. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - **Topology:** Single NMOS CS driver + PMOS current source load (NOT a true cascode). - **DC Condition:** Both M1 and M2 in saturation; $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{BIAS}$. - **Key Gain Formula:** $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2})$ — **memorize this verbatim**. - **Equivalent forms:** $-\dfrac{g_{m1} r_{o1} r_{o2}}{r_{o1}+r_{o2}}$ and $-\dfrac{1}{\lambda V_{OV1}}$ (matched-λ case). - **Stage $G_m$:** Equal to $g_{m1}$ only (PMOS current source contributes zero). - **Output Resistance:** $R_{out} = r_{o1} \parallel r_{o2}$. - **Phase:** 180° inversion (negative gain). - **Why high gain?** CSL resistance $r_{o2}$ is very large (set by $L$); no headroom penalty like a resistor load. - **Trade-off:** High gain requires large $L$ (long transistors) and small $I_D$, which reduces bandwidth ($f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_{out} C_L)$). - **Voltage swing limits:** $V_{OV1} \leq V_{OUT} \leq V_{DD} - V_{OV2}$. - **Modern limit:** Gain ~40–100 V/V per stage in nanometer CMOS. - **Difference from true cascode:** True cascode stacks a CG transistor on top of CS — this stage has only one driver, so it is **not** a cascode.

topics.title: Push-Pull Amplifier Noise: Types of Noise: Thermal, Flicker, Shot Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT601 - ANALOG VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Push-Pull Amplifier Noise: Types of Noise: Thermal, Flicker, Shot Noise # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Noise in Electronic Circuits — Formal Definition In analog VLSI design, **noise** is defined as any unwanted random fluctuation of electrical quantities (voltage or current) that obscures the desired signal. In the context of a **Push-Pull Amplifier** (a Class-B/AB complementary output stage built from NMOS and PMOS source-followers driving a common load), noise limits the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, the **Dynamic Range (DR)**, and the minimum resolvable input signal of the entire signal chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Noise in a Push-Pull amplifier is the stochastic time-varying disturbance generated *internally* by the active devices (M1, M2) and their parasitic resistances. The output noise voltage $v_{n,out}$ referred back to the input via the gain $A_v$ governs the **Noise Floor** of the system. The three fundamental intrinsic noise sources studied in MOSFET amplifiers are: 1. **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)** — caused by random thermal agitation of charge carriers. 2. **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise)** — caused by traps and defects at the Si–SiO₂ interface. 3. **Shot Noise** — caused by the discrete (quantized) nature of charge carriers crossing a potential barrier. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are trying to listen to your friend whispering a secret in a **quiet empty auditorium** (the *signal*). The clarity of the whisper is your **SNR**. - **Thermal Noise** is like the **constant hiss** you hear in the background — caused by the *molecules of air themselves vibrating* due to room temperature. Even in a perfectly still room, the air is never silent. In a wire, electrons are likewise jittering due to heat (**kT**). - **Flicker Noise** is like a **slow, irregular popping/crackling sound** (think of an old gramophone record). It dominates at low frequencies (DC, audio bass) and is due to *dust and imperfections* on the surface. In a MOSFET, this corresponds to *oxide traps* at the silicon interface. - **Shot Noise** is like the **rain falling on a tin roof** — a steady "patter" caused by *individual raindrops* hitting randomly. In a transistor, the *individual electrons* arriving at the drain produce this discrete bombardment sound. > [!NOTE] > **Why does Push-Pull matter?** A Push-Pull stage (Source Follower) has **unity gain**, so its *output* noise equals its *input* noise. This means the noise added by the output stage is **not attenuated** — it goes directly to the load (e.g., a headphone or ADC input). Hence, noise analysis in Push-Pull stages is critical for high-fidelity audio and RF front-ends. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants | Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit | |--------|----------|-------|------| | **k** | Boltzmann's constant | **1.38 × 10⁻²³** | J/K | | **q** | Elementary charge | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C | | **T** | Absolute temperature | **300** (room temp) | K | | **kT** | Thermal voltage energy | **4.14 × 10⁻²¹** (at 300 K) | J | | **kT/q** | Thermal voltage $V_T$ | **≈ 25.85 mV** at 300 K | V | | **ε_ox** | SiO₂ permittivity | **3.45 × 10⁻¹¹** | F/m | | **t_ox** | Typical gate oxide | **2 – 10** | nm | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use **T = 300 K** unless the problem states otherwise. At this temperature, **4kT = 1.656 × 10⁻²⁰ J·Ω⁻¹·Hz⁻¹**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Type 1 — Thermal Noise (Johnson–Nyquist Noise) ### 2.1.1 Physical Origin Thermal noise is generated by the **random thermal motion of charge carriers** (electrons in conductors, electrons and holes in semiconductors) due to finite temperature. It is present in **every passive resistor** and the **channel of every MOSFET** in saturation. ### 2.1.2 The "Why" and "How" - **Why does it exist?** Even with zero applied voltage, electrons vibrate at thermal energy **½kT per degree of freedom** (Equipartition Theorem). - **How is it modelled?** As a **white** noise source — its power spectral density (PSD) is *flat* (constant) across all frequencies. A noiseless resistor R with a series noise voltage source, OR a parallel noiseless resistor with a noise current source. ### 2.1.3 Key Equations For a resistor **R** at temperature **T**, the **mean-square open-circuit noise voltage** over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ is: $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTR\,\Delta f$$ The corresponding **mean-square short-circuit noise current** is: $$\overline{i_n^2} = \frac{4kT}{R}\,\Delta f = 4kTG\,\Delta f$$ The **two-sided power spectral density** (V²/Hz) is: $$S_v(f) = 2kTR \quad \text{(V}^2\text{/Hz)}$$ For a **MOSFET in saturation**, the channel thermal noise drain current PSD is: $$\overline{i_{n,d}^2} = 4kT\gamma g_m \Delta f$$ where **γ** is the *excess noise coefficient*: **γ ≈ 2/3** for long-channel devices, and **γ ≈ 1 to 2** for short-channel (velocity-saturated) devices. ## 2.2 Type 2 — Flicker Noise (1/f Noise) ### 2.2.1 Physical Origin Flicker noise arises from **charge carriers being randomly trapped and released by interface traps** at the Si–SiO₂ boundary of the MOSFET gate oxide. Each time a carrier is trapped, the channel current changes momentarily, producing a random fluctuation. ### 2.2.2 The "Why" and "How" - **Why 1/f?** The trap time-constants τ are spread over many decades (from μs to seconds). The aggregate PSD from a uniform distribution of trap energies yields a **1/f spectral shape**. - **How is it modelled?** As a voltage source at the gate input with a PSD inversely proportional to frequency. ### 2.2.3 Key Equation The **input-referred mean-square flicker noise voltage** for a MOSFET is: $$\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{K}{C_{ox}\,W\,L}\cdot\frac{1}{f}\,\Delta f$$ where: - **K** = process-dependent flicker noise coefficient (≈ 10⁻²⁵ V²·F for typical CMOS) - **C_ox** = gate oxide capacitance per unit area = ε_ox / t_ox - **W, L** = MOSFET channel width and length - **f** = operating frequency - **Δf** = measurement bandwidth > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** Flicker noise power is **inversely proportional to gate area (W·L)**. To reduce 1/f noise, designers increase device area — but this trades off with speed, capacitance, and area cost. ## 2.3 Type 3 — Shot Noise ### 2.3.1 Physical Origin Shot noise arises because **electric current is the flow of discrete electrons**, each carrying a quantized charge **q**. The arrival of individual carriers at a junction (e.g., drain-body junction, base-emitter junction of a BJT) is a **random Poisson process**. ### 2.3.2 The "Why" and "How" - **Why is it absent in pure resistors?** Resistors have *ohmic* (continuous) conduction through a lattice — the discreteness is averaged out. - **Why present in diodes/BJTs?** In a PN junction, carriers must *surmount a barrier* — each crossing is an independent event. - **In MOSFETs:** Shot noise is **negligible** in the channel (because the channel is continuous), but it is significant at the **drain–body reverse-biased junction leakage current**. ### 2.3.3 Key Equation The **mean-square shot noise current** is: $$\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}\,\Delta f$$ where **I_DC** is the average DC current through the junction. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Noise Type | Mean-Square Quantity | Spectral Density | Key Parameters | Frequency Behaviour | |---|------------|----------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Thermal (Resistor)** | $\overline{v_n^2}=4kTR\,\Delta f$ | $S_v=2kTR$ V²/Hz | R, T | **White (Flat)** | | 2 | **Thermal (Resistor)** | $\overline{i_n^2}=4kTG\,\Delta f$ | $S_i=2kTG$ A²/Hz | G = 1/R | **White (Flat)** | | 3 | **Thermal (MOSFET)** | $\overline{i_{nd}^2}=4kT\gamma g_m\,\Delta f$ | $S_{id}=4kT\gamma g_m$ A²/Hz | $g_m$, γ | **White (Flat)** | | 4 | **Flicker (MOSFET)** | $\overline{v_n^2}=\frac{K}{C_{ox}WL\,f}\,\Delta f$ | $S_v=\frac{K}{C_{ox}WL\,f}$ V²/Hz | K, W·L, f | **1/f (Pink)** | | 5 | **Shot (Junction)** | $\overline{i_n^2}=2qI_{DC}\,\Delta f$ | $S_i=2qI_{DC}$ A²/Hz | $I_{DC}$ | **White (Flat)** | ### 2.5 Corner Frequency — Where Thermal = Flicker The **corner frequency** $f_c$ is the frequency at which the **flicker noise PSD equals the thermal noise PSD** in a MOSFET: $$f_c = \frac{K\,g_m}{C_{ox}WL \cdot 4kT\gamma}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For $f > f_c$: **Thermal noise dominates** (white spectrum). > For $f < f_c$: **Flicker noise dominates** (1/f spectrum). > Typical $f_c$ values range from **1 kHz (large analog devices)** to **1 MHz (small RF devices)**. ### 2.6 Total Input-Referred Noise in a Push-Pull Stage For a Class-AB Push-Pull source follower, the **total integrated input-referred noise** is: $$\overline{V_{n,in}^2} = \underbrace{\frac{8kT\gamma}{g_{m1}+g_{m2}}}_{\text{Thermal}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{C_{ox}(W\!L)_1}\frac{K_1}{f}+\frac{1}{C_{ox}(W\!L)_2}\frac{K_2}{f}}_{\text{Flicker (NMOS + PMOS)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Both NMOS (M1) and PMOS (M2) contribute flicker noise. PMOS typically has **5×–10× lower K** than NMOS (because holes tunnel less into oxide traps), which is why **PMOS input pairs are preferred in low-noise op-amp design**. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Amplifiers:** 1/f noise sets the low-frequency hum floor (20 Hz – 1 kHz). - **RF Front-Ends:** Thermal noise limits the *sensitivity* of receivers (NF, Noise Figure). - **Image Sensors (CIS):** Flicker noise (kTC reset noise + 1/f) limits the **Dynamic Range**. - **Precision ADC Drivers:** Push-Pull buffers with low noise extend ENOB (Effective Number of Bits). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean-Square Thermal Noise Voltage of a Resistor ### Step 1: Start with Equipartition Theorem Each degree of freedom of a system at thermal equilibrium holds average energy **½kT**. For a parallel RC network, the energy stored in the capacitor is **½Cv²**. ### Step 2: Apply the Theorem Equating the average energy to ½kT: $$\frac{1}{2}C\,\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{1}{2}kT$$ Solving for the mean-square noise voltage: $$\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{kT}{C}$$ ### Step 3: Relate Capacitance to Bandwidth The bandwidth of an RC low-pass filter (–3 dB) is: $$\Delta f = \frac{1}{4RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{1}{4R\,\Delta f}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute C back into the noise equation $$\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{kT}{1/(4R\,\Delta f)} = 4kTR\,\Delta f$$ > **This is the famous Nyquist formula.** $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MOSFET Channel Thermal Noise (Classical Derivation) ### Step 1: Model the channel as a distributed RC line The MOSFET channel is modelled as a **distributed resistor** of total resistance **R_ch = 1/[μ_n C_ox (W/L)(V_GS − V_TH)]** with distributed oxide capacitance. ### Step 2: Each infinitesimal segment dx contributes noise An elemental segment of length dx has resistance: $$dR = \frac{dx}{\mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x))}$$ ### Step 3: Contribution to drain current noise Using van der Ziel's result, the integrated effect over the channel gives: $$\overline{i_{nd}^2} = 4kT\gamma g_m \,\Delta f$$ where **γ** is the **excess noise factor**: $$\gamma = \frac{2}{3} \quad \text{(long-channel, saturation)}$$ For **short-channel** (velocity-saturated) devices: $$\gamma \approx 1 \text{ to } 2$$ ### Step 4: Substitute numerical example For a MOSFET with $g_m = 10$ mS at T = 300 K, γ = 2/3, Δf = 1 Hz: $$\overline{i_{nd}^2} = 4 \times 1.38\times10^{-23} \times 300 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 10\times10^{-3} \times 1$$ $$\overline{i_{nd}^2} = 1.104 \times 10^{-22} \text{ A}^2$$ $$\sqrt{\overline{i_{nd}^2}} = 1.05 \times 10^{-11}\text{ A}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Integrated Noise at the Output of a Push-Pull Source Follower ### Step 1: Identify the two transistors NMOS M1 (pull-down) has transconductance $g_{m1}$, PMOS M2 (pull-up) has transconductance $g_{m2}$. ### Step 2: Sum the uncorrelated noise currents Since the two noise sources are **statistically independent**, their mean-square values add: $$\overline{i_{n,out}^2} = 4kT\gamma g_{m1}\Delta f + 4kT\gamma g_{m2}\Delta f + 2qI_{leak}\Delta f$$ ### Step 3: Refer to the input For a source follower, $v_{n,in} = v_{n,out}/1 = v_{n,out}$ (unity gain): $$\overline{v_{n,in}^2} = 4kT\gamma\left(\frac{1}{g_{m1}}+\frac{1}{g_{m2}}\right)\Delta f + \frac{K}{C_{ox}WL}\frac{1}{f}\Delta f + \frac{K_P}{C_{ox}WL}\frac{1}{f}\Delta f$$ ### Step 4: Identify dominant flicker noise at low frequency At low frequency, the **1/f term** dominates. To minimize it: - **Increase W·L** (larger area). - **Use PMOS** as the input device (lower K). - Use **Chopper Stabilization** or **Auto-Zeroing** techniques to modulate noise out of the signal band. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Noise Comparison Table | Property | Thermal Noise | Flicker (1/f) Noise | Shot Noise | |----------|---------------|---------------------|------------| | **Origin** | Thermal motion of carriers | Carrier trapping at Si–SiO₂ | Discrete charge carrier arrival | | **Spectral Shape** | White (1/f⁰) | Pink (1/f¹) | White (1/f⁰) | | **Dominant In** | Resistors, MOSFET channels | MOSFETs, BJTs at low f | PN junctions, photodiodes | | **Depends On** | R, T, g_m | Area (W·L), process K | DC current I_DC | | **Frequency Range** | All frequencies | Low frequencies (f < f_c) | All frequencies | | **Key Constants** | k, T, γ | K, C_ox | q, I_DC | | **Reduction Strategy** | Lower R, lower T, higher g_m | Larger W·L, PMOS, chopper | Lower bias current | | **In Push-Pull Stage** | From M1, M2 channels | From M1, M2 oxide traps | From body-junction leakage | | **Gaussian?** | Yes (Central Limit Theorem) | Yes | Yes (Poisson → Gaussian for large N) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Noise Sources in a Push-Pull Amplifier (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram shows a Push-Pull (Class-AB) source follower and the *functional flow* of noise contributions from each transistor.
