For a MOSFET, the *channel thermal noise* is naturally a **current source from drain to source**, while the *flicker noise* is an **input-referred voltage source at the gate**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define thermal noise. Derive the expression for the mean-square thermal noise voltage generated by a resistor R.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise) is the random fluctuation of voltage across a resistor caused by the thermal agitation of charge carriers at temperature T, even in the absence of any applied current. **(1 mark)** Using the Equipartition Theorem, the average energy stored in a capacitor C is ½kT, which equals ½C·v̄²ₙ. Hence, v̄²ₙ = kT/C. Substituting C = 1/(4R·Δf) from the RC bandwidth relation: **(1 mark)** $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTR\,\Delta f$$ where k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, T is in Kelvin, R in ohms, and Δf in Hz. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between thermal noise, shot noise, and flicker (1/f) noise in three points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Thermal Noise | Shot Noise | Flicker Noise | |-----------|---------------|------------|---------------| | **Origin** | Random thermal motion of electrons | Discrete charge carrier emission across a barrier | Carrier trapping at oxide interface | | **Spectral Shape** | White (1/f⁰) | White (1/f⁰) | Pink (1/f¹) | | **Key Dependency** | T, R, g_m | I_DC (DC current) | Device area W·L, frequency f | **(3 marks — 1 per row)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **forget the γ (excess noise factor)** in MOSFET thermal noise — losing 2 marks. > - For flicker noise, students confuse **1/f** with **1/f²** — flicker is **1/f¹**, not 1/f². Lose 1 mark. > - Shot noise formula uses **2qI**, not **4kTR** — using the wrong formula in a junction problem loses 3 marks. > - Always state **units** (V²/Hz, A²/Hz) in spectral density answers. > - For Push-Pull noise, remember **both NMOS and PMOS** contribute. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the **channel thermal noise current** of a MOSFET in saturation. Explain why γ ≈ 2/3 for long-channel devices. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Push-Pull Class-AB source follower uses an NMOS (W/L = 100/0.5) and a PMOS (W/L = 200/0.5) with K_NMOS = 5 × 10⁻²⁴ V²F, K_PMOS = 1 × 10⁻²⁴ V²F, and t_ox = 10 nm. Calculate the **flicker noise voltage spectral density** at f = 100 Hz, and the **corner frequency** at T = 300 K. Assume g_m = 5 mS for both devices. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Channel Thermal Noise Derivation** The MOSFET channel in saturation is modelled as a **distributed RC transmission line**. An infinitesimal channel segment dx at potential V(x) has resistance: $$dR = \frac{dx}{\mu_n C_{ox} W (V_{GS} - V_{TH} - V(x))}$$ Each segment contributes a local thermal noise source. Integrating Nyquist noise contributions over the entire channel (van der Ziel, 1962) yields: $$\overline{i_{nd}^2} = 4kT\gamma g_m\,\Delta f$$ The factor **γ** captures the integration result: - For **long-channel (saturation):** The integration gives γ = 2/3, because V(x) varies from 0 to V_DSAT along the channel and the average effective resistance differs from the DC channel resistance. **(3 marks)** - For **short-channel (velocity saturation):** The electric field is high, the channel behaves more like a uniform resistor, and γ → 1 or higher (up to 2). **(2 marks)** - Physical interpretation: γ < 1 for long-channel reflects the *pinch-off* reducing the effective noise-generating region. **(2 marks)** **Part (b) — Numerical Flicker Noise Computation** Step 1: Compute C_ox. $$C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-11}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-3}\text{ F/m}^2$$ Step 2: Flicker noise PSD (V²/Hz) for NMOS at f = 100 Hz: $$S_{vN} = \frac{K_N}{C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L \cdot f} = \frac{5\times10^{-24}}{3.45\times10^{-3} \times 100\times0.5\times10^{-6}\times 100}$$ $$S_{vN} = \frac{5\times10^{-24}}{3.45\times10^{-3} \times 5\times10^{-6}}$$ $$S_{vN} = \frac{5\times10^{-24}}{1.725\times10^{-8}} = 2.898 \times 10^{-16}\text{ V}^2/\text{Hz}$$ Step 3: For PMOS: $$S_{vP} = \frac{1\times10^{-24}}{1.725\times10^{-8}\times 100} = 5.797 \times 10^{-19}\text{ V}^2/\text{Hz}$$ Step 4: Total flicker PSD (NMOS + PMOS): $$S_{v,tot} = 2.898\times10^{-16} + 5.797\times10^{-19} \approx 2.904\times10^{-16}\text{ V}^2/\text{Hz}$$ Step 5: Thermal noise PSD: $$S_{th} = \frac{4kT\gamma}{g_m} = \frac{4\times1.38\times10^{-23}\times 300\times(2/3)}{5\times10^{-3}} = 1.104\times10^{-18}\text{ V}^2/\text{Hz}$$ Step 6: Corner frequency (where total flicker = thermal): $$f_c = \frac{K_N g_m}{C_{ox} W L \cdot 4kT\gamma} = \frac{5\times10^{-24}\times 5\times10^{-3}}{1.725\times10^{-8}\times 1.104\times10^{-18}}$$ $$f_c = \frac{2.5\times10^{-26}}{1.904\times10^{-26}} \approx 1.31\text{ Hz}$$ Wait — rechecking the corner frequency formula. The corner frequency is where flicker PSD equals thermal PSD: $$f_c = \frac{K}{C_{ox}WL \cdot 4kT\gamma/g_m} = \frac{K\cdot g_m}{C_{ox}WL\cdot 4kT\gamma}$$ Plugging values: $$f_c = \frac{5\times10^{-24}\times 5\times10^{-3}}{3.45\times10^{-3}\times 100\times0.5\times10^{-6}\times 4\times1.38\times10^{-23}\times 300\times(2/3)}$$ $$f_c = \frac{2.5\times10^{-26}}{1.904\times10^{-26}} \approx 1.31\text{ Hz}$$ **Conclusion:** At 100 Hz, the total noise PSD ≈ 2.9 × 10⁻¹⁶ V²/Hz, dominated by NMOS flicker noise. The corner frequency is very low (~1.3 Hz), meaning thermal noise dominates for f > 1.3 Hz in this design. **(2 marks for final answer and interpretation)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Explain the physical origin of **flicker (1/f) noise** in MOSFETs. Derive its input-referred voltage spectral density. Why is PMOS preferred over NMOS for low-noise design? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a diode with DC current I_DC = 1 mA. Compute the **shot noise current** spectral density and the **rms shot noise current** in a 10 kHz bandwidth at T = 300 K. If this diode is in thermal equilibrium (zero bias), what is the noise current? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Flicker Noise Origin and PMOS Preference** **Physical Origin:** Flicker noise arises from charge carriers being **randomly trapped and de-trapped** by interface states (oxide traps, dangling bonds) at the Si–SiO₂ interface of the MOSFET gate. Each trapping event causes a discrete fluctuation in the channel charge, modulating the drain current. **(2 marks)** **Derivation:** The charge density fluctuation in the channel, when referred back to the gate as a voltage, yields an input-referred flicker noise PSD: $$S_{vf}(f) = \frac{K}{C_{ox}WL \cdot f} \quad \text{(V}^2\text{/Hz)}$$ This is derived by noting that the *charge noise per unit area* from the traps has a 1/f spectrum, and dividing by the gate oxide capacitance $C_{ox}WL$ converts it to a voltage. **(3 marks)** **PMOS Preference:** The K coefficient depends on the trap density. For **PMOS**, holes (majority carriers in the channel) have a **larger effective mass and lower tunneling probability** into oxide traps, resulting in a 5–10× **lower K**. Hence, PMOS input pairs in op-amps (e.g., the classic 741) yield **lower 1/f noise** at low frequencies. **(2 marks)** **Part (b) — Shot Noise Calculation** **Step 1:** Shot noise PSD is: $$S_{is} = 2qI_{DC} = 2 \times 1.602\times10^{-19} \times 1\times10^{-3} = 3.204 \times 10^{-22}\text{ A}^2/\text{Hz}$$ **Step 2:** For a 10 kHz bandwidth: $$\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}\Delta f = 3.204\times10^{-22} \times 10^4 = 3.204\times10^{-18}\text{ A}^2$$ **Step 3:** RMS noise current: $$i_{n,rms} = \sqrt{3.204\times10^{-18}} = 1.79 \times 10^{-9}\text{ A} = 1.79\text{ nA}$$ **Step 4:** At zero bias (thermal equilibrium), the diode is no longer a "barrier-crossing" device. Shot noise **vanishes** because the current is zero and no carriers cross the junction. However, **thermal (Johnson) noise** of the diode's dynamic resistance R = V_T/I_S persists. So in thermal equilibrium, only **thermal noise** remains — shot noise is **not** present. **(2 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Noise** - ⭐ **Thermal noise formula:** $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTR\Delta f$ — applies to **every resistor and every MOSFET channel**. - ⭐ **MOSFET channel thermal noise:** $\overline{i_{nd}^2} = 4kT\gamma g_m \Delta f$ with **γ = 2/3 (long-channel)** or **γ ≈ 1–2 (short-channel)**. - ⭐ **Flicker noise formula:** $\overline{v_n^2} = \frac{K}{C_{ox}WLf}\Delta f$ — depends on **device area (W·L)** and **process K**. - ⭐ **Shot noise formula:** $\overline{i_n^2} = 2qI_{DC}\Delta f$ — only for **junctions with net DC current**. - ⭐ **White noise** = frequency-independent PSD; **Pink noise** = 1/f PSD. - ⭐ **Corner frequency** $f_c$ is where flicker = thermal. For **f > f_c**: thermal wins; for **f < f_c**: flicker wins. - ⭐ **Push-Pull Source Follower noise** = noise of NMOS + noise of PMOS (uncorrelated, so variances add). - ⭐ **PMOS has 5–10× lower flicker noise** than NMOS → PMOS is preferred in low-noise op-amp inputs. - ⭐ **At T = 300 K**: 4kT = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁰ W/Hz; V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV. - ⭐ **Units check:** PSD units are V²/Hz (voltage noise) or A²/Hz (current noise). Mean-square units are V² or A². - ⭐ **Equipartition theorem** is the foundation of the thermal noise derivation. - ⭐ **Shot noise is absent in MOSFET channels** (continuous conduction) but **present in leakage currents and BJTs**. - ⭐ **Design trade-off:** Reduce flicker noise by **increasing W·L**, but this increases **gate capacitance and chip area**. - ⭐ **Source follower gain ≈ 1** → output noise = input noise (no attenuation in the output stage).
